operation.description: Envelop artificial structures (walls, monotonous facades, infrastructural elements) in the background with layered vegetation screens to soften visual texture and reduce their dominance in the visual field

pathway.mechanism_description: Vegetation enveloping transforms harsh artificial textures into softer, natural visual patterns, reducing perceptual monotony and visual oppression. The layered green screening diminishes the negative aesthetic impact of artificial structures, thereby improving overall attractiveness by creating visual depth and naturalistic transitions from background to foreground elements

signatures.mechanism: Layered vegetation screens (climbing plants, tall shrubs, canopy trees) are introduced onto and in front of walls, monotonous facades, and infrastructural elements, replacing hard, homogeneous artificial texture with softer, heterogeneous foliar texture in the background visual field.

As the vegetation screen superimposes over built surfaces, the visible area (pixel proportion) of artificial surfaces—walls, facades, infrastructure—is reduced, directly lowering the Artificial Surface View (IND_ASV) ratio in the scene.

scope.description: This is an S2 superposition operation: SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_TEX introduces layered vegetation screens that physically overlay background built surfaces encoded by SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ. Together they target the indicator Artificial Surface View (IND_ASV) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_4. That evidence establishes a negative relationship (β = −0.31) between IND_ASV and the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_ATTR (Attractiveness). By adding vegetation texture over facades (SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_TEX) the intervention reduces the visible proportion of artificial surfaces (SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ), thereby lowering IND_ASV; given the DIR_NEG relationship, a decrease in IND_ASV is expected to improve perceived attractiveness. The superposition pattern captures the spatial logic: new vegetative material is layered directly onto existing built material within the same depth zone, simultaneously adding one object class while diminishing another.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of visible vegetation (trees and shrubs) at the block group scale to enhance streetscape green visibility and neighborhood environmental quality

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher green view index creates enhanced neighborhood amenity value and environmental appeal, attracting higher-income residents and increasing property desirability, thereby correlating with elevated median household income levels in the area

signatures.mechanism: Adding trees and shrubs increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery across the block group, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI reflects greater streetscape green visibility, which the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Amiruzzaman2023_1) associates with higher median household income (r = 0.780), indicating that neighborhoods with more visible vegetation correlate with stronger socio-economic outcomes.

scope.description: A single-signature operation (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) encoding the addition of vegetation quantity and coverage at the streetscape scale. The intervention targets IND_GVI by increasing the amount of visible trees and shrubs within block-group streetscapes. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Amiruzzaman2023_1, higher GVI is positively associated (DIR_POS, effect size 0.780) with Median Household Income under performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_DEMO, suggesting that enriching streetscape green visibility supports favorable neighborhood socio-demographic conditions. The operation is classified as SCP_S1 because it involves a single action (ACT_ADD) on a single object type (OBJ_VEG) modifying one spatial variable (VAR_SIZ) within one depth zone (TER_MG).

source.citation: Amiruzzaman, M., Zhao, Y., Amiruzzaman, S., Karpinski, A. C., & Wu, T. H. (2023). An AI-based framework for studying visual diversity of urban neighborhoods and its relationship with socio-demographic variables. Journal of Computational Social Science, 6(1), 315–337.

operation.description: Reduce visual complexity and texture variance in background building facades and streetscape elements to create more homogeneous, orderly visual environments with lower perceived entropy and chaos

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower visual complexity (measured by Shannon entropy index) in streetscapes is associated with higher median household income. Reducing textural heterogeneity, visual clutter, and disorderly elements creates more uniform and predictable visual environments that correlate with higher socioeconomic status neighborhoods. The negative relationship (DIR_NEG) between visual entropy and income suggests that socioeconomically advantaged areas exhibit more visually ordered streetscapes

signatures.mechanism: Moderating texture variance and visual complexity of background building facades reduces entropy-based diversity indices (Simpson, McIntosh, multi-category entropy), increasing visual similarity and homogeneity of the streetscape. The paper shows that higher building-level visual diversity (HVI building) negatively correlates with median household income, so reducing facade texture variance shifts the scene toward the lower-entropy visual character empirically associated with higher-income neighborhoods.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_TEX is a single-signature intervention that reduces texture variance on background building facades to lower perceived visual entropy. Evidence SVCs_P_Amiruzzaman2023_2 employs the Similarity indicator (IND_SIM) and reports a strong negative relationship (DIR_NEG, r = −0.785) between visual diversity and Median Household Income within the Social Outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_DEMO). The paper confirms that 'families/individuals with more household income tend to reside in less visually diverse areas' and that HVI of building correlates negatively with income (rs = −0.551). By modulating facade texture toward greater uniformity, the intervention increases visual similarity (decreases entropy), aligning the streetscape with the visual profile empirically characteristic of higher-income demographic contexts.

source.citation: Amiruzzaman, M., Zhao, Y., Amiruzzaman, S., Karpinski, A. C., & Wu, T. H. (2023). An AI-based framework for studying visual diversity of urban neighborhoods and its relationship with socio-demographic variables. Journal of Computational Social Science, 6(1), 315–337.

operation.description: Redistribute vegetation resources to fill gaps in underserved areas of the neighborhood, reducing the Gini coefficient of greenery distribution and achieving more equitable green space access across all residential blocks.

pathway.mechanism_description: Equitable distribution of vegetation reduces environmental inequality, which is associated with social inequality. By filling vegetation gaps in deprived areas (infilling strategy), the intervention decreases neighborhood disparities that correlate with violent crime rates. More balanced green space access promotes social cohesion, increases informal surveillance through higher usage of outdoor spaces, and reduces territorial stigmatization that can foster criminal activity. The reduced Gini coefficient indicates fairer resource allocation, addressing underlying social determinants of crime.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation to currently deficient gaps in underserved residential blocks increases the amount of greenery in low-coverage areas, thereby equalizing the spatial distribution of green resources across the neighborhood. This directly reduces the Gini coefficient of greenery, which measures heterogeneity of greenery allocation. More uniform canopy presence also eliminates barren 'no man's lands' (excerpt [8]) that facilitate criminal activity by restoring natural surveillance and resident presence.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ represents the targeted addition of canopy trees to gap areas within underserved residential blocks to equalize greenery distribution. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Amiruzzaman2023_3 demonstrates that the Gini Index of greenery (IND_GIN) has a strong positive correlation (effect size 0.751, DIR_POS) with violent crime rate under the social-outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC, PRS_SOC_CRIM). A higher Gini green value indicates more heterogeneous (unequal) greenery distribution, which is associated with higher violent crime (Table 5, Row 4: S(C1)***, M(C2)*). By filling vegetation gaps, the intervention lowers the Gini coefficient toward greater equity, and given the positive relationship direction, this reduction in inequality is expected to yield a corresponding decrease in violent crime rates.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Accelerate restoration and establishment of street-level vegetation around newly constructed buildings to increase the size and coverage of foreground greenness visible from pedestrian viewpoints within the first years after construction completion.

pathway.mechanism_description: The negative direction (DIR_NEG) indicates that higher green view index correlates with lower apartment transaction prices in this context. Early restoration of street-level greenness may signal incomplete urban development or proximity to construction zones, potentially creating temporary negative perceptions that depress property values. The mechanism operates through visual perception channels where foreground vegetation density directly affects GVI measurements and subsequent buyer valuations during the post-construction transition period.

signatures.mechanism: Replacing absent or immature post-construction street trees with established canopy specimens restores the dominant green mass in pedestrian sightlines, directly increasing the proportion of vegetation pixels captured by the Green View Index at eye level.

Adding shrub and understory planting layers beneath and between tree canopies fills vertical gaps in the foreground visual field, supplementing canopy coverage and further enlarging the total area of visible greenness registered by GVI at pedestrian eye level.

scope.description: This S2 superposition intervention coordinates two foreground vegetation signatures—SIG_REP_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary)—to rapidly rebuild green coverage around newly constructed buildings as seen from pedestrian viewpoints. Together, the replacement of immature or absent street trees with established specimens and the addition of an understory shrub layer increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI) by enlarging the size and density of vegetation within the immediate visual field. Linked evidence SVCs_P_An2023_1 associates GVI with apartment transaction prices (log-transformed per m²) in the Social interaction and lingering performance dimension (PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_ECO_HOUS), reporting a direction of DIR_NEG with an effect size of −0.003. This indicates that, in the studied metropolitan context, each unit increase in GVI corresponds to a marginal ~0.3 % decrease in housing price per square metre, suggesting that higher street-level greenness near new construction may reflect neighbourhood-scale characteristics that slightly discount property values. The intervention nonetheless prioritises accelerated vegetation establishment as a landscape-quality objective whose economic signal is context-dependent and may reverse in longer time horizons or different market settings.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: An, S., Jang, H., Kim, H., Song, Y., & Ahn, K. (2023). Assessment of street-level greenness and its association with housing prices in a metropolitan area. Scientific Reports, 13(1).

operation.description: Strategically position trees within an 80-meter spatial grid to optimize visibility from pedestrian viewpoints. Tree placement should ensure consistent visual exposure throughout the streetscape, creating a rhythmic sequence of green elements visible from ground-level observer positions. The 80m interval corresponds to effective visual detection distance for physiological stress response.

pathway.mechanism_description: Tree visibility within the foreground visual field (≤80m) triggers parasympathetic nervous system activation, reducing electrodermal activity as measured by Skin Conductance Level (SCL). The spatial positioning ensures continuous exposure to tree canopies, which promotes attention restoration and stress recovery through biophilic engagement. The negative correlation between Tree View Factor and SCL indicates that optimized tree positioning within the visible foreground effectively lowers physiological stress markers.

signatures.mechanism: Strategic positioning of canopy trees at 80-metre intervals aligns each placement with the most influential lag distance for physiological stress relief, ensuring every pedestrian viewpoint captures at least one tree within the effective visual detection range, thereby directly increasing Tree View Factor (TVF) across the streetscape.

Each tree added at the 80-metre grid also delivers substantial canopy mass at social-field range (10–50 m) as pedestrians approach and pass it, sustaining a continuous proportion of green in the visual field and maintaining elevated TVF between successive grid positions.

scope.description: SCP_S2 weaving operation coordinating two signatures to produce a rhythmic sequence of tree canopy across the streetscape. SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_POS (primary) establishes the 80-metre spatial grid of canopy trees, matching the most influential lag distance for tree-related stress relief identified in linked evidence SVCs_P_Cai2023_1. SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (secondary) ensures that each added tree contributes meaningful canopy area within the social-field depth plane, so that visual exposure remains consistent as the observer moves along the route. The coordinated result is an increase in Tree View Factor (IND_TVF), which evidence SVCs_P_Cai2023_1 shows is negatively associated (DIR_NEG, β = −0.260) with Standardised Skin Conductance Level on the performance dimension PRF_RST / subdimension PRS_MNT_PHYS. Higher TVF therefore corresponds to reduced physiological stress.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Standardized Skin Conductance Level (SCL)

source.citation: Cai, M., Xiang, L., & Ng, E. (2023). How does the visual environment influence pedestrian physiological stress? Evidence from high-density cities using ambulatory technology and spatial machine learning. Sustainable Cities and Society, 96, 104695.

operation.description: Morphing the skyline configuration by creating concave building facades and manipulating urban morphology to increase sky view factor (SVF) at street level; operate within 300m spatial grid units to optimize visible sky dome proportion and enhance openness perception in urban canyons.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased SVF through building facade morphing enhances perceived spatial openness and sky exposure, reducing psychological stress and physiological arousal. Greater sky visibility improves environmental legibility and sense of prospect, leading to parasympathetic nervous system activation and decreased skin conductance levels (SCL), indicating reduced stress response and improved psychological restoration in urban environments.

signatures.mechanism: Reshaping building facades into concave profiles widens the visible sky dome from street level, directly increasing Sky View Factor (SVF). The modified facade geometry reduces the proportion of built-mass obstruction perceived by pedestrians, enlarging the open-sky aperture within urban canyons without removing buildings.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SHP encodes a single-signature intervention that modifies the shape of building facades (OBJ_BLT → facade) at the background tier (TER_BG, operating across 300 m grid units). By introducing concave facade profiles and terraced podium geometries, the intervention increases the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF), the indicator linked in evidence SVCs_P_Cai2023_2. That evidence reports a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −0.181) between SVF and Standardized Skin Conductance Level (SCL) under performance dimension PRF_RST (Stress relief and psychological restoration) / subdimension PRS_MNT_PHYS. Consequently, the higher SVF achieved through facade shape modification is expected to reduce physiological arousal and psychological stress at street level. The source paper confirms SVF is the strongest single predictor of SCL spatial variance (R² = 0.55 at 200 m lag), validating that background-tier built-form reshaping is the operative lever.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation density by adding trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery along roadways and pedestrian corridors to enhance Green View Index (GVI) in densely built urban areas with high ambient temperatures.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street-level vegetation coverage increases GVI, providing shade canopy that blocks direct solar radiation, reduces surface heat absorption, and promotes evapotranspiration cooling. This multi-layered vegetative barrier intercepts shortwave radiation before it reaches ground surfaces and building facades, thereby lowering ambient air temperatures at pedestrian level through combined shading and evaporative cooling effects.

signatures.mechanism: Adding trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery (climbing plants) along roadways and pedestrian corridors increases the proportion of green vegetation visible in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI is negatively associated with average monthly ambient temperature (coefficient = −0.049, p<0.001), producing a cooling effect through enhanced shading, evapotranspiration, and reduced surface heat absorption in densely built urban areas.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention (SCP_S1) that introduces street-level vegetation—canopy trees, shrubs, and climbing plants—along roadways and pedestrian corridors to increase the Green View Index (GVI). As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Chan2024_1, higher GVI is negatively associated (DIR_NEG) with average monthly ambient temperature (outcome measure: °C; effect size: −0.049 per unit GVI increase). The targeted performance dimension is thermal comfort (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_TEMP). The expected outcome direction is a reduction in ambient temperature as street-level green coverage rises, consistent with the negative coefficient reported in the geographically weighted regression analysis.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Average monthly ambient temperature (°C)

source.citation: Chan, T.-C., Lee, P.-H., Lee, Y.-T., & Tang, J.-H. (2024). Exploring the spatial association between the distribution of temperature and urban morphology with green view index. PLOS ONE, 19(5), e0301921.

operation.description: Modify the sky view configuration in street canyons by reducing canopy obstruction to maintain higher Sky View Factor (SVF) values, thereby preserving greater sky openness and direct atmospheric exposure at the pedestrian level

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher SVF increases radiative heat exchange between ground surfaces and the open sky, particularly enhancing nocturnal longwave radiation cooling. This expanded view of the sky vault facilitates more effective heat dissipation during nighttime hours, reducing minimum temperatures while allowing solar gain during daytime, thereby dampening the diurnal temperature range (reducing the difference between daily maximum and minimum temperatures)

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the extent and density of canopy tree crowns within street canyons decreases tree-view-factor (TVF) obstruction of the sky hemisphere. Because SVF + TVF + BVF = 100% (Excerpt 22), a reduction in canopy coverage directly increases SVF, preserving greater sky openness and atmospheric exposure at the pedestrian level. This shifts the thermal regime captured by the IND_SVF indicator.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that modifies the size and coverage of canopy trees within street canyons to increase Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chan2024_3 reports a DIR_NEG relationship between SVF and average monthly temperature difference (Max–Min), indicating that higher SVF values are associated with reduced diurnal/monthly temperature amplitudes. The performance dimension is PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP: by maintaining greater sky openness through canopy reduction, the intervention dampens extreme temperature oscillations at the pedestrian level, contributing to more stable thermal comfort conditions. Although the IOM describes 'sky view configuration,' the actually manipulated physical element is the canopy tree layer (sky is excluded per encoding rules); it is the vegetation that is pruned or thinned to modulate the proportion of visible sky.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase street-level tree canopy coverage in foreground viewing zones to elevate Green View Index (GVI) above the 30% threshold, with priority implementation in suburban and peri-urban districts where current GVI levels fall below optimal perception standards. Deploy strategic tree planting along roadways, pedestrian corridors, and public spaces to maximize visual green exposure in human eye-level panoramic views.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding foreground tree presence directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby raising GVI metrics. The study demonstrates a negative correlation between current GVI levels and aesthetic performance in Pearl River Delta Urban Agglomeration, indicating that areas below 30% GVI threshold exhibit suboptimal visual quality. Adding trees in foreground spatial tier maximizes their contribution to eye-level green visibility, as proximity amplifies their coverage in human visual fields and street-view assessments. This size-based intervention (increasing vegetative mass in viewer proximity) addresses the identified deficiency in urban greening perception.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees at street level directly increases the percentage of green pixels captured in human eye-level panoramic views, raising the Green View Index from the current 11.3% baseline toward the 30% favourable-impression threshold identified in the literature.

Strategic spatial distribution of new canopy trees along roadways, pedestrian corridors, and public spaces ensures that green coverage is registered across the maximum number of 300 m × 300 m street-view sampling points, broadening the district-level GVI uplift rather than concentrating it at isolated sites.

scope.description: SCP_S2 coordinates two superimposed signatures on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) encodes the volumetric addition of canopy trees that enlarges visible green area, while SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS (secondary) encodes the deliberate spatial arrangement of those trees to maximise coverage across street-view panoramas. Together they target the indicator Green View Index (IND_GVI) reported in evidence SVCs_P_Chen2020_3, which currently registers a mean of 11.3 ± 7.5% across the Pearl River Delta Urban Agglomeration—well below the 30% aesthetic-preference threshold. By jointly increasing canopy amount and optimising its placement, the intervention is expected to shift the relationship direction (DIR_NEG) toward positive performance on the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), elevating perceived urban greening quality in suburban and peri-urban districts where the deficit is greatest.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: For areas with low Green View Index (GVI), implement multi-layer planting strategies combining trees, shrubs, and herbaceous plants to create vertical green volume in the foreground space. Prioritize eye-level vegetation coverage (1.5-3m height) to maximize immediate visual impact, while ensuring diverse canopy layers that enhance the perception of green abundance within pedestrian view corridors.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing foreground vegetation size through multi-layer planting directly enhances GVI by expanding green pixels in human eye-level photography and street view images. The vertical stratification creates denser green mass within the critical viewing angle (±30° from horizontal), thereby elevating perceived greenness and improving aesthetic environmental satisfaction. Multi-layer configurations also increase visual complexity and color variation, which positively correlates with green perception quality metrics.

signatures.mechanism: Adding shrub-layer vegetation at eye-level height (1.5–3 m) directly fills the visual cone most registered by GVI pixel counts, producing the largest per-unit gain in green pixel ratio within the pedestrian view corridor.

Adding canopy trees establishes the upper vertical green layer, increasing overall green volume and sky-blocking leaf area that registers as green pixels in the upper portions of the street-view image sphere.

Adding herbaceous ground-cover completes the lowest stratum of the multi-layer composition, contributing green pixels in the lower visual field and eliminating bare-ground gaps between tree trunks and shrub bases.

scope.description: Three superimposed SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ signatures—shrub (primary), canopy_tree, and ground_cover—act simultaneously in the foreground tier to construct a vertically continuous green volume. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chen2022_1 reports that the current mean Green View Index (IND_GVI) in Changsha's Second Ring is only 12.56%, rated 'very poor green perception'. By stacking vegetation layers that collectively occupy low-, mid-, and high-elevation bands of the street-view image sphere, the coordinated addition is designed to raise IND_GVI substantially above this baseline. The expected outcome aligns with the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU: a higher proportion of green pixels perceived at pedestrian eye-level increases the aesthetic naturalness rating and overall green perception level. The superposition pattern is critical because no single vegetation layer alone can fill the full vertical extent of the foreground view; the three layers together maximise green pixel coverage across the 360° spherical image used in GVI calculation.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Chen, Y., Zhang, Q., Deng, Z., Fan, X., Xu, Z., Kang, X., Pan, K., & Guo, Z. (2022). Research on Green View Index of Urban Roads Based on Street View Image Recognition: A Case Study of Changsha Downtown Areas. Sustainability, 14(23), 16063.

operation.description: In old urban streets with narrow sidewalks and limited planting space, implement vertical greening systems using climbing plants on building facades, walls, and vertical structures to increase vegetation coverage without occupying ground-level pedestrian space.

pathway.mechanism_description: Vertical greening increases the Green View Index (GVI) by adding visible vegetation layers in the foreground viewing zone. Climbing plants create green facades that occupy the primary visual field of street-level observers, effectively expanding the proportion of greenery in the streetscape. This approach is particularly effective in space-constrained environments where horizontal expansion is impossible, as it transforms underutilized vertical surfaces into green infrastructure that directly contributes to visual greenery perception and regional greenery assessment.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing climbing plants on vertical surfaces directly increases the amount of visible green coverage captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising GVI without consuming limited ground-level planting space on narrow sidewalks.

Building facades and walls serve as the substrate for the climbing plants; their surface texture transforms from hard, impervious material to a vegetated layer, shifting the visual character of the vertical plane from grey infrastructure to green and supporting the GVI increase registered by the primary signature.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two simultaneous signatures in a superposition pattern—layering living vegetation onto existing built surfaces. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ introduces climbing plants onto facades and walls, directly adding green biomass to the street-level visual field. SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_TEX captures the consequent modification of the facade surface from bare masonry/concrete to a vegetated texture. Together they target the indicator Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Chen2022_2, which shows that old urban areas exhibit significantly lower GVI than new urban areas due to higher building density and constrained planting space. By superimposing vegetation onto vertical built elements, the intervention is expected to increase GVI in these deficit areas, improving performance on PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU (perceived naturalness). Although the relationship direction in the evidence is DIR_NA (descriptive comparison between old and new districts), the design logic follows the paper's explicit recommendation that vertical greening—climbing plants, plant landscape walls—compensates for the absence of ground-level planting opportunities, narrowing the GVI gap and enhancing the regional greenery level perceived by pedestrians.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase tree canopy coverage (size) in urban streetscapes through strategic planting of additional trees to expand the vertical and horizontal extent of green canopy in the middleground visual layer.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced tree canopy coverage increases the visual prominence and attractiveness of urban nature, creating photogenic scenes that draw visitors and social media engagement. Higher tree density generates clustered green spaces that serve as destination points, elevating implied urban nature vitality (IUNV) as measured through geotagged photo density on platforms like Flickr. The expanded canopy creates layered visual depth and shade amenities that encourage prolonged human interaction with streetscape environments.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees increases the total tree canopy coverage area (size), directly raising Tree View Factor (TVF) in street-level imagery by occupying a larger proportion of the visual field with green canopy mass in both vertical and horizontal extent.

Strategic spatial arrangement (position) of newly planted trees distributes canopy across the streetscape to maximise continuous middleground coverage, ensuring the TVF gain is not clustered in isolated patches but forms a coherent visual canopy layer.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_POS (secondary)—to increase tree canopy coverage in the middleground visual layer of urban streetscapes. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Chen2024_1, the intervention targets the Tree View Factor (IND_TVF) indicator. The primary signature captures the addition of canopy-tree volume and extent (size), directly expanding the proportion of green canopy pixels registered in street-view imagery and thus raising TVF. The secondary signature captures the deliberate spatial arrangement (position) of these additional trees—described in the IOM as 'strategic planting'—to distribute canopy coverage uniformly across the streetscape rather than in isolated clusters, thereby amplifying the TVF increase. Together, these coordinated variable manipulations on the same object and tier are expected to elevate TVF, which evidence SVCs_P_Chen2024_1 shows is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 0.042) with Implied Urban Nature Vitality (IUNV), derived from Flickr photo density and clustering, within the social-outcomes performance dimension (PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_SOC_VITL). The superposition pattern reflects that size increase and positional distribution are applied simultaneously to the same element type, reinforcing each other's contribution to the perceptual outcome.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add trees strategically in foreground spaces to increase canopy coverage and reduce sky view factor (SVF) in pedestrian zones during hot seasons.

pathway.mechanism_description: Tree canopy addition increases the size and extent of overhead shade coverage, which directly reduces SVF by blocking visible sky hemisphere. Lower SVF diminishes shortwave and longwave radiation exposure to pedestrians, thereby reducing Physiological Equivalent Temperature (PET) and improving thermal comfort in urban environments.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing canopy trees increases the total amount of vegetative canopy cover in the pedestrian zone. The added leaf-area and branch mass physically obstruct incoming solar radiation and occlude the visible sky hemisphere, directly lowering Sky View Factor (SVF). During the hot season, full foliage maximises this obstruction (Excerpt 2), reducing Physiological Equivalent Temperature (PET) beneath the canopy.

Strategic spatial arrangement of the new trees within pedestrian zones ensures canopy placement where sky exposure is highest and foot traffic is greatest. Optimising the positional distribution of trees maximises the proportion of the sky hemisphere that is blocked at pedestrian level, amplifying the SVF reduction per tree added and ensuring shade falls on occupied areas rather than empty spaces.

scope.description: This intervention is encoded as SCP_S2 (coordinated, superposition pattern) because it layers two spatial variables—amount (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) and arrangement (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS)—onto the same object class (canopy trees) within the same depth zone (foreground pedestrian space). The primary signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ captures the volumetric increase in canopy that directly occludes the sky hemisphere, while the secondary signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS captures the deliberate spatial targeting described by 'strategically.' Together they modify the indicator Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) referenced in linked evidence SVCs_P_Chiang2023_1. The evidence reports a significant positive relationship (DIR_POS, r = 0.785) between SVF and Physiological Equivalent Temperature (PET), meaning higher SVF yields higher PET and poorer thermal comfort. By adding canopy trees (reducing SVF), the intervention lowers PET, producing a positive outcome within the performance dimension PRF_THR (Thermal Comfort) and its subdimension PRS_THR_PET. The large effect size (0.785) underscores that even moderate SVF reductions via canopy addition can meaningfully improve pedestrian thermal comfort during hot seasons.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Replace evergreen trees with deciduous trees along sidewalks to optimize seasonal thermal performance through dynamic canopy characteristics that provide summer shading while allowing winter solar penetration

pathway.mechanism_description: Deciduous trees reduce Physiological Equivalent Temperature (PET) through seasonal leaf cycle: dense summer foliage increases Green View Index (GVI) and provides evaporative cooling and solar shading during hot periods, while winter leaf loss permits solar radiation to reach ground surfaces for passive warming, thereby balancing year-round thermal comfort needs in urban pedestrian environments

signatures.mechanism: Substituting evergreen canopy trees with deciduous canopy trees along sidewalks transforms the foliage texture from a static, year-round dense pattern to a seasonally dynamic one. In summer, full deciduous foliage produces high GVI and dense canopy shadow (Excerpt 25), lowering Physiological Equivalent Temperature through shading and evapotranspiration (Excerpt 8). In winter, leaf-drop reduces GVI and increases SVF, permitting solar radiation to reach pedestrians and ground surfaces, improving thermal comfort during cool seasons (Excerpts 1, 4). The replacement thus optimises the GVI-PET relationship across both seasons rather than maximising it statically.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single signature SIG_REP_VEG_FG_TEX representing the replacement of evergreen with deciduous canopy trees in the immediate sidewalk zone. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chiang2023_2 demonstrates that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) has a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −0.311) with Physiological Equivalent Temperature (PRS_THR_PET) within the thermal comfort dimension (PRF_THR). By introducing seasonally variable foliage texture, the intervention dynamically modulates GVI: high summer GVI drives PET downward through canopy shading, while reduced winter GVI allows beneficial solar penetration, collectively improving year-round pedestrian thermal comfort along street canyons.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation coverage to achieve a minimum Green View Index (GVI) of 25% along pedestrian corridors, prioritizing tree canopy and vertical greenery within the immediate viewing zone (0-50m from observer).

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street greenery at GVI ≥25% improves pedestrian psychological comfort through multiple pathways: (1) increased visual connection with nature triggers restorative psychological responses, (2) green canopy provides thermal comfort through shading and evapotranspiration, (3) vegetation softens harsh urban geometry and reduces visual stress, and (4) creates perceived safety and place attachment. The 25% threshold represents a critical perceptual tipping point where street users report significant improvements in comfort and positive sentiment.

signatures.mechanism: Adding tree canopy along pedestrian corridors in the 10–50 m social field maximises the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI) toward the ≥25% target. Canopy trees contribute the largest single share of visual greenery owing to their broad crown projection area visible from the pedestrian eye-height.

Introducing vertical greenery (climbing plants on walls and structures) within the 0–10 m immediate sensory zone fills near-field portions of the pedestrian's visual cone that canopy trees alone cannot occupy, supplementing the GVI by increasing green coverage on otherwise hard surfaces at close range.

scope.description: This operation is scoped as SCP_S2 (coordinated, superposition) because the IOM explicitly prioritises two complementary vegetation layers—tree canopy and vertical greenery—across two depth zones to jointly elevate the same performance indicator. SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (primary, canopy_tree) targets the mid-ground street corridor where the majority of green visual mass is perceived, while SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (secondary, climbing_plant) fills the near-field view with vertical greenery. Together, the two signatures superimpose green surface area at different depths in the pedestrian's field of view to raise the indicator IND_GVI above the 25% threshold. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Dong2018_4 reports a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 0.25) between GVI and the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_CMFT—pedestrian comfort/sentiment), confirming that the combined increase in street-visible vegetation coverage is expected to improve perceived streetscape comfort.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: For wide motorways (Type T1, >40m width), establish multi-layered central green belts featuring tall canopy trees (8-15m height) combined with dense understory shrubbery (2-4m height) to maximize vertical green volume and compensate for the negative correlation between road width and Green View Index observed in Beijing's urban streets.

pathway.mechanism_description: Wide roads reduce pedestrian-level GVI due to increased viewing distance from roadside vegetation. Central median planting with tall trees and dense shrubs creates additional green mass in the visual field, increasing the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic images. The multi-layered structure ensures vegetation visibility from multiple viewing angles, directly counteracting the dilution effect of road width on perceived greenness and improving aesthetic performance through enhanced visual green exposure.

signatures.mechanism: Tall canopy trees (8–15 m) inserted into the central median belt introduce a dominant upper vegetation layer that substantially increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, directly raising the Green View Index by filling the mid-to-upper visual field from the pedestrian vantage point across the carriageway.

Dense understory shrubbery (2–4 m) layered beneath the canopy trees closes the vertical gap between ground level and the tree crowns, ensuring continuous green coverage across the lower-to-mid visual field and maximising the cumulative vertical green volume that contributes to GVI.

scope.description: A superposition of SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (shrub) that vertically stacks two vegetation layers within the central green belt of wide motorways (>40 m). Evidence SVCs_P_Dong2018_3 documents a significant negative correlation (r = −0.332, p < 0.001) between road width and Green View Index (IND_GVI) for Type T1 motorways, meaning wider roads systematically deliver lower visual greenery. The two-signature superposition directly counteracts this deficit: the primary canopy-tree signature fills the upper visual field, while the secondary shrub signature fills the lower-to-mid visual field, together maximising vertical green volume in the pedestrian's field of view. On the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU, the combined intervention is expected to shift the observed negative relationship toward a neutral or positive direction by compensating for the large unvegetated carriageway area with concentrated, multi-layered green mass in the median.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of eye-level vegetation along secondary roads and bike-sharing corridors to enhance visual greenness perception from cyclists' viewpoint.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced eye-level greenness (measured by Green View Index) creates more pleasant and aesthetically appealing cycling environments, which increases users' willingness to choose dockless bike sharing as a transportation mode. The visual exposure to vegetation during trips provides psychological benefits and reduces perceived environmental stress, thereby promoting higher frequency of bike sharing usage.

signatures.mechanism: Adding eye-level vegetation (shrubs and allied planting) along secondary roads and bike-sharing corridors increases the proportion of green pixels captured at the cyclist's viewpoint, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Because GVI is computed from street-level imagery at human eye height, foreground vegetation that fills the visual field within a few metres of the road edge is the most efficient driver of the index.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ targeting the quantity and spatial coverage of eye-level vegetation along secondary roads. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Gao2021_1 demonstrates that the Green View Index (IND_GVI)—measuring the share of visible greenery in street-level panoramas—is positively associated (DIR_POS) with dockless bike-sharing usage frequency across weekdays, weekends, and holidays. The intervention therefore acts on the performance dimension PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_CYCL: by adding foreground shrub-layer vegetation that maximises the cyclist's eye-level green exposure, GVI rises, which the evidence links to increased cycling uptake and trip frequency. No coordinated second element or phasing is required; the operation is a direct volumetric addition of a single object type in a single depth zone.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage area of street-level vegetation (trees, shrubs, and ground cover plants) to elevate Green View Index (GVI) to optimal levels through systematic planting along sidewalks, building setbacks, and roadside buffers

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher vegetation coverage in the pedestrian viewshed directly increases GVI values captured through street view image segmentation. Enhanced visual greenness improves perceived spatial quality by creating naturalistic streetscapes, moderating microclimate, providing visual relief from hard surfaces, and fostering psychological restoration effects that collectively elevate street spatial quality scores

signatures.mechanism: Street trees planted along sidewalks and roadside buffers introduce the largest single-element green pixel area in street-view images, directly elevating GVI by occupying substantial portions of the visual field at eye level and above.

Mid-height shrubs fill the visual gap between canopy crowns and the ground plane, adding continuous green pixels in the lower-middle band of street-view images and increasing GVI through vertical layering with the tree canopy.

Ground-cover planting along buffers and setbacks converts hard-surface pixels (sidewalk, soil) to green pixels in the lowest image band, further supplementing GVI and completing the multi-strata vegetation profile.

Trees added within building setbacks (10–50 m from the pedestrian viewpoint) contribute background green mass that fills gaps between foreground canopy crowns, increasing aggregate green pixel proportion captured in panoramic street-view imagery used for GVI computation.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes four vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (ground_cover), and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree, supporting)—to systematically raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as documented in evidence SVCs_P_Gao2024_2. GVI is computed as the combined pixel proportion of trees, grass, and plants in street-view images (Equation 2 in source). By vertically stacking canopy trees, shrubs, and ground cover in the foreground tier and adding setback trees in the midground tier, the intervention maximises the green pixel ratio across the full image frame. Evidence SVCs_P_Gao2024_2 demonstrates a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.1836) between GVI and Street Spatial Quality Score under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL, meaning that the cumulative increase in green coverage from all four signatures is expected to improve perceived aesthetic quality of the streetscape.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add street furniture elements (benches, lighting fixtures, waste receptacles, bicycle racks) in the foreground zone along pedestrian pathways to enhance pedestrian convenience and usability

pathway.mechanism_description: Street furniture additions directly increase the Pedestrian Convenience Index (PCI) by providing essential amenities for rest, wayfinding, and basic needs. The strategic positioning of these elements in accessible foreground locations improves functional quality and perceived serviceability of street spaces, which are key components evaluated in street spatial quality assessments through street view image segmentation analysis

signatures.mechanism: Introducing multiple street furniture elements (benches, lighting fixtures, waste receptacles, bicycle racks) where they were previously absent increases the aggregate pixel proportion of public-facility objects in street view images, directly raising the Public-Facility Convenience Index (PCI) by increasing the amount of detectable facility coverage

Distributing functionally distinct furniture types (seating, lighting, waste, bicycle storage) at regular intervals along pedestrian pathways introduces a deliberate spatial arrangement that ensures consistent facility presence across successive street-view frames, broadening PCI coverage across the pathway corridor

scope.description: This S2 operation coordinates two superimposed signatures acting on the same object class and tier. The primary signature SIG_ADD_BLT_FG_SIZ captures the quantitative introduction of furniture items that were previously absent, directly increasing the pixel-proportion metric underlying the Public-Facility Convenience Index (IND_PCI) reported in evidence SVCs_P_Gao2024_3. The secondary signature SIG_ADD_BLT_FG_POS captures the intentional spatial arrangement of diverse furniture types along pedestrian pathways, ensuring that the added elements register across multiple street-view sampling points rather than clustering in one location. Together, both signatures act on the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL (Street Spatial Quality Score), with a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.0488): as the combined amount and distributed positioning of foreground furniture increase, the perceived convenience component of overall street spatial quality improves.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Optimize spatial composition to increase Sky View Factor (SVF) by maximizing visible sky area in the background layer through strategic arrangement of foreground and middleground elements, reducing visual obstruction of the sky dome.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher SVF creates visual openness and simplifies the visual field by reducing the number of competing visual elements in the upper field of view. Increased sky visibility provides a uniform, less complex background that reduces visual information density and cognitive processing demands. The negative correlation (DIR_NEG) between SVF and perceived scene complexity indicates that greater sky exposure directly decreases the visual complexity perception, thereby reducing mental stress and cognitive load associated with visually cluttered environments.

signatures.mechanism: Repositioning foreground canopy trees to open sight-lines toward the sky dome, directly reducing visual obstruction and increasing the percentage of visible sky area (SVF). Foreground elements exert the greatest angular obstruction on the sky hemisphere, so their rearrangement has the largest per-unit effect on SVF.

Rearranging middleground canopy trees to complement foreground repositioning, ensuring that cumulative crown overlap across depth layers does not re-occlude the sky corridors opened at the foreground tier. This coordinated adjustment further widens the solid angle of unobstructed sky visible from the observation point.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two signatures—SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS and SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS—in a weaving pattern across depth tiers. Both manipulate the spatial arrangement (VAR_POS) of canopy trees: the primary signature re-positions foreground (0–10 m) canopy trees, while the secondary signature adjusts middleground (10–50 m) canopy trees. Together they maximise the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) by reducing cumulative visual obstruction of the sky dome across successive depth layers. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Guan2022_1, 'percentage of visible sky area' is a top-4 predictor of perceived scene complexity. The relationship direction is DIR_NEG: increasing SVF through this coordinated rearrangement is expected to decrease perceived complexity scores on the aesthetic-richness subdimension (PRS_AES_RICH) of the performance dimension PRF_AES, moving ratings toward the 'Very simple' end of the 1–5 scale. Sky itself is not an actionable object; the physical manipulation targets the vegetation elements whose canopies define SVF.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Eradicate walls and fences separating residential blocks to transform gated communities into open-access neighborhoods, enabling visual and physical connectivity to green spaces across property boundaries

pathway.mechanism_description: Removing physical barriers alters the spatial position relationship between residents and green spaces, expanding the viewshed area and increasing green visibility exposure for previously blocked populations. This redistributes visual access to vegetation from privatized enclaves to the broader community, directly addressing spatial inequity in green visibility distribution by eliminating exclusionary infrastructure that creates visual dead zones

signatures.mechanism: Eradicating perimeter walls and fences around gated residential blocks eliminates the built barriers that privatize interior green spaces, thereby extending lines of sight and physical access to greenery across formerly sealed property boundaries. By removing these opaque obstructions at the block-boundary scale, green visibility is redistributed from exclusively high-visibility enclaves to a more even distribution across neighbouring communities, directly influencing the Gini coefficient of green visibility.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_REM_BLT_MG_SIZ) encoding the complete removal of boundary walls and fences that enclose residential blocks. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Guo2025_2 reports a Gini coefficient (IND_GIN) range of 0.4–0.5 for green visibility distribution, indicating moderate-to-high spatial inequity. By removing the built barriers, formerly privatised green views become shared public views, which is expected to compress the upper tail of that distribution and lower the Gini index. This targets the performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_EQTY (spatial equity of green visibility). The relationship direction is recorded as DIR_NA (descriptive), so an exact effect magnitude is not quantified, but the logic of opening sealed block perimeters to public sightlines is consistent with a reduction in distributional inequity as captured by the Gini metric.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Introduce evergreen tree species (Cinnamomum camphora, Osmanthus fragrans, Pinus spp.) to maintain consistent green coloration and visual continuity throughout winter months in street foreground planting zones.

pathway.mechanism_description: Evergreen foliage sustains year-round green visual index (GVI) values by preventing seasonal canopy loss, thereby maintaining consistent biophilic visual exposure that correlates with elevated self-reported relaxation scores through sustained nature contact and perceived environmental stability.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing evergreen species (Cinnamomum camphora, Osmanthus fragrans, Pinus spp.) guarantees persistent green coloration in the street-level visual field during winter, preventing the seasonal loss of green hue that deciduous species exhibit. This stabilises the chromatic green component captured by pixel-level semantic segmentation, thereby sustaining Green View Index values year-round.

The physical addition of new tree canopies increases the total vegetation pixel area within the street-view frame, directly raising the Green View Index by enlarging the proportion of classified vegetation in the scene. Combined with the evergreen trait, this added canopy mass remains visually present across all seasons.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two signatures in superposition on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_COL (primary) encodes the introduction of year-round green coloration through evergreen species selection, while SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (secondary) encodes the increase in vegetation coverage volume. Together they target the Green View Index (IND_GVI) reported in evidence SVCs_P_Hao2024_1: GVI is computed as the proportion of vegetation pixels in street-view images, so both adding canopy area (size) and ensuring that area remains chromatically green in winter (colour) jointly elevate and stabilise GVI. Per SVCs_P_Hao2024_1, higher GVI is associated with improved self-reported relaxation scores (mean 2.8 → 3.9) within the performance dimension PRF_EMO / subdimension PRS_MNT_REST. The expected outcome direction is positive—the combined colour-persistence and size-increase signatures should raise winter GVI values and thereby support restorativeness and mental well-being for street users.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Selectively prune tree canopies in foreground areas where Tree-Sky View Factor (TSV) exceeds optimal thresholds or where dense foliage creates perception of enclosure and reduced safety, focusing on thinning techniques that maintain tree health while opening vertical sight lines to sky.

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing excessive canopy density through pruning increases TSV ratio, enhancing visual connection to sky and improving perceived openness. Moderate TSV levels (avoiding both excessive closure and over-exposure) correlate with higher relaxation scores by balancing sense of enclosure with adequate openness, reducing claustrophobic sensations while maintaining shade benefits. Improved visibility and reduced shadowing also enhance perceived safety in street environments.

signatures.mechanism: Thinning canopy foliage density reduces the proportion of tree pixels relative to sky in the vertical visual field, directly lowering Tree-Sky View Factor (TSV) from above-optimal levels toward the threshold range associated with peak restorative perception.

Selective branch removal reshapes crown outlines and opens vertical sight lines through the canopy, reducing perceived visual enclosure and improving the sense of openness and safety, which complements the density reduction by enhancing sky access through the crown silhouette.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 coordinated intervention applies two simultaneous variable modifications to foreground canopy trees via superposition. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) thins canopy foliage density, directly modulating the Tree-Sky View Factor indicator (IND_TSV) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Hao2024_2. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP (secondary) reshapes crown outlines to open vertical sight lines, reinforcing the perceptual shift from enclosure toward openness. Together these signatures target performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST. The linked evidence reports a mean self-reported relaxation score of 3.92 (SD = 0.79) with a non-linear relationship direction (DIR_NA), indicating that TSV must be brought within an optimal band rather than simply maximised or minimised. By selectively pruning only where TSV exceeds optimal thresholds, the intervention is expected to shift relaxation outcomes toward the restorative optimum. The evidence further aligns with excerpt findings that moderate enclosure supports positive perception [excerpt 17], while excessive foliage density degrades perceived safety [excerpt 18], justifying the paired thinning-and-reshaping strategy.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Improve the quality of existing green spaces with high potential GVI (like parks and temples) through regular maintenance and facility enhancement.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhancing existing structures to maintain high visual quality.

signatures.mechanism: Regular maintenance of existing vegetation in high-potential GVI spaces (parks, temples) improves canopy health, density, and fullness. Healthier, better-maintained canopies occupy a greater proportion of the visual field, directly increasing the Green View Index—a pixel-ratio measure of visible greenery in street-level imagery.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a quality-driven modification of existing park and temple vegetation through systematic maintenance. As evidenced by SVCs_P_Hu2023_2, the Green View Index (IND_GVI) captures the three-dimensional greenery volume perceived by pedestrians, and the paper demonstrates that current 3D greenery volumes often fall below the minimum limits set by 2D planning indicators (excerpts [21],[22]). By maintaining canopy trees so they achieve their full visual potential, the intervention closes this gap. The performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL is served because denser, healthier canopies yield a richer visual greenery volume (DIR_POS, effect size 39.62), enhancing perceived landscape aesthetic quality. Facility enhancement mentioned in the IOM description supports visitor access and experience but does not directly alter green pixel coverage; the operative landscape-architectural move is the vegetation quality adjustment captured in this single signature.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Directional change in Green visibility by landscape object type is context-dependent because the indicator adjustment is non-directional.

source.citation: Hu, A., Yabuki, N., Fukuda, T., Kaga, H., Takeda, S., & Matsuo, K. (2023). Harnessing multiple data sources and emerging technologies for comprehensive urban green space evaluation. Cities, 143, 104562.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation density (trees and shrubs) in central urban neighborhoods by adding multi-layered greenery to create denser foreground texture along pedestrian pathways and building facades, enhancing the Green View Index (GVI) at eye-level viewing angles.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher vegetation density at eye-level increases perceived safety by improving natural surveillance opportunities (prospect-refuge theory), creating defined territorial boundaries, increasing legitimate street activity through attractive green environments, and enhancing perceived maintenance and social order signals. Dense foreground vegetation serves as symbolic barriers that deter criminal behavior while maintaining visual permeability for legitimate users, thereby reducing fear of robbery, harassment, and break-ins.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees along pedestrian pathways directly increases the quantity of green pixels captured at eye-level viewing angles, raising the Green View Index (GVI) ratio. Canopy trees provide the dominant upper-layer green mass that fills the largest portion of the street-view image frame.

Adding shrubs beneath and between canopy trees creates a multi-layered textural density that fills mid-height and lower portions of the eye-level view. This layering closes visual gaps between tree canopies and the ground plane, contributing additional green pixels to the GVI and signalling well-maintained, cared-for greenery that reinforces perceptions of territorial control.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 superposition operation coordinates two foreground vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (shrub, secondary)—that vertically stack within the same TER_FG depth zone along pedestrian pathways and building facades. Together they increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI) by maximising green-pixel coverage across the full height of the street-view image frame. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Jing2021_1 demonstrates that higher neighbourhood-level GVI is associated with lower self-reported fear of crime (five-item Likert scale covering robbery, harassment, break-in, theft, and assault) with a significant multilevel regression coefficient of −1.01 (p = 0.02), confirming a DIR_NEG relationship within the performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_SAFE. The mechanism operates through two complementary pathways identified in the source paper: (1) well-maintained, visibly dense street greenery reduces perceived physical incivilities (a confirmed mediator), and (2) greener streetscapes foster neighbourhood social integration and territorial signalling, both of which suppress fear of crime. The superposition pattern ensures that canopy trees supply broad upper-frame green mass while shrubs seal the lower-frame visual gaps, jointly pushing GVI above the study-area mean of 0.27 and toward the upper observed range (0.44), thereby maximising the fear-reduction benefit.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Jing, F., Liu, L., Zhou, S., Song, J., Wang, L., Zhou, H., Wang, Y., & Ma, R. (2021). Assessing the Impact of Street-View Greenery on Fear of Neighborhood Crime in Guangzhou, China. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 18(1), 311.

operation.description: Optimize the size and density of street-side vegetation to achieve a minimum Green View Index (GVI) of 25% along urban roads, ensuring vegetation occupies at least one-quarter of the visual field from pedestrian eye-level perspective. This involves calibrating canopy width, planting density, and vertical layering of trees and shrubs to meet the quantitative greening threshold while maintaining visual comfort.

pathway.mechanism_description: Achieving a GVI of ≥25% enhances aesthetic experience by creating a visually balanced streetscape where greenery provides sufficient natural visual relief without overwhelming the urban context. This threshold ensures adequate green coverage in the pedestrian's field of view, which has been empirically linked to improved perceived environmental quality, psychological comfort, and overall satisfaction with urban green space. The size optimization of vegetation elements directly increases the proportion of natural elements visible to street users, thereby elevating the aesthetic performance of the street environment.

signatures.mechanism: Calibrating canopy tree width and planting density to increase the proportion of tree canopy occupying the upper and middle portions of the pedestrian visual field, directly raising the pixel-based GVI toward and above the 25% threshold
Adjusting shrub size and density to fill the lower and mid-height bands of the visual field, complementing canopy tree coverage and contributing additional green pixels to the GVI calculation

Coordinating the vertical layering arrangement of trees and shrubs so that vegetation occupies continuous height bands in the visual field, minimizing gaps between canopy and understory layers and maximising cumulative green coverage for GVI

scope.description: This SCP_S2 coordinated intervention uses a superposition pattern, layering three simultaneous vegetation modifications within the same foreground zone to meet a quantitative greening target. The primary signature SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree) adjusts canopy width and planting density—the dominant driver of green visual coverage. The secondary signature SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub) modifies shrub size and density to supplement green coverage in the lower visual field. A third signature SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS coordinates the vertical layering arrangement of both tree and shrub layers to close visual gaps and maximise aggregate green pixel coverage. Together the three signatures target the indicator IND_GVI documented in evidence SVCs_P_Jing2024_2, which establishes a positive relationship (DIR_POS) between Green View Index and Urban Green Space Quality within the aesthetic-preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL). By superimposing size optimisation of two vegetation subtypes with positional layering, the intervention is designed to push GVI above the 25% threshold, ensuring at least one-quarter of the pedestrian-level visual field registers as green vegetation, thereby improving perceived green-space quality.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street-level vegetation in the foreground visual field, including roadside trees, planting strips, and green infrastructure elements along pedestrian pathways to enhance the Green View Index (GVI).

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher street-level vegetation visibility creates more aesthetically pleasing and thermally comfortable walking environments, which positively influences pedestrian route choice, walking frequency, and duration. The increased greenery provides visual interest, shade, and perceived naturalness that encourages active mobility behaviors.

signatures.mechanism: Adding roadside trees in the immediate pedestrian view field directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level, raising the Green View Index (GVI). Canopy trees command the largest share of the vertical visual field and are the dominant driver of GVI scores.

Planting strips composed of shrubs fill the mid-height band of the foreground visual field (roughly 0.5–2 m), contributing additional green coverage beneath the canopy layer and closing gaps in the vertical green profile perceived at eye-level.

Low-level green infrastructure elements (ground cover, bioswales, verge plantings) along pedestrian pathways add green pixels in the lower portion of the street-view frame, complementing the upper-canopy and mid-shrub layers to maximise cumulative GVI.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates three superimposed vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (ground_cover)—within the same foreground tier to create a vertically layered green profile. Evidence SVCs_P_Kang2020_1 links the resulting increase in the Green View Index (IND_GVI) to the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK, with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS): higher eye-level green coverage is associated with improved walking behaviours. The superposition pattern ensures that canopy, shrub, and ground-cover layers jointly maximise the proportion of green pixels in street-view imagery, which is the operational definition of GVI. Although the effect size is not numerically reported (not numerically reported), the review converges on a consistent positive association between street-level greenness and pedestrian activity across multiple cited studies.

operation.description: Add or increase street trees at eye-level (foreground) along cycling routes to maximize visual contact with green vegetation, prioritizing canopy trees that create visible greenness from cyclist perspective rather than overhead satellite-measurable coverage.

pathway.mechanism_description: Eye-level green view index (GVI) creates immediate visual engagement with nature during cycling, enhancing aesthetic pleasure and perceived environmental quality. Foreground vegetation provides proximate sensory stimulation that satellite-measured greenness (NDVI/canopy cover) fails to capture, directly influencing route preference and cycling motivation through improved visual experience and psychological comfort.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new canopy trees along cycling routes where none currently exist directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level, raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as perceived from the cyclist's vantage point.

Increasing the canopy spread and density of existing street trees enhances the amount of visible greenness at eye-level, further boosting GVI without relying on additional planting sites; this ensures routes with sparse existing trees also gain measurable green exposure.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two complementary actions on the same object, tier, and variable. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) introduces new canopy trees, while SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (secondary) enlarges existing canopy coverage—together they capture the IOM's directive to 'add or increase' street trees. Both signatures act on OBJ_VEG (canopy_tree) within TER_FG to maximise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Kang2020_3. That evidence establishes a DIR_POS relationship between eye-level street greenery and the probability of cycling behaviours (PRS_WAL_CYCL within PRF_USE). Crucially, the linked excerpts confirm that GVI—not overhead NDVI—is the metric positively correlated with cycling, justifying the foreground, eye-level focus of both signatures rather than a canopy-top or satellite-derived measure.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and visible area of green vegetation in the foreground visual field through strategic planting to enhance Green View Index (GVI) in urban streetscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI creates a perception of natural surveillance and environmental care, which signals maintained and monitored spaces. The presence of visible greenery reduces visual obstruction anxiety while increasing aesthetic appeal and sense of territoriality, thereby enhancing perceived safety. Green vegetation also attracts more pedestrian activity, creating 'eyes on the street' that further improve safety perceptions.

signatures.mechanism: Adding green vegetation in the foreground visual field increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI reflects greater visible greenery in the streetscape, which, per the linked evidence (Li, Zhang, & Li 2015a as cited in source), is positively associated with perceived safety in urban areas.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ: strategically planting vegetation in the foreground zone of urban streetscapes to increase the quantity and visible area of greenery. This directly raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Kang2020_4. The performance dimension is PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes), subdimension PRS_SOC_SAFE, with the relationship direction DIR_POS — meaning that increasing GVI through added foreground vegetation is positively associated with enhanced perceived safety in urban areas. The intervention operates on a single object type (OBJ_VEG) within a single depth zone (TER_FG), satisfying the S1 unit scope criterion.

source.citation: Kang, Y., Zhang, F., Gao, S., Lin, H., & Liu, Y. (2020). A review of urban physical environment sensing using street view imagery in public health studies. Annals of GIS, 26(3), 261–275.

operation.description: Reduce visual complexity of foreground vegetation by simplifying planting patterns, limiting species variety, and maintaining clear sightlines through selective thinning and pruning to achieve low-to-medium density arrangements that balance greenery provision with visual permeability.

pathway.mechanism_description: High visual complexity in vegetation creates visual barriers and concealment opportunities that reduce perceived safety by limiting surveillance capability and the prospect–refuge balance. By reducing excessive complexity through simplified textures and organized patterns, the intervention enhances visual penetration depth, improves detection of potential threats, and increases perceived control over the environment, thereby strengthening the sense of safety and comfort in urban green spaces.

signatures.mechanism: Simplifying planting patterns and limiting species variety reduces the number of shape-defining points and irregular canopy dispersions that drive visual complexity, directly lowering the compositional contribution to the Total Vegetation Index (IND_VEG).

Selective thinning and pruning reduces vegetation density from high to low-to-medium levels, decreasing the proportional area of vegetation in the scene and restoring visual permeability and clear sightlines.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_TEX (primary) and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (secondary) form a coordinated reduction on foreground vegetation. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Kawshalya2022_3, the Total Vegetation Index (IND_VEG) exhibits a negative relationship (DIR_NEG) with Perceived Safety (PRS_SOC_SAFE) under the social outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC). The primary signature simplifies planting patterns and limits species variety, reducing the shape-defining points and irregular branching textures that Kawshalya et al. identify as key drivers of visual complexity. The secondary signature complements this by thinning canopy density to low-to-medium levels, reducing the proportional vegetation area captured by IND_VEG and improving visual permeability. Together the two signatures target the medium-complexity optimum the authors identify as ideal for perceived safety and comfort, following the significant bell-shaped (cubic) relationship between complexity and safety (R²=0.439, p<.000). Lowering IND_VEG through both pattern simplification and density reduction is therefore expected to improve perceived safety outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.
