source.citation: Kawshalya, L. W. G., Weerasinghe, U. G. D., & Chandrasekara, D. P. (2022). The impact of visual complexity on perceived safety and comfort of the users: A study on urban streetscape of Sri Lanka. PLOS ONE, 17(8), e0272074.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of eye-level vegetation along street corridors to enhance green visual interface within pedestrian viewsheds (typically 1.5-2m height range). This includes adding street trees with lower canopy branches, vertical greenery systems, planted buffers, and layered shrub compositions that maximize green visibility from walking perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Green View Index (GVI) at eye-level creates visually appealing walking environments that increase pedestrian comfort and psychological restoration, thereby motivating longer duration and greater frequency of utilitarian walking trips (e.g., commuting, errands). The enhanced visual greenery acts as a positive environmental cue that reduces perceived effort and increases route attractiveness for functional walking activities.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees with low canopy branches (1.5–2 m) directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level in streetscape imagery, raising GVI along the corridor and strengthening the green visual interface that supports utilitarian walking.

Layered shrub compositions and planted buffers fill the eye-level visual band (1.5–2 m) between tree trunks and ground plane, closing gaps in the green view and contributing continuous green coverage that further elevates GVI from the pedestrian perspective.

Vertical greenery systems on walls and structures convert hard-surface pixels to green pixels within the pedestrian viewshed, extending green coverage onto built surfaces that would otherwise reduce GVI, thereby amplifying the composite green visual interface.

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates three simultaneous vegetation additions—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary)—all operating in the foreground tier to maximise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Ki2021_3. By layering canopy trees with low branches, dense shrub buffers, and vertical greenery systems within the same eye-level visual band, the intervention produces a compounding increase in green pixel proportion captured from the pedestrian walking perspective. The linked evidence (SVCs_P_Ki2021_3) reports a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 11.070) between GVI and Utilitarian Walking Time within the performance dimension PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_WALK, indicating that the combined GVI uplift from this superimposed vegetation strategy is expected to meaningfully increase the duration of utilitarian walking along treated street corridors.

source.citation: Ki, D., & Lee, S. (2021). Analyzing the effects of Green View Index of neighborhood streets on walking time using Google Street View and deep learning. Landscape and Urban Planning, 205, 103920.

operation.description: Increase street tree canopy coverage and density along utilitarian walking routes in low-income neighborhoods to enhance green view index. Focus on arterial roads, commercial corridors, and pedestrian pathways connecting residential areas to transit stations, schools, and shopping districts.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher green visibility (GVI) along utilitarian walking routes creates more pleasant pedestrian environments that reduce perceived walking effort and heat stress, thereby encouraging residents to choose walking over motorized transportation for daily functional trips. The negative direction indicates that increased GVI is associated with reduced utilitarian walking time, likely because improved route aesthetics makes walking more efficient and comfortable, potentially shifting trip patterns. Low-income populations show heightened sensitivity as they have fewer transportation alternatives and greater reliance on walking infrastructure quality.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees along utilitarian walking routes directly increases the proportion of green pixels in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater canopy coverage and density along corridors amplifies visible green space, which the evidence links to changes in utilitarian walking time.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a single-operation intervention that introduces canopy trees along arterial roads, commercial corridors, and pedestrian pathways in low-income neighborhoods to boost the Green View Index. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Ki2021_5, increasing IND_GVI is associated with a negative directional effect (DIR_NEG, coefficient −2.779) on utilitarian walking time under the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK). The evidence base—drawing on street-view-derived visible green space and tree cover metrics that consistently differentiate high- and low-perception environments (Excerpts 1–4)—supports the premise that augmenting street tree canopy coverage elevates GVI, which in turn is associated with reduced utilitarian walking time, indicating that greener streetscapes facilitate more efficient or more frequent purposive walking trips.

operation.description: Install visible accessibility features including sidewalks with clear wayfinding, pedestrian-scale streetlights, benches at regular intervals, and curb cuts to enhance walkway infrastructure and visual permeability in the foreground layer

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced walkway visibility and accessibility features (IND_VAC) improve perceived safety (PRF_SAF) through increased legibility of circulation routes, better nighttime illumination reducing concealment opportunities, and passive surveillance from amenity users. The addition of structured pedestrian infrastructure creates territorial definition and signals maintained public space, thereby increasing TrueSkill Safety Scores through enhanced environmental cues of safety and social presence

signatures.mechanism: Installing sidewalks and curb cuts increases the amount of visible, accessible walkway surface in the foreground, directly raising the proportion of image pixels classified as accessibility features by PSPNet segmentation, thereby increasing the Visible Accessibility Index (IND_VAC).

Adding pedestrian-scale streetlights and benches at regular intervals introduces a rhythmic arrangement of accessibility-classified built elements across the foreground view, amplifying the visible accessibility signal and reinforcing perceptual cues of a maintained, safe pedestrian environment.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes ground-plane and built-furniture additions within the same foreground tier. SIG_ADD_GRD_FG_SIZ adds sidewalk area and curb cuts, while SIG_ADD_BLT_FG_POS introduces streetlights and benches in a regularised positional rhythm. Together they compound the Visible Accessibility Index (IND_VAC) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Larkin2021_1. That evidence reports a 1.45 percentage-point difference in visible accessibility between the most-safe (Q4) and least-safe (Q1) street locations, with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS) to the TrueSkill Safety Score under performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_SAFE. By simultaneously increasing the segmented sidewalk pixel proportion and the count of accessibility-classified furniture objects, the combined intervention is expected to shift IND_VAC upward, driving a positive change in perceived safety consistent with the observed Q4–Q1 differential.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Larkin, A., Gu, X., Chen, L., & Hystad, P. (2021). Predicting perceptions of the built environment using GIS, satellite and street view image approaches. Landscape and Urban Planning, 216, 104257.

operation.description: Optimize street vegetation distribution to create coherent, clustered patterns rather than random or scattered configurations along roadways, emphasizing spatial continuity and visual grouping of green elements

pathway.mechanism_description: Clustered vegetation arrangement enhances visual coherence and spatial legibility, creating predictable rhythmic patterns that improve environmental readability. This structured green distribution establishes clear visual hierarchy and territorial definition, reducing spatial ambiguity and perceived risk. The coherent vegetation texture provides consistent visual cues for wayfinding and territorial demarcation, which enhances user confidence and perceived safety through improved spatial orientation and reduced concealment opportunities

signatures.mechanism: Rearranging street trees from scattered, random placements into deliberate spatial clusters along the roadway. This directly increases the Visible Green Distribution indicator by raising the spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I) of green pixels in street-level views, shifting the pattern from fragmented to coherently grouped canopy masses perceived across 10–50 m stretches.

Coordinating understory shrub plantings within and between tree clusters to fill visual gaps at pedestrian eye level. This reinforces the spatial continuity of green elements captured by the distribution metric, ensuring the clustering reads as a continuous vegetated zone rather than isolated canopy events when viewed from the immediate foreground.

scope.description: SCP_S2 coordinates two superimposed signatures—SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS (canopy_tree) and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS (shrub)—to optimise the spatial distribution of street vegetation from scattered to clustered configurations. As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Larkin2021_2, Visible Green Distribution (IND_VGD) is positively associated with perceived safety (PRS_SOC_SAFE): locations in the highest safety quintile exhibit a mean green-space distribution score of 93.38% compared with 88.43% in the lowest quintile (Δ = 4.95, p < 0.001), while the 'plant distribution' variable contributes the largest single street-image importance weight (0.79) in the safety regression. By clustering canopy trees at the mid-ground scale and reinforcing these clusters with foreground shrub continuity, the intervention raises the spatial coherence of visible green pixels, increasing IND_VGD and thereby improving TrueSkill Safety Scores in the PRF_SOC performance dimension (DIR_POS).

source.citation: Larkin, A., Gu, X., Chen, L., & Hystad, P. (2021). Predicting perceptions of the built environment using GIS, satellite and street view image approaches. Landscape and Urban Planning, 216, 104257.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and coverage of visible vegetation (including trees, shrubs, and herbaceous plants) in street-level foreground views to enhance Green View Index (GVI) values in urban streetscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced visual exposure to green vegetation in the foreground improves psychological restoration, reduces mental fatigue, and promotes emotional well-being through biophilic responses. Higher GVI correlates with lower stress levels, improved mood states, and enhanced overall mental health outcomes at the population level, as measured through psychometric assessments and health surveys.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees in the immediate street-level view contributes the largest single gain in green-pixel proportion within GVI semantic segmentation, as tree canopies occupy a substantial vertical and horizontal share of the photographic frame captured by street-view cameras.

Adding shrubs fills the mid-height visual band of the street-level photograph, increasing cumulative green-pixel coverage in the GVI calculation where tree canopies leave gaps between trunk base and crown.
Adding herbaceous ground-cover plants replaces non-green ground-plane pixels (sidewalk, bare soil) with green pixels in the lower portion of the street-view frame, further raising GVI values.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three foreground vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (ground_cover, secondary)—to collectively maximise the proportion of green pixels detected by deep-learning semantic segmentation in street-level photographs. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Lee2024_1, the target indicator is the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which quantifies the ratio of vegetation-classified pixels to total pixels in horizontal street-view imagery. By layering canopy trees (upper frame), shrubs (mid-height frame), and herbaceous ground cover (lower frame) within the same foreground tier, the intervention fills successive visual strata and minimises non-green gaps. The performance dimension is PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_PHY_GNRL, with the expected outcome being improved population-level health status and human well-being through enhanced everyday greenness exposure. While the evidence reports no stable direction and no quantified effect size for direction and effect size, the review establishes a methodological link between higher GVI and positive health associations measured across multiple studies.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Lee, D.H. et al. (2024). A Review on Recent Deep Learning-Based Semantic Segmentation for Urban Greenness Measurement. Sensors, 24, 2245.

operation.description: Mitigate the 'wall effect' by applying vertical greening systems or artistic decorative elements to blank wall surfaces to transform monotonous vertical facades into visually enriched interfaces in the foreground viewing zone.

pathway.mechanism_description: Large blank walls create visual monotony and oppressive spatial enclosure that elevates perceived depression scores. By overlaying green vegetation or artistic textures onto wall surfaces, the intervention breaks up the uniform plane, introduces natural or cultural visual interest, softens hard architectural edges, and provides positive visual stimulation that reduces psychological distress and depressive feelings in urban environments.

signatures.mechanism: Vertical greening systems (climbing plants, flower boxes, vertical gardens) are introduced onto previously blank wall surfaces, layering living texture over inert masonry. This directly reduces the perceived dominance of bare wall area captured by the Wall Ratio indicator (IND_WAL) by converting monotonous hard surface into biophilic, visually complex interface, thereby lowering the Perceived Depressing Score.

Artistic decorative elements are applied to remaining exposed facade surfaces, modifying their visual texture from blank and monotonous to varied and enriched. This complements the greening layer by ensuring that any residual wall area still visible contributes visual interest rather than reinforcing the 'wall effect', further attenuating the negative perceptual impact captured by IND_WAL.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes two treatments onto the same blank wall surfaces in the foreground tier. The primary signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX introduces climbing plants and vertical greening systems, while the secondary signature SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_TEX applies artistic decorative treatments to the underlying facade. Together they target the Wall Ratio indicator (IND_WAL) documented in evidence SVCs_P_Lei2024_1, which shows a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.136) with the Perceived Depressing Score (0–5) under the performance dimension PRF_EMO / PRS_MNT_MOOD. By visually transforming monotonous wall surfaces into enriched, textured interfaces, the combined intervention is expected to invert the 'wall effect'—reducing the perceptual prominence of bare walls and thereby lowering residents' depressive perceptions, improving mental-health mood outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Perceived Depressing Score (0-5)

source.citation: Lei, Y., Zhou, H., Xue, L., Yuan, L., Liu, Y., Wang, M., & Wang, C. (2024). Evaluating and Comparing Human Perceptions of Streets in Two Megacities by Integrating Street-View Images, Deep Learning, and Space Syntax. Buildings, 14(6), 1847.

operation.description: Envelop underside surfaces of elevated viaduct structures with green infrastructure elements (vertical gardens, hanging vegetation, modular planting systems) to visually soften and naturalize the ceiling texture, transforming oppressive overhead concrete into biophilic overhead canopy within pocket parks beneath transportation infrastructure.

pathway.mechanism_description: Green enveloping of viaduct ceilings reduces psychological depression by: (1) masking oppressive gray concrete surfaces that induce negative affect through environmental monotony and institutional aesthetics; (2) introducing natural textures and organic patterns that provide visual interest and cognitive restoration; (3) creating perceived enclosure by vegetated 'sky' that enhances sense of intimate refuge rather than infrastructural exposure; (4) signaling environmental care and place identity that counters perceptions of neglected residual spaces. The textural transformation from hard industrial surfaces to soft living materials directly counteracts depressing environmental cues.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vertical gardens, hanging vegetation, and modular planting systems onto the underside of the viaduct creates a new layer of living green texture directly overhead. This biophilic textural addition visually masks the exposed concrete ceiling, replacing a hard, monolithic surface with varied, organic foliage. By changing the visual composition of the overhead view, the intervention reduces the dominance of bare ceiling material captured by the Ceiling View Index (IND_CEI), thereby lowering its positive contribution to Perceived Depressing Score.

The existing concrete underside of the viaduct, which registers as a dominant 'ceiling' element in street-view segmentation, is visually modified from an oppressive uniform grey surface to a softened, vegetated overhead plane. This textural transformation does not remove the structure but alters its perceived material character, reducing the psychological weight that artificial ceiling surfaces exert on mood perception as evidenced by the positive association between IND_CEI and depressing scores.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two superimposed signatures within the immediate sensory zone beneath elevated viaducts. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX introduces climbing plants, hanging vegetation, and modular green systems onto the overhead structure, while SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_TEX simultaneously transforms the perceived texture of the built overhead surface from bare concrete to a biophilic canopy. Together they target the Ceiling View Index (IND_CEI) identified in evidence SVCs_P_Lei2024_3, which shows a positive association (β = 0.236) with Perceived Depressing Score (0–5) under the performance dimension PRF_EMO / PRS_MNT_MOOD. By layering vegetation over the dominant ceiling element, the intervention is expected to attenuate the depressing perception triggered by exposed artificial overhead surfaces—a relationship confirmed in Shanghai's viaduct corridors—mirroring the successful Chengdu precedent where viaduct underspaces were converted into green pocket parks that effectively reduced residents' negative perceptions (Excerpts 3, 5, 9). The expected outcome direction is a decrease in depressing scores as the visually oppressive ceiling is naturalized.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Lei, Y., Zhou, H., Xue, L., Yuan, L., Liu, Y., Wang, M., & Wang, C. (2024). Evaluating and Comparing Human Perceptions of Streets in Two Megacities by Integrating Street-View Images, Deep Learning, and Space Syntax. Buildings, 14(6), 1847.

operation.description: Quantify the visible green vegetation coverage at street level using modified Green View Index (GVI) methodology with enhanced sampling: capture 18 panoramic images per location (6 horizontal headings at 60° intervals × 3 vertical angles: 0°, 30°, 60°) to measure the proportion of green pixels in the pedestrian visual field

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher visible greenery coverage in the foreground street environment enhances perceived safety through multiple pathways: (1) increased visual softness and naturalness reduce perceived threat, (2) well-maintained vegetation signals community care and informal surveillance, (3) green elements provide psychological restorative effects reducing anxiety, and (4) vegetation presence indicates active use and maintenance of public spaces, collectively improving crowdsourced safety perception scores

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees at street level increases the proportion of green pixels captured across the pedestrian panoramic visual field, directly raising the modified Green View Index (GVI). The paper demonstrates that vegetation above the horizon (i.e., tree canopies taller than the 2.5 m camera height) is a stronger predictor of perceived safety than vegetation below the horizon, because tall canopies provide visual enclosure and natural-surveillance cues ('eyes on the street') without obstructing sightlines at eye level, contrasting with view-obstructing shrubs which are associated with increased crime.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ is a single-signature operation encoding the addition of visible canopy-tree vegetation to increase the modified Green View Index (IND_GVI). As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Li2015_1, GVI exhibits a positive relationship (DIR_POS, r = 0.388) with crowdsourced perceived safety scores (Place Pulse 1.0) within the social-outcomes performance dimension (PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_SAFE). Increasing the amount (VAR_SIZ) of canopy-tree coverage in the streetscape raises the green-pixel proportion across 18 panoramic sampling directions (6 headings × 3 vertical angles), thereby shifting the GVI upward and producing the expected positive safety-perception outcome. The paper specifically finds that in residential, institutional, commercial, and open-land areas, the visibility of vegetation higher than 2.5 m has significant positive correlations with perceived safety, confirming canopy trees as the operative subtype.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and canopy size of street trees and foreground vegetation along roadways to enhance the proportion of visible greenery in street-level views, specifically targeting the eye-level visual field captured from pedestrian and vehicular perspectives.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding street trees and vegetation in the foreground tier directly increases the Green View Index (GVI) by expanding the pixel proportion of green elements within Google Street View imagery. Street-level trees positioned in close proximity to viewing angles occupy larger portions of the visual field, thereby maximizing their contribution to GVI calculations through computational image analysis that quantifies green pixel density.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional street trees in the immediate pedestrian zone directly increases the count of green pixel clusters captured in eye-level panoramic imagery, raising the Green View Index (GVI) proportion.

Enlarging canopy spread of existing street trees along the roadway corridor expands the areal coverage of green pixels across the mid-depth visual field, amplifying GVI values beyond what tree count alone achieves.

Adding low-to-mid-height foreground vegetation fills the lower band of the eye-level visual field that canopy trees leave open, contributing additional green pixels to the GVI calculation at the stratum closest to the observer.

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates three simultaneous vegetation signatures to maximise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Li2017_1. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree) serves as the primary driver, introducing new street trees in the 0–10 m pedestrian zone where each added tree contributes a large block of green pixels to every panoramic frame. SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree) augments canopy spread of existing trees visible at 10–50 m along the road corridor, broadening the green silhouette in the middle depth of the view and increasing cumulative GVI without requiring new planting sites. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub) layers understory vegetation beneath and between tree canopies, filling the lower portion of the eye-level visual field that overhead canopies cannot occlude. Together the three signatures superimpose across two vertical strata and two depth zones, jointly raising the proportion of visible greenery captured from pedestrian and vehicular viewpoints. The linked performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU indicates that increasing GVI is associated with enhanced perceived naturalness of the streetscape; the relationship direction (DIR_NA) and effect size in SVCs_P_Li2017_1 reflect the paper's focus on validating the measurement method rather than reporting a directional preference coefficient, so the expected outcome is an increase in GVI magnitude rather than a quantified preference shift.

operation.description: Increase tree canopy coverage in street canyons through strategic planting of trees to reduce sky view factor and enhance spatial enclosure

pathway.mechanism_description: Tree canopies occupy overhead space within the street canyon, physically obstructing the visible sky area when viewed from street level. Larger canopy size and increased tree density progressively reduce the Sky View Factor by blocking sightlines to the sky hemisphere, thereby modulating the degree of openness and creating shaded microenvironments along streetscapes

signatures.mechanism: Strategic planting of canopy trees within street canyons increases the amount of overhead vegetation cover, directly occluding sky pixels visible from the pedestrian viewpoint. This additional canopy mass reduces the Sky View Factor (a geometric ratio of visible sky to total hemisphere) and thereby heightens the sense of spatial enclosure experienced along the street.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that adds canopy tree volume to street canyons. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2017_2 uses the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) indicator, which quantifies the degree of sky visibility within street canyons. By increasing the size/amount of canopy tree coverage, the intervention reduces SVF values. The performance dimension is PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL; enhanced spatial enclosure from denser tree canopy is expected to alter the perceived visual quality of the streetscape, though the original study reports no directional relationship (DIR_NA) and no quantified effect size, as the paper focuses on measurement methodology (GSV-based mapping) rather than preference testing.

operation.description: Implement strategic shrub planting in industrial zones to increase shrub coverage area. Focus on establishing continuous shrub layers that can provide effective ground shading and evapotranspiration surfaces, particularly in areas with high heat emission sources such as parking lots, equipment yards, and buffer zones between industrial facilities.

pathway.mechanism_description: Shrubs reduce land surface temperature through multiple mechanisms: (1) canopy shading reduces direct solar radiation absorption by built surfaces; (2) evapotranspiration from shrub foliage converts sensible heat to latent heat, cooling ambient air; (3) shrub layers create vertical thermal barriers that mitigate heat radiation from industrial surfaces; (4) dense shrub coverage increases surface albedo compared to bare ground or impervious surfaces. In industrial contexts, shrubs demonstrate significant cooling performance despite lower height compared to trees, as they effectively cover larger ground areas and provide continuous thermal regulation at the micro-scale.

signatures.mechanism: Adding continuous shrub layers across parking lots, equipment yards, and buffer zones increases shrub coverage area (Shrub Ratio, IND_SHR). The expanded shrub canopy provides ground-level shading that intercepts incoming solar radiation before it heats impervious surfaces, while simultaneously contributing evapotranspiration that converts sensible heat into latent heat, collectively reducing Land Surface Temperature.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ that introduces new shrub planting to increase the Shrub Ratio (IND_SHR) across industrial zones. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2022_1, increasing Shrub Ratio has a negative relationship (DIR_NEG) with Land Surface Temperature (LST) under the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP), with an effect size of -3.07, meaning each unit increase in shrub ratio is associated with an approximate 3.07-unit decrease in LST. The intervention directly raises the indicator value by converting bare or impervious ground into vegetated shrub cover, thereby driving the expected cooling outcome.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and coverage of vegetation elements within the middleground visual field by adding green infrastructure, street trees, green walls, or landscape plantings to enhance visible greenery from eye-level perspectives in urban streetscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Green View Index (GVI) at middleground distances provides visual access to natural elements that trigger psychological restoration processes through attention restoration theory mechanisms. Increased vegetation visibility reduces mental fatigue, promotes involuntary attention, and facilitates stress recovery by offering softly fascinating natural stimuli that allow directed attention to rest, thereby enhancing perceived restorativeness as measured by PRS.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vegetation elements (street trees, green walls, landscape plantings) into the middleground visual field increases the pixel ratio of greenery captured in eye-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). The added canopy and foliage mass enlarges the proportion of green pixels within the scene, which the evidence links to higher perceived psychological restoration.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that adds vegetation to the middleground zone of urban streetscapes or park paths, directly increasing the Green View Index (IND_GVI)—the pixel ratio of greenery in eye-level imagery. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_5 reports a positive relationship (DIR_POS, standardised coefficient 0.044) between GVI and the Perceived Restoration Scale (PRS) under the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO, subdimension PRS_MNT_REST). The study validated greenery as a globally significant explanatory factor for psychological restoration (excerpts 11, 18, 19, 20), with the effect strongest in areas where middleground vegetation dominates the visual field (excerpt 7). Thus, increasing vegetative coverage at this tier is expected to raise GVI and, in turn, enhance perceived restorativeness.

operation.description: Introduce visible water features (e.g., fountains, ponds, streams) in the middleground viewing zone to create visually accessible blue space elements that enhance environmental legibility and spatial coherence. Position water elements at 10-30 meters from primary viewing points to optimize perceptual engagement while maintaining adequate visual detail and auditory accessibility.

pathway.mechanism_description: Visible water features facilitate restorative experiences through multiple pathways: (1) providing 'soft fascination' that enables attention restoration theory (ART) mechanisms—water's dynamic yet predictable movement captures involuntary attention without mental fatigue; (2) enhancing perceived naturalness and environmental preference, which triggers stress reduction via psycho-evolutionary theory (PET) pathways; (3) creating multi-sensory engagement through visual and auditory stimulation that promotes mindfulness and present-moment awareness; (4) serving as focal points for contemplation that support reflection and emotional regulation. The middleground placement optimizes these effects by balancing visual prominence with spatial comfort, allowing sustained observation without overwhelming proximity.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing previously absent water features increases the proportion of visible blue-space pixels captured in scene imagery, directly raising the Water View Index (IND_WAT). The added water surface area at middleground distance provides sufficient visual detail and auditory cues (e.g., flowing or splashing sounds) to trigger innate affective responses described by Stress Recovery Theory, thereby supporting psychological restoration.

scope.description: SCP_S1 operation comprising a single signature SIG_ADD_WAT_MG_SIZ. The intervention adds water elements (fountains, ponds, or streams) into the middleground zone, directly increasing the Water View Index (IND_WAT) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_9. That evidence links IND_WAT to the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST (psychological restoration), with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS): a higher water view index is associated with greater restorative benefit. The paper notes that water is an attractive visual element whose visibility presents positive influence on restoration (excerpts 13, 16) and that green/blue space accessibility relates to better mental status (excerpt 5). While the OLS model identified water as a globally significant positive predictor of environmental restoration (excerpt 9), the quantile regression showed non-significant effects at specific quantiles (excerpt 22), introducing some uncertainty. Nevertheless, the global positive association and the theoretical grounding in SRT support the encoding of a positive expected outcome.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation coverage to achieve >50% Green View Index (GVI) through strategic placement of trees, shrubs, and vertical greening elements within pedestrian viewshed at eye level (1.6m height). Prioritize canopy trees along sidewalks and planting strips to maximize visual greenness in foreground perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced visual exposure to greenery at street level reduces psychological stress and encourages active transportation (walking/cycling), which collectively lower cardiovascular disease risk factors including hypertension, sedentary behavior, and chronic inflammation. The 50% GVI threshold represents a critical dose-response point where cumulative visual contact with nature during daily mobility patterns produces measurable reductions in ischemic heart disease incidence through stress mitigation pathways and increased physical activity.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along sidewalks and planting strips introduces the largest single contributor to green pixel ratio in street-level panoramic imagery. Tree canopies at eye-level viewshed (1.6 m) dominate the upper-middle portion of the visual field, directly elevating GVI toward the >50% target.

Shrubs fill the lower band of the pedestrian visual field beneath tree canopies, adding green pixels in the zone between ground surface and canopy, thereby supplementing GVI where trunk gaps would otherwise expose hard surfaces.

Vertical greening elements (climbing plants on walls and facades) convert building-view pixels into vegetation pixels in the semantic segmentation used to compute GVI, reducing the Building View Index (BUI) while simultaneously raising GVI within the same viewshed frame.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 superposition operation coordinates three vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary)—to jointly maximise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as measured by DeepLabv3+ semantic segmentation of street-view imagery. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2025_1 demonstrates that higher GVI is associated with reduced Ischemic Heart Disease (IHD) incidence (rate ratio = 0.88, DIR_NEG) within the performance dimension PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_CVD. The superposition logic layers canopy (upper visual field), shrub (middle-lower visual field), and climbing plant (vertical surfaces) within the same foreground tier so that green pixels accumulate across distinct bands of the panoramic image, pushing GVI past the 50% threshold at which the study finds statistically significant cardiovascular protection. The three layers are non-substitutable: canopy alone leaves trunk-level gaps exposing hard surfaces, while climbing plants alone cannot provide overhead shade or lower-field greenness. Their simultaneous deployment ensures continuous green coverage across the full eye-level visual cone.

operation.description: Optimize street canyon geometry by adjusting building height-to-width ratios to maximize sky view factor (SVF), ensuring adequate visual openness and reducing spatial enclosure in urban corridors

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher SVF values increase visible sky proportion from ground level, enhancing natural light exposure, reducing perceived spatial oppression, and promoting psychological restoration. Increased daylight penetration and improved air circulation may reduce cardiovascular stress responses, thereby lowering ischemic heart disease (IHD) incidence through enhanced environmental quality and restorative urban experiences

signatures.mechanism: Modifying building height-to-width ratios within the street canyon reduces the vertical massing of facades relative to street width, thereby increasing the proportion of sky visible from street level (SVF). Greater visual openness lowers perceived spatial enclosure and improves daylight and air-flow conditions in the urban corridor.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ) targeting the proportional geometry of built facades that define street canyons. By modulating the height-to-width ratio of buildings, the intervention increases the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) as measured through street-view image segmentation. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2025_2 demonstrates that higher SVF is negatively associated (DIR_NEG, effect size 0.7714) with Ischemic Heart Disease (IHD) incidence rate ratio within the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_PHY_CVD). Thus, increasing SVF through canyon geometry optimisation is expected to reduce cardiovascular disease burden among urban residents.

operation.description: Increase the visible area and density of trees in the foreground viewing zone to enhance Green View Index (GVI), particularly in streetscapes with low architectural quality. Priority should be given to street-level planting that maximizes canopy coverage within the visual field.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI increases the proportion of natural green elements in the visual field, compensating for poor architectural aesthetics through biophilic attraction. The relationship between GVI and general landscape score is non-linear (DIR_NLN), suggesting threshold effects where modest increases in visible greenery yield disproportionate perceptual benefits, especially in visually degraded environments. Trees in the foreground tier provide immediate visual screening and aesthetic enhancement.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new canopy trees into the foreground streetscape directly increases the total area of green-coloured pixels captured in panoramic street-level imagery, raising the Green View Index (GVI). The IOM targets streetscapes with low existing greenery where each added tree produces a proportionally large gain in visible green coverage.

Strategic arrangement and spacing of the added street trees maximises canopy-to-canopy density within the pedestrian visual field. By positioning trees to fill gaps in the viewing cone and optimise overlap of canopy silhouettes, the proportion of green pixels per unit of streetscape length is increased beyond what random placement would achieve, amplifying the GVI effect of Signature 1.

scope.description: This is an S2 coordinated operation combining SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS (secondary) in a superposition pattern — both signatures act on the same object type (OBJ_VEG / canopy_tree) within the same depth tier (TER_FG), layering quantity and arrangement considerations onto one element class. As linked evidence SVCs_P_Cheng2017_2 documents, the indicator Green View Index (IND_GVI) — defined as the proportion of green-coloured pixels in panoramic street-level images — is associated with the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL, measured via a General Landscape Score averaged across 100 volunteers. The relationship is DIR_NLN (non-linear), suggesting that GVI gains improve perceived streetscape quality but with diminishing or threshold-dependent returns. The coordinated addition and strategic positioning of foreground canopy trees is expected to shift GVI upward, thereby improving perceived aesthetic quality, with particular efficacy in streetscapes characterised by low architectural quality where green elements dominate the evaluative response.

operation.description: Increase foreground tree coverage and density to elevate Green View Index (GVI) levels, transforming low-vegetation urban corridors ('Urban Jungle' typology) into greener streetscapes ('Flanked by Nature' or 'Boulevard' typologies) through strategic planting of street trees along pedestrian sight lines.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced foreground tree presence directly increases the proportion of vegetation visible in the visual field, which positively correlates with aesthetic perception scores. The study reveals spatio-temporal evolution patterns showing that higher GVI values (achieved through increased tree coverage) significantly improve visual beauty ratings in street-level environments. Trees in the foreground tier have maximum visual impact as they dominate the immediate viewing cone of pedestrians.

signatures.mechanism: Planting street trees along pedestrian sight lines increases the proportion of vegetation pixels captured in street-level imagery, directly elevating the Green View Index (GVI). Higher canopy coverage transforms the visual composition from built-dominated ('Urban Jungle', Cluster 1 — highest construction view factor, lowest vegetation) toward vegetation-rich typologies ('Flanked by Nature', Cluster 2 — highest green view factor), thereby raising perceived visual beauty.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that adds canopy trees in the foreground tier to increase vegetative coverage and density within pedestrian sight lines. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_1 demonstrates that elevating the Green View Index (IND_GVI) has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, r = 0.66) with the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF), measured via the Visual Beauty Score (Place Pulse). The intervention shifts streetscape classification from low-greenery clusters (Cluster 1: 'Urban Jungle') toward high-greenery clusters (Cluster 2: 'Flanked by Nature' or Cluster 6: 'Boulevard') by increasing the vegetation view factor at the expense of built-environment dominance, directly improving perceived visual quality.

operation.description: Increase street tree canopy coverage and density in residential neighborhoods to enhance Green View Index (GVI) at eye-level perspective, targeting visual field occupation of vegetation within pedestrian viewsheds along primary walking routes and residential frontages.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced GVI through increased tree canopy presence creates restorative visual environments that reduce psychological stress and negative emotional states. The mechanism operates through attention restoration theory: natural green elements in the visual field reduce rumination, lower cortical arousal, and facilitate emotional regulation, thereby decreasing negative affect as measured by PANAS scale (direction: negative correlation).

signatures.mechanism: Adding street tree canopy along primary walking routes and residential frontages increases the proportion of green pixels captured in eye-level street view imagery, thereby raising the neighbourhood-averaged Green View Index (GVI). Greater visual occupation of vegetation within the pedestrian viewshed fills the horizontal visual field with foliage, which the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Liu2021_1) associates with reduced Negative Affect (PANAS, β = −10.293, p < 0.01).

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, in which street tree canopy coverage and density are increased along neighbourhood walking routes. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Liu2021_1, the indicator IND_GVI (Green View Index, measured from Tencent street-view imagery within a 1000 m residential buffer) is the independent variable. A higher GVI is associated with lower Negative Affect scores (PANAS 10–50 scale; β = −10.293, SE = 3.423, p < 0.01), mapping to performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_MOOD, with relationship direction DIR_NEG. The intervention therefore targets a measurable increase in eye-level vegetation visibility to produce a beneficial reduction in negative mood among residential pedestrians.

operation.description: Remove visual obstructions (vegetation, structures, or barriers) between streets and water bodies to increase blue visibility index (BVI) from pedestrian viewpoints, creating clear sightlines to blue spaces in the background layer.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced visual access to water features increases blue visibility index, which positively correlates with life satisfaction (SWLS scores 5-35) through psychological restoration, stress reduction, and improved environmental quality perception. The presence of visible water in urban landscapes provides calming visual stimuli and strengthens human-nature connection.

signatures.mechanism: Removes waterfront vegetation (shrubs, hedges, dense planting) that physically obstructs the horizontal sightline from the pedestrian street-level viewpoint toward the water body, directly increasing the proportion of blue pixels captured in the Blue View Index (BVI).

Removes built barriers (walls, railings, fences along waterfront walkways) that further occlude the view corridor to background blue spaces, complementing the vegetation removal to maximise the continuous visible water surface area registered in the BVI.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention employs a reduction pattern: SIG_REM_VEG_MG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_REM_BLT_MG_SIZ (secondary) work together to strip away the middle-ground visual obstructions that intercept sightlines between pedestrian streets and background water bodies. Evidence SVCs_P_Liu2021_3 demonstrates that increasing the Water View Index (IND_WAT)—the share of blue pixels visible from streetscape viewpoints—is positively associated (DIR_POS) with the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), specifically the sub-dimension PRS_MNT_WLBG (Wellbeing), measured via the Satisfaction with Life Scale (SWLS, 5–35). The source paper (Excerpts 9, 12, 25) notes that 'waterfront pedestrian walkways and waterfront vegetation blocked the view of water bodies,' depressing BVI and attenuating the salutogenic effect of blue space visibility. By coordinating the removal of both vegetative and built obstructions in the middle ground, the intervention opens clear view corridors to TER_BG water, raising BVI and thereby strengthening the positive link to life satisfaction reported in the evidence.

operation.description: Select tree species with low fractal dimension (simple leaf shapes, larger leaf surfaces) for street planting in areas with low green exposure ratio. Priority should be given to broadleaf species with smooth margins and reduced complexity in canopy texture to maximize cooling performance.

pathway.mechanism_description: Trees with low fractal dimension possess larger, less complex leaf structures that create denser, more continuous canopy coverage. This morphological characteristic enhances shading efficiency and increases the leaf area available for evapotranspiration, thereby producing stronger cooling effects on land surface temperature. The nonlinear relationship indicates that in streets with limited green space, selecting low-FD species amplifies the cooling benefit per unit of vegetation, compensating for lower overall green exposure.

signatures.mechanism: Substituting existing street tree species that have high fractal dimension (complex, finely dissected leaves and intricate canopy texture) with broadleaf species possessing low fractal dimension (simple leaf shapes, larger leaf surfaces, smooth margins). This replacement reduces the geometric complexity of the canopy texture, which increases the concentration and continuity of shade cast onto the street surface, thereby lowering land surface temperature more effectively in streets with low green exposure.

scope.description: SIG_REP_VEG_MG_TEX encodes a single species-substitution operation targeting the textural complexity of street canopy trees. As supported by evidence SVCs_P_Liu2022_1, the intervention lowers the Fractal Dimension indicator (IND_FRD) by replacing high-FD species with low-FD broadleaf species (FD < 0.65) in streets where green exposure is low (GVI < 0.1, NDVI < 2.5). The linked evidence shows a positive relationship (DIR_POS) between fractal dimension and Land Surface Temperature under the performance dimension PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP: as FD decreases (simpler, larger leaves), LST decreases, improving thermal comfort. The study demonstrates that streets with low green environment exposure are most sensitive to these morphological characteristics, meaning texture simplification in this context yields disproportionate cooling gains.

operation.description: Select vegetation species with high Color Saturation Index (CSI), characterized by light-colored or vivid leaves, for street green space planting. Prioritize species displaying bright green, yellow-green, or variegated foliage with enhanced color intensity rather than dark or dull leaf colors.

pathway.mechanism_description: High CSI vegetation exhibits greater reflectance in the visible spectrum, reducing solar radiation absorption and subsequent heat accumulation. The lighter and more saturated leaf colors increase albedo effect, thereby lowering surface temperatures through enhanced reflection of shortwave radiation. This color-mediated cooling mechanism operates through reduced thermal conversion at the leaf surface, creating a nonlinear cooling effect on Land Surface Temperature in street environments.

signatures.mechanism: Replacing dark- or dull-leaved vegetation species (low CSI) with light-colored or vivid-leaved species (high CSI, bright green, yellow-green, or variegated foliage) increases the collective Color Saturation Index of the street green space. Lighter-coloured foliage reflects a greater proportion of incoming shortwave radiation rather than absorbing it, reducing canopy heat loading and subsequent longwave re-emission, which lowers Land Surface Temperature at street level.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_REP_VEG_MG_COL) that substitutes dark- or dull-leaved street tree species with high-CSI, light-coloured or vivid-leaved species within the street green space corridor. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Liu2022_2, increasing the Color Saturation Index (IND_CSI) exhibits a negative relationship (DIR_NEG) with Land Surface Temperature (LST) under the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP). By raising CSI above the empirically identified threshold of ~13, the intervention enhances the cooling contribution of street vegetation, particularly on streets with low green environment exposure (GVI < 0.1, NDVI < 2.5) or high surrounding building density, where greenery morphology characteristics exert proportionally stronger influence on LST.

operation.description: Increase texture richness and visual complexity on building facades through architectural detailing, ornamentation, and material variation to enhance visual distinctiveness of middleground elements in public open spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced facade texture and architectural details provide stronger visual cues and memorable landmarks that improve spatial legibility. Deep learning-based analysis demonstrates that textured building surfaces with ornamental features create distinctive visual anchors that help users form cognitive maps and navigate spaces more effectively, contributing significantly to high legibility scores by increasing the recognizability and differentiation of urban environments

signatures.mechanism: Increasing texture richness and visual complexity on building facades through architectural detailing, ornamentation, and material variation enriches the surface pattern diversity of middleground built elements. This heightened textural differentiation strengthens facades as visually distinctive landmarks and boundary-defining elements, thereby improving the Legibility Index (IND_LEG) by making the spatial structure of the public open space more readable and memorable to users.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_TEX) that modifies the texture attribute of middleground building facades to enhance their visual distinctiveness. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Liu2023_3, visual elements such as landmarks and boundaries were identified as dominant contributors to high legibility scores (IND_LEG). By enriching facade texture through detailing and material variation, the intervention strengthens the landmark and boundary qualities of surrounding buildings—elements the source paper (excerpts [4], [5], [25]) confirms as significantly shaping visual quality of greenway/open-space landscapes. The expected outcome on the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_LEGI, is an increase in legibility through greater visual differentiation of middleground built form, consistent with the evidence that richer, more distinguishable visual elements support spatial legibility.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and spatial coverage of street-level vegetation within pedestrian viewsheds to enhance Green View Index (GVI) along recreational corridors and public outdoor spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street greenery at eye-level increases visual exposure to natural elements, creating more attractive and inviting outdoor environments. Higher GVI values improve environmental aesthetics and thermal comfort, which psychologically motivates residents to engage in recreational walking, jogging, and outdoor leisure activities. The visible presence of vegetation acts as a positive environmental cue that reinforces pro-health behaviors.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new street-level vegetation (predominantly canopy trees) increases the proportion of green pixels captured within pedestrian-perspective panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI along recreational corridors signals a greener, more restorative streetscape that is positively associated with greater engagement in recreational physical activity.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation, SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, representing the addition of street-level vegetation to increase its quantity and spatial coverage within pedestrian viewsheds. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Long2019_1 reports that a higher Green View Index (IND_GVI) is positively associated (DIR_POS) with recreational physical activity in outdoor environments (performance dimension PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_PHY_ACTV). The intervention directly elevates IND_GVI by expanding the volumetric presence of canopy trees along sidewalks and recreational corridors, thereby enlarging the share of visible greenery in eye-level street views. Because the evidence links increased visible street greenery to more frequent and sustained outdoor physical activity, the expected outcome direction is positive: greater vegetation quantity → higher GVI → enhanced recreational physical activity.

operation.description: Morph building envelope geometry by adjusting height-to-width ratios (H/W aspect ratio) of street canyons to optimize sky view factor and control solar access patterns throughout diurnal and seasonal cycles

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying building height relative to street width alters the sky view factor (SVF), which directly governs the amount of visible sky hemisphere from street level. Higher aspect ratios (taller buildings, narrower streets) reduce SVF and create deeper shadows with limited solar penetration, while lower aspect ratios increase SVF allowing greater solar exposure. This geometric transformation controls the spatiotemporal distribution of direct and diffuse solar radiation within the street canyon microclimate

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the height-to-width (H/W) aspect ratio of building facades forming street canyon walls directly reshapes the canyon cross-section geometry. This altered envelope proportion changes the solid angle of visible sky hemisphere from a pedestrian vantage point, thereby modifying Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). A lower H/W ratio opens the canyon, increasing SVF and solar radiation admission, while a higher H/W ratio constricts it, reducing SVF and solar access. The optimised geometry controls the spatiotemporal distribution of solar radiation within the canyon across diurnal and seasonal cycles, influencing mean radiant temperature (PRS_THR_MRT) at the human scale.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SHP encodes the morphing of building envelope geometry through modification of street canyon H/W aspect ratios. As supported by evidence SVCs_P_Long2019_2, this shape adjustment of built facades directly governs the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF), which mediates the spatiotemporal pattern of solar radiation reaching the canyon floor. The performance dimension is thermal comfort (PRF_THR), specifically mean radiant temperature (PRS_THR_MRT), with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS): optimised H/W proportions yield improved thermal comfort outcomes by calibrating solar exposure. The intervention operates on a single object type (OBJ_BLT/facade) within one depth zone, satisfying SCP_S1 scope.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and density of vegetation elements at eye-level (1.5-2m height) along street corridors to enhance Green View Index (GVI), focusing on foreground visual field through strategic placement of street trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery systems within pedestrian sightlines.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced eye-level greenness creates visually appealing streetscapes that positively influence walking behavior through multiple pathways: (1) increased aesthetic quality and visual interest reduce perceived walking distance and effort, (2) street-level vegetation provides psychological restoration and stress reduction during walking, (3) green corridors serve as wayfinding cues and destination attractors, and (4) foreground vegetation creates a sense of enclosure and comfort that encourages pedestrian activity. The positive association between GVI and walking odds suggests that visible greenery at human scale acts as a behavioral nudge for active mobility choices.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees along corridors increases the proportion of greenery pixels captured at eye-level in pedestrian sightlines, directly raising Green View Index values. Street trees contribute the largest single green-pixel area in streetscape imagery due to their canopy spread intersecting the visual cone.

Adding shrubs at 1.5–2 m height fills the mid-canopy gap in the eye-level visual field, increasing green pixel density in the band most salient to pedestrians and closing vertical gaps between tree canopies and ground plane.

Vertical greenery systems convert hard facade and wall surfaces into green pixels within the streetscape image, supplementing tree and shrub cover on surfaces that would otherwise register as built mass, thereby maximising GVI across the full panoramic sweep.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three foreground vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary)—to collectively increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as measured at eye-level along street corridors. Evidence SVCs_P_Lu2018_1 demonstrates a positive relationship (DIR_POS) between GVI and walkability (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK), with an odds ratio of 1.149 (95 % CI 1.035–1.276, p = 0.009) indicating that a one standard deviation increase in GVI raises the likelihood of walking by 14.9 % within a 400 m residential buffer. The superposition layers street trees as the dominant green-pixel source, shrubs to saturate the 1.5–2 m eye-level band, and climbing plants to reclaim vertical hard surfaces, ensuring the intervention maximises green pixel proportion across the full panoramic visual field captured by street-level imagery. Together the three signatures amplify IND_GVI beyond what any single vegetation type could achieve alone, directly supporting the positive walkability outcome documented in the linked evidence.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street-level vegetation (trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery) along pedestrian routes within 400m residential buffers to maximize visible greenness from eye-level perspectives in high-density urban contexts.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street greenness at foreground viewing distances creates visually appealing walking environments that encourage prolonged pedestrian activity. The immediate presence of vegetation in the walker's visual field improves aesthetic quality, provides psychological restoration benefits, and increases perceived safety and comfort, thereby extending total walking duration among residents.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street-level vegetation (canopy trees, shrubs, and vertical/climbing greenery) along pedestrian routes increases the proportion of greenery pixels captured in eye-level streetscape views, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI reflects greater visible green coverage in the immediate sensory field of pedestrians, which the evidence links to increased walking time.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that increases the quantity and spatial coverage of foreground vegetation along pedestrian routes within 400 m residential buffers. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Lu2018_3, this operation targets the Green View Index (IND_GVI)—the ratio of greenery pixels to total pixels in four-directional eye-level streetscape images. The intervention acts on the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK, with the outcome measure being total walking time (minutes). The relationship direction is DIR_POS (β = 0.149): as the added vegetation raises GVI values, residents' total walking time within the buffer is expected to increase. Although three vegetation subtypes are named (canopy_tree, shrub, climbing_plant), they all belong to a single top-level object (OBJ_VEG), are manipulated through the same action (ACT_ADD) and variable (VAR_SIZ), and operate within the same depth tier (TER_FG), satisfying the S1 single-signature criterion.

operation.description: Reduce foreground vegetation density in cycling corridors to maintain moderate GVI levels (avoiding excessive visual enclosure). Strategic thinning of street-level vegetation to ensure visual openness and sight lines while preserving green aesthetic benefits for cyclists.

pathway.mechanism_description: High GVI creates visual obstruction and perceived enclosure that restrains cycling activity. Excessive foreground vegetation reduces wayfinding clarity, limits sight distances for safety assessment, and creates psychological barriers. Moderate vegetation density balances greenness appreciation with spatial legibility, encouraging cycling behavior by maintaining visual permeability and perceived accessibility along cycling routes.

signatures.mechanism: Strategic thinning of foreground street-level vegetation reduces canopy density and foliage mass, thereby lowering the Green View Index (IND_GVI) from excessively high levels toward a moderate range. This decreases visual enclosure, widens sight lines, and improves perceived safety and spatial openness for cyclists, counteracting the negative association between GVI and cycling activity documented in the linked evidence.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ that modifies (reduces) the amount/density of foreground vegetation along cycling corridors. Evidence SVCs_P_Luo2022_1 establishes that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) has a statistically significant negative relationship (coefficient = −0.138, p < 0.01) with the Cycling Index on the performance dimension PRF_USE (subdimension PRS_WAL_CYCL). The paper explains that once a basic greenery threshold is met, further increases in GVI hinder cycling by creating excessive visual enclosure (Excerpt 14: 'a further increase in the green view index will hinder cycling activities'; Excerpt 17: 'provide enough broad vision for cyclists'). By selectively thinning street-level vegetation, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ reduces GVI from over-saturated levels, which is expected to positively shift cycling uptake — i.e., reversing the negative DIR_NEG relationship so that the outcome (cycling index) improves. The intervention preserves a moderate green presence to retain aesthetic and comfort benefits while eliminating the enclosure penalty.

operation.description: Reduce street tree canopy density and visual coverage in foreground view to optimize green view index in old town areas, particularly for running activity zones. Control tree size and prune excessive foliage to maintain moderate visual greenery levels.

pathway.mechanism_description: The nonlinear relationship between GVI and running behavior suggests excessive visual greenery in constrained old town streets may create perception of limited space, poor ventilation, or safety concerns. Reducing foreground tree canopy size decreases visual enclosure, improves sightlines and perceived openness, potentially enhancing running comfort. The spatially varying impact indicates area-specific GVI thresholds exist where reduction improves running conditions.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing canopy density and overall visual coverage of street trees lowers the proportion of greenery pixels captured in 360° street-view imagery, thereby decreasing the Green View Index toward the moderate range where it supports rather than inhibits running activity in old town areas.

Pruning excessive foliage and controlling tree form reshapes the canopy outline, removing indiscriminate growth that visually clutters the streetscape. This refines the spatial quality of greenery from oppressive overgrowth to a legible, well-maintained canopy silhouette, supporting comfortable visual exposure at eye level.

scope.description: SCP_S2 coordinates two simultaneous modifications to foreground street tree canopy through a reduction pattern. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) decreases canopy density and visual coverage, directly lowering the Green View Index (IND_GVI) measured at eye level. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP (secondary) reshapes tree form through pruning of indiscriminate growth noted in old town areas (Excerpt 19: 'trees grow indiscriminately without being pruned'). Together the signatures respond to evidence SVCs_P_Luo2022_3, which demonstrates a non-linear relationship (DIR_NLN) between GVI and the spatially varying Running Index under PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_ACTV. The GWR analysis (Excerpt 15) shows the GVI coefficient for running varies from −0.868 to +0.590 (mean ≈ −0.005), confirming that in specific old-town locations excessive greenery suppresses running (Excerpts 5, 18, 20). The coordinated reduction aims to shift GVI from the inhibiting high range toward the moderate optimum that promotes running activity.

operation.description: Increase the percentage and coverage of visible vegetation elements in street-level views through strategic addition of greenery in the foreground zone, targeting enhanced visual exposure to green infrastructure within pedestrian sightlines to elevate neighborhood environmental quality perception.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Green View Index (GVI) serves as a visible indicator of neighborhood investment and environmental quality, with increased foreground vegetation coverage directly correlating with reduced urban poverty markers (IMD). The visual presence of greenery signals community care, investment in public realm, and environmental amenity access, which are inversely associated with deprivation indices. Street-level greenery visibility functions as both physical infrastructure improvement and social equity indicator.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees in the foreground zone directly increases the proportion of greenery pixels captured in street-level panoramic views, raising the Green View Index by expanding upper-canopy vegetation visible within pedestrian sightlines.

Adding shrub-layer vegetation supplements canopy tree coverage by filling the lower visual field in street views, increasing total greenery pixel percentage and ensuring continuous vertical green coverage from ground level to canopy level.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 operation coordinates two superimposed vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary)—to maximise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) identified in linked evidence SVCs_P_Meng2020_1. The combined addition of canopy trees and shrubs in the foreground zone increases the total percentage of greenery pixels in street-level imagery. Evidence SVCs_P_Meng2020_1 demonstrates that increased greenery perception percentage is negatively associated (DIR_NEG) with the Index of Multiple Deprivations (IMD) under the social outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_DEMO). By vertically layering vegetation at multiple heights within pedestrian sightlines, the intervention elevates neighbourhood-scale green perception and its associated reduction in urban poverty indicators. The superposition pattern reflects the simultaneous stacking of canopy and shrub layers in the same depth zone to compound their pixel-level contribution to GVI.

operation.description: Redistribute vegetation elements across urban areas to minimize spatial aggregation and ensure equitable geographic distribution, targeting areas with high poverty indices and low green space access to reduce environmental inequality.

pathway.mechanism_description: By reducing the spatial aggregation of greenery (IND_AGG_GRN) through strategic redistribution, this intervention addresses environmental justice issues captured by the Index of Multiple Deprivations (IMD). More evenly distributed vegetation improves access to green spaces in deprived neighborhoods, mitigating the clustering effect where affluent areas monopolize green infrastructure. The positive correlation with social performance (PRF_SOC) suggests that reduced aggregation enhances equity in ecosystem service delivery, psychological well-being, and community cohesion across socioeconomic strata.

signatures.mechanism: Redistributing vegetation across the urban fabric modifies the city-scale spatial arrangement of greenery, directly reducing the Aggregation of Greenery Perception (IND_AGG_GRN) by dispersing concentrated clusters into a more uniform geographic pattern, thereby lowering the positive correlation between aggregation and urban poverty.

Targeted addition of vegetation in areas with high poverty indices and low green space access increases local street-level greenery perception, addressing the spatial inequality that drives the aggregation indicator and boosting perceived greenery in precisely those neighbourhoods where deprivation is greatest.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention pairs SIG_MOD_VEG_BG_POS (primary) with SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (secondary) in a weaving pattern that disperses vegetation across the urban area while selectively augmenting it in underserved zones. Evidence SVCs_P_Meng2020_3 establishes that the Aggregation of Greenery Perception (IND_AGG_GRN) is positively correlated (DIR_POS) with urban poverty as measured by the Index of Multiple Deprivations (IMD), within the PRF_SOC performance dimension and PRS_SOC_DEMO subdimension. The primary signature reduces aggregation by reshaping the district-scale distribution pattern; the secondary signature ensures that the net quantity of perceived greenery rises in deprived neighbourhoods. Their coordination is expected to lower IND_AGG_GRN, which in turn reduces IMD scores and environmental inequality.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and coverage of vegetation elements (including trees and grass) in the foreground visual field to enhance the green view index (GVI) and create a more nature-dominant visual environment.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher vegetation proportion in the foreground increases visual exposure to natural elements, which triggers positive psychological responses through biophilic mechanisms. The increased green visual mass reduces visual clutter from built environment, enhances perceived naturalness, and improves visual comfort by providing softer textures and cooler color tones that are inherently preferred by human visual perception systems.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees in the foreground increases the proportion of tree pixels captured in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI) and shifting the visual scene toward a nature-dominant composition.

Adding grass ground cover in the foreground supplements tree canopy coverage at a lower visual stratum, further increasing the cumulative green pixel ratio that constitutes GVI and enhancing vegetation diversity (VDi), which the study links to visual comfort.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two superimposed signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree) as primary and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (grass) as secondary—within the same foreground tier to jointly elevate the Green View Index (GVI). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Meng2024_6, GVI is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 0.602) with Visual Comfort Score under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT. By increasing both the canopy-tree and grass layers simultaneously, the intervention maximises the visible green pixel proportion in street-level views, creating the 'more nature-dominant visual environment' specified in the IOM. The superposition pattern captures the multi-stratum layering: overhead canopy and ground-plane grass occupy complementary vertical bands in the same depth zone, compounding their contribution to GVI and, through it, to aesthetic visual comfort.

source.citation: Meng, Y., Shi, J., Lyu, M., Sun, D., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Research into the Influence Mechanisms of Visual-Comfort and Landscape Indicators of Urban Green Spaces. Land, 13(10), 1688.

operation.description: Increase the diversity of vegetation types (trees, shrubs, grasses, and herbaceous plants) within the foreground visual field to create a heterogeneous vegetative composition with varied forms, textures, and spatial arrangements.

pathway.mechanism_description: Vegetation diversity enhances visual texture complexity and landscape richness, which positively influences aesthetic perception through increased visual interest and sensory stimulation. A mix of different plant forms (vertical trees, medium-height shrubs, low grasses) creates layered visual depth and textural variation that machine-learning models associate with higher visual comfort scores. This compositional heterogeneity reduces visual monotony and provides dynamic focal points that engage viewer attention, thereby improving predicted aesthetic performance.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing trees with varied canopy forms into the foreground raises the tree fraction (Tn/GVIn) in the Simpson-type Vegetation Diversity Index, adding dominant vertical structure and distinct crown silhouettes that anchor the heterogeneous composition.

Adding shrubs provides an intermediate-height layer with distinct branching forms, increasing the plant fraction (Pn/GVIn) in the diversity index and enriching vertical stratification between tree canopy and ground plane.

Introducing grasses contributes fine-textured ground-plane vegetation, raising the grass fraction (Gn/GVIn) in the diversity index and providing textural contrast against coarser shrub and tree foliage.

Adding herbaceous ground-cover plants introduces varied leaf textures and seasonal flowering patterns at close range, further balancing the proportional evenness among vegetation types that drives the diversity index upward.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 intervention coordinates four superimposed vegetation layers within a single foreground zone. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SHP (canopy_tree, primary) establishes vertical structural diversity; SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SHP (shrub) supplies intermediate-height form variation; SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (grass) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (ground_cover) deliver fine-grained textural heterogeneity at ground level. Together, the four signatures directly increase the Vegetation Diversity Index (IND_VEG_DIV) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Meng2024_5, which is calculated as a Simpson-type formula measuring proportional evenness of trees, plants, and grasses relative to the total green vegetation index. Because the index penalises dominance by any single fraction, the coordinated addition of all four subtypes maximises evenness and therefore the index value. SVCs_P_Meng2024_5 reports a strong positive relationship (effect size 0.836, DIR_POS) between IND_VEG_DIV and the Visual Comfort Score within the performance dimension PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_CMFT. The superposition pattern ensures that the varied forms (VAR_SHP) and textures (VAR_TEX) are experienced simultaneously as a layered, heterogeneous composition rather than as isolated additions.
