operation.description: Increase tree canopy coverage and density in street foreground to reduce Sky View Factor (SVF) from high values (>0.6) to moderate-low values (0.3-0.5), creating enhanced overhead shading through infill planting of large-canopy street trees

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing SVF through increased tree canopy coverage blocks direct solar radiation exposure during summer periods, decreases mean radiant temperature (MRT), and creates cooler microclimates that improve comprehensive thermal comfort scores for pedestrians in summer-hot urban climates. Lower SVF values correspond to reduced sky hemisphere visibility and greater shading protection

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new large-canopy street trees increases the total amount of overhead vegetation mass, directly intercepting the sky hemisphere visible from pedestrian level and reducing SVF from >0.6 to the 0.3–0.5 range.
Infill planting reorganises the spatial arrangement of street trees, closing gaps in the existing canopy plane to create continuous, denser overhead shade coverage rather than isolated shade patches.

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates two signatures on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) encodes the addition of large-canopy street trees, increasing the absolute quantity of canopy cover in the foreground zone. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS (secondary) encodes the infill rearrangement of these trees to densify canopy distribution and eliminate open-sky gaps. Together they target the Sky View Factor indicator (IND_SVF) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Ni2025_2, which has a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −0.436) with the Comprehensive Thermal Comfort Score under the performance dimension PRF_THR / PRS_THR_VOTE. By reducing SVF from high (>0.6) to moderate-low (0.3–0.5), the coordinated operation is expected to improve thermal comfort outcomes. The source paper confirms that tree canopy coverage within 10 m significantly reduces temperature (Excerpt 1) and that increasing tree canopy coverage in public street spaces is the recommended planning action (Excerpts 7, 19).

operation.description: Optimize building height-to-street width ratios to maintain high Sky View Factor (SVF ≥ 0.6) in quasi-residential districts. Establish design guidelines that proportionally relate building heights to adjacent road widths, ensuring sufficient sky openness while accommodating urban density requirements.

pathway.mechanism_description: By controlling the dimensional relationship between building height (vertical element) and street width (horizontal distance), this optimization directly regulates the visible sky hemisphere from ground level. Lower height-to-width ratios reduce vertical obstruction angles, thereby increasing SVF values and enhancing perceived openness in residential environments. This geometric relationship creates a predictable correlation between built form dimensions and sky exposure metrics.

signatures.mechanism: Modulating building facade heights in proportion to adjacent road widths controls the D/H (road-width-to-building-height) ratio, which is the primary geometric determinant of the Sky View Factor at street level. By constraining building height so that the D/H ratio sustains SVF ≥ 0.6, the intervention preserves sufficient sky openness even where medium-to-high-rise construction (5–7 storeys) is permitted by zoning.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that modulates building facade height (OBJ_BLT, VAR_SIZ) via proportional adjustment (ACT_MOD) at the mid-ground tier (TER_MG). As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Nishio2019_1, quasi-residential districts in Shibuya achieve a mean SVF of 63.40% because existing zoning already implicitly couples permissible building height to the width of the fronting road: along the 40 m-wide Yamanote-dori Street, buildings of up to 6–7 storeys still yield SVF ≥ 60%, whereas the same building height on a 10 m-wide road lowers SVF to approximately 55%. The intervention formalises this relationship through design guidelines that explicitly define maximum building height as a function of road width, thereby adjusting the indicator Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) upward toward the ≥ 0.6 target. On the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), maintaining high SVF is associated with perceived openness and spatial quality in quasi-residential streetscapes. Road geometry itself is treated as a given urban condition rather than an intervention target; the actionable variable is building massing (height) calibrated to that condition.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Nishio, S., & Ito, F. (2019). Application of Method for Calculating Sky View Factor Using Google Street View: Relation Between Sky View Factor and Physical Elements in Urban Space. Proceedings of the ICA, 2, 1–8.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation density and green visual index (GVI) within a 2000m buffer zone of the target area by adding trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery systems along streets and public spaces to enhance overall greenery texture at eye-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street-level vegetation density increases the leaf surface area available for PM2.5 filtration and deposition. The increased GVI indicates greater greenery visibility and coverage in the pedestrian viewshed, which correlates with higher particulate matter capture efficiency through foliar uptake, dry deposition, and turbulence reduction. The 2000m buffer represents the effective spatial scale at which vegetation influences local air quality through pollutant dispersion and microclimate modification.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery (climbing plants) along streets and public spaces increases the proportion of green pixels captured in eye-level street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). The combined canopy, mid-layer, and vertical vegetation layers fill the foreground visual field with green texture, augmenting vegetation density as perceived from the pedestrian viewpoint.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX encodes a single-element intervention in which new vegetation (trees, shrubs, and climbing plants) is introduced along streets to increase the Green View Index (GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_ORegan2022_1 demonstrates that higher GVI is associated with lower PM2.5 concentrations (effect size = −1.94, DIR_NEG), operating within the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_PHY_AIRH). By adding layered vegetation in the foreground tier, the intervention raises the green pixel ratio in street-level imagery—i.e., increases GVI—which in turn corresponds to reduced particulate air pollution. Although three vegetation subtypes (canopy_tree, shrub, climbing_plant) are involved, they all belong to OBJ_VEG acting within one depth zone, warranting a single S1 signature rather than an S2 coordination.

source.citation: O'Regan, A. C., Byrne, R., Hellebust, S., & Nyhan, M. M. (2022). Associations between Google Street View-derived urban greenspace metrics and air pollution measured using a distributed sensor network. Sustainable Cities and Society, 87, 104221.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of vegetation elements in the foreground of streetscapes, enhancing the Green View Index through strategic placement of plants, trees, shrubs, or vertical greenery within pedestrian sightlines.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI achieved through increased foreground vegetation directly enhances visual exposure to natural elements, which triggers psychological restoration responses. The prominence of green elements in the visual field reduces cognitive load, promotes attention restoration, and evokes associations with natural, tranquil environments, thereby elevating subjective perceptions of calmness and greenery (Class 2 perception scores).

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements (trees, shrubs, ground cover, climbing plants) within the immediate pedestrian sightline increases the pixel-level proportion of green in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). The regression weight of 3.34 for the Vegetation predictor in Class 2 indicates that each unit increase in vegetation pixel share produces a substantial positive shift in the Calm/Greenery subjective perception score, linking increased foreground green coverage to enhanced aesthetic naturalness.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that introduces or increases vegetation quantity and coverage in the foreground tier of streetscapes. As evidenced by SVCs_P_Ogawa2024_1, the Vegetation landscape element carries a statistically significant positive weight of 3.34 (p < 0.001) on Class 2 (Calm/Greenery) subjective perception scores. Class 2 is defined by strong negative eigenvectors on 'greenery' (−0.355) and 'calm' (−0.446), confirming it captures tranquil, nature-integrated perceptions. The indicator IND_GVI (Green View Index) is increased through the addition of vegetative cover, which drives a positive outcome (DIR_POS) on the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), specifically the naturalness subdimension PRS_AES_NATU. The paper's ℓ1-regularized sparse modelling confirms that among all landscape elements, vegetation is one of the strongest positive predictors for this perception class, while artificial objects tend toward negative associations.

operation.description: Morph building massing and urban form configuration in the background layer to increase the sky view factor through strategic manipulation of building heights, setbacks, and block spatial arrangements while maintaining functional density requirements.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased Sky View Factor (SVF) through building morphology optimization enhances visual openness and spatial permeability at street level, which negatively correlates with Walk Score as higher SVF may indicate lower density of walkable destinations and reduced facility accessibility within proximity. The negative relationship suggests that excessive sky openness may reflect dispersed urban form with fewer amenities

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting building heights and overall massing volumes reduces the vertical obstruction of the sky hemisphere as perceived from the street, directly increasing the ratio of sky pixels to total pixels (SVF). Evidence excerpts [1][2] confirm that high-density buildings block sky view, and excerpt [10] shows streets with lowest SVF have higher building density.

Modifying building setbacks and block spatial arrangements widens the angular gap between opposing facades at street level, increasing the visible sky aperture. Excerpt [11] notes that wider roads prevent sky view blockage even where many buildings exist, supporting positional reconfiguration as a complementary lever to height adjustment.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two deformation signatures—SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ (primary, height adjustment) and SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_POS (secondary, setback and layout reconfiguration)—acting simultaneously on background-tier built form to reshape urban massing. Together they increase the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) as measured by the pixel-ratio method described in evidence SVCs_P_Fang2022_1. That evidence reports a negative relationship (r = −0.292) between SVF and Walk Score on the performance dimension PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_PERC, indicating that while the intervention succeeds in opening up the sky dome, the resulting lower-density, more set-back urban form is associated with reduced composite walkability—likely because the wider spacing decreases the proximity and concentration of amenities that drive Walk Score. The deformation pattern captures how size and positional variables are jointly morphed on the same object class and tier to reconfigure the overall street cross-section geometry.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Directional change in Walk Score (Composite measure of walkability based on facility access) is context-dependent because the indicator adjustment is non-directional.

source.citation: Fang, Q., Inoue, T., ZHIQIU, X., & Qiang, L. (2022). Street Sky Openness Estimation Based on Deep Learning——Case Study of Hakata Station Area, Fukuoka. Proceedings of the 58th ISOCARP World Planning Congress.

operation.description: Increase tree canopy coverage in the background spatial tier to reduce sky view factor (SVF) and create enhanced vertical enclosure within the streetscape viewing cone

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing SVF through strategic tree canopy expansion diminishes excessive sky exposure, creating a more enclosed and intimate spatial quality that enhances subjective aesthetic preference by providing visual balance, sense of protection, and appropriate spatial definition at the street level

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy tree mass in the background tier increases the total area of vegetation coverage within the streetscape viewing cone, directly displacing visible sky area and thereby reducing Sky View Factor (SVF).

The expanded canopy silhouette reshapes the upper boundary of the streetscape cross-section, transforming the outline from an open sky-dominant profile to a more enclosed, vertically defined spatial envelope. This shape change is the geometric mechanism through which increased canopy produces perceived enclosure.

scope.description: SCP_S2 superposition of SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_MOD_VEG_BG_SHP (secondary) on the same object-tier layer. The primary signature increases the amount of canopy tree coverage at 50+ m, directly reducing the indicator Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) documented in evidence SVCs_P_Qiu2022_1. The secondary signature captures the concurrent reshaping of the canopy outline into a vertically enclosing silhouette, which is the spatial mechanism linking canopy addition to the perceptual outcome. Together the two variable changes act on the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL, with a negative relationship direction (DIR_NEG): as canopy coverage increases and SVF decreases, subjective streetscape quality ratings improve. This aligns with Qiu et al.'s finding that people favour streetscapes with less sky exposure and more trees (Excerpt 18).

source.citation: Qiu, W., Li, W., Liu, X., & Huang, X. (2022). Subjectively Measured Streetscape Qualities for Shanghai with Large-Scale Application of Computer Vision and Machine Learning. Proceedings of the 2021 DigitalFUTURES, 242–251.

operation.description: Optimize the dimensional allocation and spatial configuration of pedestrian walkways by expanding pavement width, ensuring adequate separation from vehicular traffic zones, and maximizing continuous pedestrian circulation space to accommodate comfortable walking speeds and densities.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased walkway size enhances spatial quality perception through multiple pathways: (1) provides sufficient physical space for pedestrian flow reducing congestion stress, (2) creates psychological distance buffer from vehicular hazards improving perceived safety, (3) enables comfortable walking speeds and social distancing contributing to positive walking experience, and (4) allows for visual openness in the foreground territory that street view imagery can capture as enhanced spatial quality metrics.

signatures.mechanism: Expanding the dimensional allocation (width) and continuous area of pedestrian sidewalks increases the Spatial Feasibility Index (SFI) by raising the proportion of walkable pavement relative to the overall carriageway cross-section. A higher SFI value reflects a more generous, unobstructed pedestrian realm with clearer separation from vehicular traffic, directly improving perceived walking comfort, safety, and spatial quality.

scope.description: A single-signature operation SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ encodes the widening and dimensional optimisation of pedestrian sidewalks. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Rui2023_1 identifies the Spatial Feasibility Index (IND_SFI) as a strong driver of street spatial quality ratings (Extract 9) and of residents' walking experience and sense of safety (Extract 3). The study reports that current SFI values cluster in the low range [0, 0.27] with a median of only 0.075, indicating that pedestrian space is severely under-allocated (Extract 16). Modifying the size of ground-surface elements (sidewalk width and continuity) shifts SFI upward, producing a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with the aesthetic-quality subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL of performance dimension PRF_AES, as pedestrians perceive wider, better-separated walkways as higher-quality streetscapes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source_indicator.indicator_name: Green View Index by Vertical Angle

operation.description: Select and plant tree species with high canopy structures, arching branches, and broad crown forms to maximize overhead green view index (GVI) along pedestrian pathways and recreational routes. Prioritize species with dense foliage and wide-spreading canopies (e.g., Zelkova, Platanus) that create continuous overhead greenery visible from eye-level perspective. Position trees at 6-10m intervals along walkways to ensure canopy overlap and maximize angular green coverage in upper visual fields.

pathway.mechanism_description: Overhead tree canopies increase the angular green view index by occupying upper portions of the visual field (30-60° elevation angles), creating a psychologically comforting 'green tunnel' effect that enhances aesthetic pleasure and perceived safety during walking. This overhead greenery provides visual continuity, shade provision, and enclosure that encourages longer duration leisure walking by improving thermal comfort and creating more inviting pedestrian environments. The canopy structure acts as a natural visual guide that defines walking routes and enhances wayfinding.

signatures.mechanism: Replacing generic or low-canopy tree species with high-canopy, broad-crowned species (e.g., Zelkova, Platanus) substitutes compact or low-branching crown morphologies for arching, wide-spreading canopy forms that maximise angular green coverage at high vertical angles (67.5°–90°) as perceived by pedestrians walking beneath the canopy. The 6–10 m planting interval ensures crown overlap, producing a continuous overhead green surface in the upper visual field.

scope.description: SIG_REP_VEG_FG_SHP encodes a single-signature intervention (SCP_S1) in which existing or planned street-tree species are replaced (ACT_REP) with broad-crowned, high-canopy species whose arching, wide-spreading crown forms (VAR_SHP) dominate the overhead visual field at foreground range (TER_FG). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Sakamoto2023_2 demonstrates that this manipulation increases the Green View Index by Vertical Angle (IND_GVI_ANG)—specifically at the 67.5°–90° latitude band—which is significantly and positively associated with self-reported leisure walking time per week (β = 2.385, DIR_POS). The performance dimension is Spatial use and physical activity (PRF_USE), subdimension Physical Activity (PRS_PHY_ACTV): greater overhead canopy shape coverage creates thermally comfortable and aesthetically favourable streetscapes, thereby encouraging residents to increase their leisure walking time.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

Effects of greenery at different heights in neighbourhood streetscapes on leisure walking: A cross-sectional study using machine learning of streetscape images in Sendai City, Japan. International Journal of Health Geographics, 22(1).

source_indicator.indicator_name: Green View Index by Vertical Angle

operation.description: Implement selective canopy pruning to elevate the vertical distribution of tree foliage by removing lower branches and maintaining crown density at eye level and above (1.5-4m height). Focus on street trees and pedestrian corridor vegetation to reduce foreground visual obstruction while preserving overhead canopy cover.

pathway.mechanism_description: Raising tree canopy height reduces eye-level green visual incidence (GVI at pedestrian viewing angles), which negatively correlates with leisure walking time. Lower foreground GVI may decrease perceived enclosure and visual engagement with nature along walking routes, thereby reducing motivation for recreational walking. The mechanism operates through diminished visual contact with accessible greenery that typically stimulates leisure physical activity.

signatures.mechanism: Removing lower branches (below ~1.5 m) eliminates vegetation mass at low visual angles (0°–22.5°), directly reducing the low-angle component of Green View Index by Vertical Angle (IND_GVI_ANG), the component whose negative association with leisure walking (β = −0.222) the intervention targets.

Reshaping the canopy's vertical profile by maintaining crown density at 1.5–4 m and above lifts the foliage distribution upward, preserving green visibility at higher vertical angles (45°–90°) that are positively associated with leisure walking (β up to +3.065), thus sustaining shade, psychological restoration, and streetscape appeal.

scope.description: This S2 deformation intervention coordinates SIG_REM_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary removal of lower-branch mass) with SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP (secondary reshaping of crown vertical profile) to redistribute the foliage envelope of foreground street trees. Evidence SVCs_P_Sakamoto2023_3 shows that green visibility at the lowest vertical band (0°–22.5°, measured via IND_GVI_ANG) holds a negative association (β = −0.222, DIR_NEG) with self-reported leisure walking time per week on the Spatial use and physical activity dimension (PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_WALK). By pruning lower branches, SIG_REM_VEG_FG_SIZ reduces the low-angle GVI that acts as visual obstruction discouraging walking, while SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP preserves the elevated canopy that drives the strong positive associations at higher angles—thereby jointly shifting the vertical greenery distribution toward the configuration most supportive of pedestrian leisure walking.

operation.description: Adjust the proportional size of vegetation elements in the street view to achieve balanced visual composition where greenery, sky, and building features share equal visual field (approaching 1:1:1 ratio). Modify foreground vegetation coverage to optimize the Green View Index while maintaining visual equilibrium among major streetscape components.

pathway.mechanism_description: Balanced proportions of greenery, sky, and building elements in the visual field enhance perceived street livability by creating a harmonious aesthetic experience. Equal distribution of these three primary visual components (approximately 33% each) prevents visual dominance by any single element, reducing monotony from excessive buildings or overwhelming vegetation. This equilibrium promotes positive aesthetic perception and psychological comfort, as the Green View Index at moderate levels (around 30-35%) combined with balanced sky and building views creates optimal visual diversity and spatial openness, contributing to higher street livability ratings.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the proportional coverage of foreground canopy vegetation directly modifies the pixel-level greenery ratio (Green View Index) in the immediate visual field, serving as the principal lever for reaching the target ~33% greenery share in the balanced 1:1:1 composition.

Calibrating midground canopy tree coverage modulates the depth-layered greenery proportion while simultaneously governing how much sky openness and building facade area remain visible, fine-tuning the overall streetscape toward the 1:1:1 visual equilibrium across the panoramic field.

scope.description: A coordinated S2 superposition intervention combining SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ (secondary). Both signatures target the Green View Index (IND_GVI) identified in linked evidence SVCs_P_Shen2018_2. The primary foreground signature directly increases or decreases the pixel-level greenery proportion, while the secondary midground signature fine-tunes the depth-layered balance among greenery, sky openness, and building facade exposure. Together they pursue the 1:1:1 ratio of major visual-field components that the source paper associates with street livability and balanced composition. On the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), the expected outcome direction is DIR_POS: optimizing the balanced GVI improves perceived aesthetic quality and street livability. Effect size is not numerically reported, as the paper provides a visual-analytics framework rather than a controlled experiment with measured effect magnitudes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Optimize the spatial proportion of walkway elements (sidewalks, pedestrian fences) relative to vehicular road pavement in the streetscape foreground to increase walkway visual dominance and perceived accessibility.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing the relative size/proportion of pedestrian infrastructure elements enhances visual cues of walkability by: (1) strengthening pedestrian-scale spatial definition, (2) improving legibility of pedestrian circulation zones, (3) reducing visual dominance of vehicular spaces, and (4) signaling prioritization of walking activity, collectively enhancing perceived walkability as measured through streetscape perception surveys.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing the visual proportion of sidewalk area in the streetscape foreground enlarges the pedestrian-dedicated ground surface, raising the perceived walkability by signalling spatial priority for foot traffic.

Reducing the visual proportion of vehicular road pavement in the same foreground view reciprocally shifts the pedestrian-to-vehicular ratio, diminishing vehicular visual dominance and reinforcing a pedestrian-oriented character.
Optimising the proportion of pedestrian fences (classified as built street furniture) delineates walkable territory from vehicular lanes, improving perceived safety and accessibility for pedestrians.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention applies a deformation pattern that simultaneously adjusts three foreground signatures—SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (sidewalk, primary), SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (road, secondary), and SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_SIZ (fence/furniture, secondary)—to reshape the proportional balance between pedestrian and vehicular visual elements. As linked evidence SVCs_P_Tao2022_1 demonstrates, the Walkability Score (IND_WLK_SCR) is computed from the pixel-level proportions of street-scene elements including roads (mean 0.326), sidewalks (mean 0.022), and fences (mean 0.017). The paper notes that high walkability scores are 'characterized by a pedestrian-friendly streetscape, such as decent building proportions, good greenery, and adequate space for pedestrians' (excerpt 15), and that 'installing fences to improve pedestrian safety' is linked to walkability and enclosure (excerpt 9). By increasing the sidewalk share and fence presence while reducing the road share within the foreground view, the intervention is expected to shift the mean walkability perception score upward within the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC. The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive/non-directional effect size), so the expected outcome is an increase in the composite walkability perception index through proportional rebalancing rather than a single causal coefficient.

operation.description: Optimize tree placement and canopy positioning to maximize vertical coverage within the 0-30 degree viewing angle zone from typical pedestrian eye height (1.5-1.7m). This involves strategic placement of trees at appropriate distances from viewing points and selection of species with canopy heights that occupy this optimal visual range, considering both the vertical angle geometry and horizontal distance relationships.

pathway.mechanism_description: Trees positioned to occupy the 0-30 degree vertical viewing angle create optimal visual greenery perception by aligning with the natural comfortable viewing zone of human vision. This angle range corresponds to the most frequently scanned visual field during pedestrian movement, where green canopy elements are perceived with minimal neck strain and maximum visual acuity. The strategic positioning enhances the Tree View Factor (TVF) by ensuring canopy mass appears within this preferential viewing cone, thereby maximizing aesthetic appreciation and perceived greenness without requiring upward gaze adjustments that reduce visual comfort.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the horizontal placement distance and vertical canopy position of trees relative to pedestrian viewing points ensures that canopy mass falls squarely within the 0–30° optimal vertical viewing angle from eye height (1.5–1.7 m). By controlling the distance-to-height geometry, the proportion of tree canopy pixels captured in the perceptually dominant viewing cone is maximised, thereby increasing Tree View Factor (TVF).

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS) that repositions canopy trees at calibrated distances from typical pedestrian viewpoints so their crowns occupy the 0–30° optimal vertical viewing angle. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Tong2022_1 demonstrates that Tree View Factor (IND_TVF), computed within this optimal angle band, diverges significantly from whole-image calculations and better predicts actual streetscape perception. By modifying tree arrangement to concentrate canopy mass inside this cone, the intervention drives a positive change (DIR_POS, effect size 24) in the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU—enhancing the perceived naturalness and greenery of the streetscape as experienced by pedestrians.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source_indicator.current_value: Low (e.g., 0.376 in Old City branch roads)

operation.description: Add understory vegetation layers (shrubs and herbaceous plants) to increase vertical structural complexity in the foreground vegetation stratum, creating multi-layered plant communities beneath existing tree canopies along street corridors.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased vegetation structural diversity (VSD) through understory addition creates visual clutter and textural complexity in the foreground, which reduces visual penetration depth and occludes sight lines. The multi-layered vegetation structure fragments the visual field, thereby decreasing the Green View Index (GVI) by reducing the visible proportion of green canopy in street-level eye-height photography. Higher structural heterogeneity correlates negatively with simplified green visibility metrics.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing a shrub layer beneath existing street tree canopies directly increases the number of distinct vertical vegetation strata, raising VSD by adding a previously absent structural tier in the pedestrian view field.

Adding herbaceous ground-cover plants supplies the lowest vegetation layer, further diversifying the vertical profile counted by the Shannon-based VSD metric and filling the gap between shrub layer and bare ground.

scope.description: SCP_S2 superposition of SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (ground_cover, secondary) encodes the simultaneous vertical layering of two understory vegetation subtypes within the same foreground tier. Together these signatures increase the Vegetation Structural Diversity indicator (IND_VSD) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Tong2020_2 by raising the count of distinct vegetation types (N) and balancing their proportional representation (pi) in the Shannon-index formulation. However, the linked evidence reports a DIR_NEG relationship (effect size −0.256) between VSD and the outcome measure Green View Index (GVI) within the PRF_AES performance dimension (subdimension PRS_AES_NATU). This implies that while adding shrub and herbaceous layers enriches structural diversity, the resulting multi-layered composition may redistribute or partially occlude overall perceived greenness as captured by GVI, producing a trade-off that planners must weigh when targeting aesthetic naturalness.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Tong, M., She, J., Tan, J., Li, M., Ge, R., & Gao, Y. (2020). Evaluating Street Greenery by Multiple Indicators Using Street-Level Imagery and Satellite Images: A Case Study in Nanjing, China. Forests, 11(12), 1347.

operation.description: Implement vertical greening systems (green walls, climbing plants on facades) and modular box planting (elevated planters, container gardens) along branch roads in high-density old city districts where horizontal planting space is constrained by narrow sidewalks and utility infrastructure.

pathway.mechanism_description: Addition of vertical and containerized vegetation in the foreground tier increases the visible green surface area within the street canyon's visual field, directly elevating Green View Index (GVI) measurements captured from pedestrian eye-level perspectives. This strategy compensates for limited ground-level planting space by expanding greenery into the vertical dimension of building facades and deploying portable planting systems that maximize greenery density per unit of available horizontal space.

signatures.mechanism: Adding climbing plants on building facades introduces vertical green surface at and above eye level on narrow branch roads, directly increasing the proportion of vegetation pixels captured in street-level imagery and thus raising the Green View Index (GVI).

Adding shrubs and herbs in modular box planters supplements lower-level greenery (as recommended in Excerpt [3]) that contributes additional green-pixel area to the GVI calculation, compensating for the absence of ground-level planting beds on constrained sidewalks.

Elevated planters and container garden structures provide the physical substrate enabling vegetation establishment where in-ground planting is precluded by narrow sidewalks and subsurface utility infrastructure; they do not directly alter GVI but are a necessary enabling component.

scope.description: This is an SCP_S2 coordinated intervention using a superposition pattern: two layers of newly introduced vegetation (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ as climbing_plant and as shrub) are superimposed within the same foreground zone, supported by added built infrastructure (SIG_ADD_BLT_FG_SIZ as furniture/planters). Evidence SVCs_P_Tong2020_4 documents that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) on old-city branch roads currently averages only 0.152, substantially below the new-city branch-road mean of 0.196. The intervention targets the performance dimension PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU (perceived naturalness). By simultaneously introducing facade-mounted climbing plants (vertical greening) and elevated modular box planting (lower-level shrubs and herbs), the combined green-pixel area in pedestrian-level views is expected to increase, raising GVI toward or beyond the new-city benchmark. The outcome direction is positive (increased GVI → enhanced street greenery perception), though the evidence itself is descriptive-diagnostic (DIR_NA) rather than experimentally causal.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and coverage of natural elements, primarily vegetation and water features, within the foreground visual field to enhance naturalness perception in urban surroundings

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher proportion of natural elements in the foreground reduces visual complexity and artificial stimuli, triggering biophilic responses that facilitate attention restoration through soft fascination, lower physiological stress markers (cortisol, heart rate), and enhance overall psychological well-being and environmental satisfaction

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation coverage in the foreground visual field directly increases the pixel-proportion of natural elements in the scene, raising the Naturalness Index (percentage of natural vs. human-made elements). As the dominant natural element class in urban street-level imagery, vegetation contributes the largest share of the naturalness score.

Introducing water features in the foreground supplements vegetation by adding a second natural-element class to the scene segmentation, further increasing the Naturalness Index. Water is among the 16 natural labels in the ADE20K classification used by the study and diversifies the natural composition beyond greenery alone.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two foreground additions—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_ADD_WAT_FG_SIZ (secondary)—to jointly raise the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Verma2018_1. The Naturalness Index is computed as the percentage of natural elements in the scene; by increasing the areal coverage of both vegetation and water within the immediate visual field, the proportion of natural pixels rises relative to human-made elements. According to SVCs_P_Verma2018_1, a higher Naturalness Index is positively associated (DIR_POS) with the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST, supporting restoration of attention, stress recovery, satisfaction, and well-being. The two signatures operate in the same tier and share the same variable-action pair (VAR_SIZ × ACT_ADD) but target distinct object classes, producing a superposition that amplifies the naturalness signal beyond what either element would achieve alone.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and canopy size of street trees and vertical greenery elements in core urban districts with low existing GVI values (e.g., Yau Tsim Mong, Hong Kong), focusing on pedestrian-level visual exposure at eye-level (1.5-2m height) along primary walkways and street frontages.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding foreground trees at pedestrian eye-level directly increases the Green View Index by expanding the proportion of vegetation pixels captured in horizontal viewshed analysis. Larger canopy sizes and strategic placement in high-density, low-GVI districts maximizes visual green exposure, improving perceived greening distribution across the urban fabric and enhancing environmental performance through increased biophilic visual contact.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees and enlarging canopy coverage directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured at pedestrian eye-level (1.5–2 m), raising the Green View Index across the walkway visual field.

Introducing vertical greenery on street-facing building surfaces fills vertical visual gaps that tree canopies alone cannot cover, supplementing GVI gains by adding green mass across the full breadth of the pedestrian's eye-level panorama.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary) operate in superposition within the foreground tier to jointly increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Wang2023_2. The primary signature adds street-tree quantity and canopy volume along primary walkways, while the secondary signature layers climbing-plant greenery onto adjacent street frontages. Together they raise the Greening Distribution Mean, with the linked evidence reporting a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 0.177) between GVI and the walkability-perception subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC within the performance dimension PRF_USE. The coordinated addition of horizontal canopy and vertical greenery maximises the green-pixel ratio visible at eye level, targeting districts such as Yau Tsim Mong that the source study identifies as having the lowest existing GVI values and lowest pedestrian greening satisfaction.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street trees in the foreground of lower socioeconomic neighborhoods through systematic tree planting programs along streets, sidewalks, and pedestrian zones to achieve equitable distribution of green view index across different neighborhood SES levels.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding street trees in the foreground increases the size/quantity of visible greenness captured in street view imagery (Green View Index). This directly addresses the environmental inequity documented by Wang et al. (2021), where lower SES neighborhoods exhibited significantly lower street view greenness. Foreground vegetation has the strongest visual impact on perceived greenness and provides immediate canopy coverage, enhancing both objective greenness metrics and subjective environmental quality for residents in disadvantaged communities.

signatures.mechanism: Systematic planting of new street trees increases the quantity and canopy coverage of vegetation visible at eye level, directly raising the Green View Index (proportion of green pixels in street-level imagery) in lower-SES neighborhoods where tree cover is currently deficient.

Modification of ground surfaces along streets, sidewalks, and pedestrian zones—creating tree pits, planting strips, and permeable surrounds—provides the physical infrastructure that supports new tree establishment and interlocks with the canopy additions along the street corridor.

scope.description: This is an S2 coordinated intervention with a weaving pattern, where SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary: adding canopy trees) and SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (secondary: modifying sidewalks/streets to accommodate planting) are spatially interlocked along the street network of lower-SES neighborhoods. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2021_1 demonstrates that neighbourhood SES is positively associated (β = 0.018, DIR_POS) with street view greenness quantity (IND_GVI), meaning wealthier neighborhoods currently enjoy more visible greenery. The intervention targets the performance dimension PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_SOC_EQTY, by adding trees specifically in underserved lower-SES areas, thereby closing the GVI gap and moving toward equitable distribution of street-level greenness across socioeconomic strata.

operation.description: Increase Green View Index (GVI) in outdoor activity spaces by adding low-height vegetation elements including trees and shrubs to enhance visual greenery coverage within pedestrian eye-level sightlines (1.5-1.7m height). Focus on strategic placement of vegetation to maximize visible green surfaces from typical viewing positions in frequently used outdoor spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI achieved through increased foreground vegetation provides enhanced shading, evapotranspiration cooling, and wind speed modification at the pedestrian level. The visual presence of greenery in the immediate foreground creates psychological cooling effects while the physical presence of vegetation intercepts solar radiation and releases latent heat through evapotranspiration, directly reducing ambient air temperature and mean radiant temperature (MRT) within the microclimate zone. This combined physiological and psychological effect leads to improved thermal comfort as measured by lower Thermal Comfort Vote (TCV) scores on the ASHRAE scale, indicating a negative correlation between GVI and thermal discomfort.

signatures.mechanism: Adding low-height vegetation elements (shrubs and low trees) at pedestrian eye-level sightlines enlarges the proportion of visible green surfaces in the horizontal visual field, directly increasing the Green View Index (GVI). Low vegetation intercepts low-angle solar radiation and modifies local ventilation, mediating microclimatic variables that shape thermal perception in winter outdoor activity spaces.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes the addition of low-height vegetation (shrubs and low trees) in outdoor activity spaces to increase the Green View Index (GVI), the indicator assessed in linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2022_1. By raising plant density at eye-level within the foreground zone, the intervention increases the ratio of greenery visible from pedestrian perspectives. According to the evidence, higher GVI is associated with a negative effect (DIR_NEG, coefficient = −0.286) on Thermal Comfort Vote (TCV) on the ASHRAE 5-point scale, within the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE). In the winter cold-region context of the study, this negative shift in TCV indicates that increased visible greenery modulates perceived thermal comfort through both microclimatic mediation (low shrubs and tree leaves shielding relatively low-level solar radiation, altering wind speed and mean radiant temperature) and psychophysical perception of greenery quality, as the GVI simultaneously reflects occupants' psychological assessment of urban greenery.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase Tree View Factor (TVF) by strategically planting trees with dense, broad canopies in the background layer to maximize visible green coverage from pedestrian viewpoints. Prioritize evergreen species in winter months and deciduous species with high leaf area density in summer to maintain year-round visual presence.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher TVF increases the proportion of trees visible in the field of view, which enhances perceived thermal comfort through psychological cooling effects and visual association with shade. The negative direction (DIR_NEG) indicates that increased TVF correlates with lower Thermal Comfort Vote values, suggesting improved comfort perception. Dense canopies maximize the visual green volume while providing psychological relief from urban heat stress, even when direct shading may not be present at the observation point.

signatures.mechanism: Evergreen canopy trees planted in the background layer maintain persistent foliage during winter, increasing the proportion of sky hemisphere occluded by tree canopy in vertical-upward fisheye views and thereby raising TVF year-round—critically during the leafless season when deciduous species cannot contribute.

Deciduous canopy trees with high leaf-area density complement evergreens by maximising TVF during summer when their dense, broad canopies fully expand, blocking intense solar radiation and further occluding the sky hemisphere in the upward view.

scope.description: SCP_S2 superposition of two coordinated signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ (evergreen canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ (deciduous canopy_tree, secondary)—acts on the same spatial variable (amount of canopy coverage), action (addition), tier (background), and object (vegetation) but with complementary species subtypes layered together. Their combined effect increases the Tree View Factor (IND_TVF) across all seasons. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2022_2, higher TVF is associated with a negative shift in Thermal Comfort Vote (TCV) (β = −0.331, DIR_NEG) within performance dimension PRF_THR / subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE. This indicates that greater overhead canopy coverage moderates perceived thermal conditions—primarily by mediating solar radiation reaching pedestrian level (excerpts [3], [10], [13]). The superposition ensures that the TVF-driven thermal regulation pathway is not interrupted seasonally: evergreens sustain coverage during winter when deciduous species are bare (excerpt [14]), while deciduous trees with high LAI maximise coverage in summer (excerpt [3]).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street trees within 1-4km radius of central urban sites to enhance vertical street greening (measured as Green View Index - GVI). Focus on establishing continuous tree canopy along street corridors at the pedestrian eye-level viewshed to maximize visible greenery from street perspectives.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street tree coverage increases the Green View Index (GVI), which represents the proportion of vegetation visible from human eye level. Higher GVI correlates with reduced Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) concentrations through multiple pathways: (1) increased leaf surface area for NO2 absorption and dry deposition, (2) altered street canyon microclimate that affects pollutant dispersion patterns, (3) vegetative barriers that intercept vehicular emissions before reaching pedestrian zones, and (4) potential reduction in surface temperatures that indirectly affects photochemical reaction rates of NOx species.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees increases the quantity and vertical coverage of canopy vegetation along street corridors, raising the Green View Index (GVI) as captured from pedestrian eye-level street view imagery. The greater vertical distribution of greenery enhances particulate interception and gaseous pollutant absorption, reducing ambient NO2 concentrations along the streetscape.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes the establishment of additional canopy trees along urban street corridors to increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Wu2020_1, higher GVI is significantly associated with lower Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) concentrations (DIR_NEG), supporting the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_PHY_AIRH). The single-signature scope reflects a unitarily targeted intervention: augmenting the amount of visible street-level canopy vegetation so that the vertical distribution of greenery—measured as GVI from street view images—increases, thereby strengthening the pollution-filtering capacity documented across 1–4 km radii in central Beijing, Shanghai, and Nanjing (Excerpts 9, 10, 11). The expected outcome is a reduction in summertime street-level NO2, consistent with the negative relationship reported in the evidence record.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Optimize building volume index (BVI) in street canyons by controlling vertical building density to maintain appropriate building-to-street width ratios that facilitate natural ventilation and pollutant dispersion. Avoid excessive vertical mass that creates confined air spaces at pedestrian level.

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower BVI values reduce vertical obstruction in street canyons, allowing improved air circulation and dilution of traffic-generated NO2 emissions. Excessive building vertical density creates canyon effects that trap pollutants at ground level. The study found non-significant correlation between BVI and NO2 concentration, suggesting BVI optimization should be considered alongside other morphological factors (sky view factor, street orientation) rather than as a standalone intervention for air quality improvement.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing or controlling the vertical building volume (Building Volume Index) in street canyons adjusts the building-height-to-street-width aspect ratio, opening the canyon cross-section. This lessens the confinement of air at pedestrian level, allowing greater natural ventilation and turbulent mixing that facilitate pollutant (NO2) dispersion rather than trapping emissions within the canyon.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ that modifies the vertical size/volume of built facades defining street canyons. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Wu2020_3, the vertical distribution of street buildings (quantified via Building Volume Index from street-view imagery) was tested against NO2 concentration (indicator: IND_WAT used as proxy Water View Index in the dataset, but the operative measure is NO2). The relationship direction was DIR_NS (p = 0.077, marginally non-significant), indicating a weak positive association between increased vertical building mass and higher NO2. Within the performance dimension PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_AIRH (Spatial use and physical activity – Air pollution health exposure), the expected outcome direction of the intervention is a reduction in NO2 concentration achieved by lowering the building volume index, although the evidence base supports this only at a marginal, non-significant level.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation coverage in densely populated areas and urban outskirts by adding trees and tall shrubs along sidewalks, building setbacks, and roadside buffers to enhance green view index at eye-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding foreground vegetation increases the proportion of greenery visible within the pedestrian view cone (0-180° horizontal field), directly elevating GVI values. The negative direction with Quality (PC4) suggests that excessive foreground greenery may obstruct visual access to higher-quality background landscapes or create monotonous green walls. Strategic placement should balance GVI enhancement with maintaining visual permeability to mid- and background elements that contribute to overall aesthetic quality.

signatures.mechanism: Adding trees and tall shrubs along sidewalks, building setbacks, and roadside buffers increases the proportion of green pixels in street-view imagery at eye level, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). The intervention augments the amount of vegetation within the immediate pedestrian visual field, expanding visible green coverage captured by semantic segmentation of street-view photographs.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes the introduction of new canopy trees and tall shrubs within the foreground tier along pedestrian-adjacent streetscape zones (sidewalks, building setbacks, roadside buffers) to increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Wu2024_2 reports a negative association (effect size β = −0.658, DIR_NEG) between GVI and the aesthetic quality subdimension (PRS_AES_QUAL) under performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference). This means that while the intervention successfully raises the quantity of visible street-level greenery, the evidence indicates that higher GVI values are inversely loaded on Principal Component 4 (Quality). This suggests that simply increasing vegetation amount does not linearly improve perceived streetscape quality—potentially because indiscriminate vegetation addition without compositional or spatial-design considerations can reduce visual coherence, depth legibility, or façade visibility. Urban planners in priority zones (the ≈139.62 km of high-accessibility, low-greenery roads identified in Nanjing) should therefore pair quantity increases with design strategies that address qualitative perception.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation coverage to achieve Green View Index (GVI) exceeding 30% threshold through strategic addition of trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery along pedestrian viewsheds in foreground zones.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced foreground vegetation increases visual contact with nature in immediate viewing range, triggering biophilic responses that elevate subjective satisfaction and generate positive psychological feelings through stress reduction, attention restoration, and improved environmental quality perception. The 30% GVI threshold represents a critical tipping point where residents perceive sufficient greenness to activate psychological benefits.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along pedestrian viewsheds directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, constituting the largest single contributor to pushing the Green View Index above the 30% threshold.

Adding shrubs fills the mid-height vegetation stratum beneath canopy trees, supplementing green pixel coverage in the lower portions of the pedestrian viewshed and reinforcing GVI gains through vertical layering.

Adding vertical greenery (climbing plants on walls and structures) converts hard surfaces into green pixels within the street-view frame, capturing additional GVI share on surfaces that trees and shrubs cannot occupy.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes three vegetation subtypes—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary)—within the same foreground tier to maximize green pixel coverage across the full vertical extent of the pedestrian viewshed. Together they drive the indicator IND_GVI past the 30% threshold identified in the linked evidence SVCs_P_Liu2023_5. That evidence establishes a positive relationship (DIR_POS) between GVI and the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_MOOD, with an effect-size benchmark of 30 (the GVI percentage). Exceeding this threshold is associated with improved subjective satisfaction and positive psychological feelings. The superposition pattern ensures that canopy, understory, and facade-mounted vegetation layers complement each other without spatial conflict, collectively saturating the street-level view with greenery.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the coverage area and density of vegetation (trees and grass) in public open spaces through strategic planting to enhance visual greenness in street-level views

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced vegetation coverage increases the visibility and prominence of natural elements in the visual field captured by street view imagery, which positively influences perceived environmental quality scores through biophilic response, improved aesthetic appeal, and strengthened sense of naturalness in urban spaces

signatures.mechanism: Strategic planting of trees increases the canopy-layer pixel share in street-level views, directly raising the green-coverage component of the Total Vegetation Index (ss_tree = 1.797)
Strategic planting of grass increases the ground-layer green pixel share in street-level views, contributing an additional vegetation-index uplift (ss_grass = 1.596)

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (grass) form a vertical superposition: canopy trees are layered above a ground-cover grass plane, together maximising the pixel-ratio green coverage captured in street-level panoramic imagery. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chen2023_1 reports the indicator Total Vegetation Index (IND_VEG), composed of SVI-derived green coverage and NDVI, within the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU (Aesthetic Preference – Naturalness). Both vegetation layers show a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with the Perceived Environment Score, with canopy trees contributing a slightly larger standardised effect (1.797) than grass (1.596). Coordinating the two subtypes addresses both the upper and lower portions of the visual field, amplifying overall visual greenness beyond what either layer alone would achieve.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage proportion of street trees in the foreground layer to enhance visual green density along streetscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher green view index (GVI) from increased tree presence creates visually richer natural elements that positively influence perceived beauty through biophilic responses. The adversarial scoring system (human-machine) validated that greater vegetation proportion correlates with enhanced aesthetic perception, likely mediated by increased visual complexity, natural color diversity, and softening of hard urban surfaces.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional street trees into the foreground streetscape increases the absolute quantity of green vegetation pixels captured in street-view images, directly raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) by expanding the number of discrete canopy sources within the 0–10 m sensory zone.

Adjusting the canopy spread and coverage proportion of existing and newly planted street trees increases the areal dominance of green pixels per view direction, amplifying the cumulative greenness ratio (Gi) that feeds IND_GVI beyond what tree count alone achieves.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two simultaneous actions on the same object class and depth zone. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) introduces new canopy trees along the streetscape, while SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (secondary) enlarges the canopy coverage proportion of those trees. Together they raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Dai2021_1, which exhibits a strong positive relationship (β = 0.780, DIR_POS) with the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_PREF (perceived beauty score derived from human-machine adversarial scoring). By superimposing quantity addition and coverage modification at the foreground tier, the intervention maximises visual green density and thereby enhances residents' aesthetic perception of the streetscape.

source.citation: Dai, L., Zheng, C., Dong, Z., Yao, Y., Wang, R., Zhang, X., Ren, S., Zhang, J., Song, X., & Guan, Q. (2021). Analyzing the correlation between visual space and residents' psychology in Wuhan, China using street-view images and deep-learning technique. City and Environment Interactions, 11, 100069.

operation.description: Increase the amount and density of visible street vegetation (trees, shrubs, grass, and herbaceous plants) in the foreground and middleground of the pedestrian streetscape to maximize green view index (GVI) within the immediate visual field.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher green view index in the street environment provides continuous visual contact with nature during daily activities, which reduces psychological stress through attention restoration and emotional regulation. The visible presence of vegetation in the foreground creates a restorative microenvironment that buffers urban stressors and promotes positive affect, thereby reducing depressive symptoms in elderly populations as measured by GDS-15 scores.

signatures.mechanism: Canopy trees added in the foreground (0–10 m) contribute the largest contiguous green-pixel area to the street-level visual field, directly raising GVI by filling upper and mid portions of the image frame with foliage.
Canopy trees added in the middleground (10–50 m) extend continuous green backdrop depth, increasing GVI by occupying the mid-frame visual band and closing gaps between foreground crowns.
Foreground shrubs fill the vertical stratum between ground plane and canopy, adding green pixels in the lower-to-mid image band and reducing exposed built or paved surfaces in the near view.
Middleground shrubs densify the understory layer at social-field distances, increasing green-pixel proportion in the lower image band where facades, walls, and pavement would otherwise dominate.
Foreground grass replaces bare or hard ground surfaces at eye level, converting ground-plane pixels from grey/brown to green, contributing to GVI at the image base.
Middleground grass extends the green ground plane deeper into the visual field, reinforcing the overall vegetated character detected by the segmentation network.
Foreground herbaceous ground cover adds fine-textured green pixels at the lowest image stratum and along edges, increasing marginal GVI where other vegetation layers leave gaps.

Middleground herbaceous planting completes the multi-layer vegetation superposition, ensuring that the deep-learning segmentation classifies the maximum possible pixel share as green space at social-field distances.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes four vertical vegetation strata—canopy trees (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ with subtype canopy_tree), shrubs (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ with subtype shrub), grass (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ with subtype grass), and herbaceous ground cover (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ with subtype ground_cover)—across both the foreground and middleground tiers of the pedestrian streetscape. The superposition pattern ensures that green pixels occupy the maximum possible share of every vertical band in the street-view image, thereby maximising the Green View Index (IND_GVI). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Helbich2019_1, higher GVI exhibits a negative association (DIR_NEG) with geriatric depression scores (GDS-15) within the performance dimension PRF_RST / PRS_MNT_DEPR. The coordinated addition of all eight signatures simultaneously is essential because the GVI metric—computed via fully convolutional neural network segmentation of Tencent Street View imagery—aggregates all green-classified pixels regardless of plant type or distance, so omitting any stratum or tier would leave non-green pixel gaps that suppress the index.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Helbich, M., Yao, Y., Liu, Y., Zhang, J., Liu, P., & Wang, R. (2019). Using deep learning to examine street view green and blue spaces and their associations with geriatric depression in Beijing, China. Environment International, 126, 107–117.

operation.description: Increase the visibility and presence of blue spaces (water bodies, fountains, water features) in the foreground of street views by adding or enhancing water elements that are directly visible to pedestrians at eye level.

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater blue space visibility in the street view reduces geriatric depression scores (GDS-15) through restorative environmental effects. Water features provide visual fascination, reduce mental fatigue, and trigger positive emotional responses. The foreground presence ensures direct visual engagement during daily activities, promoting stress reduction and mood enhancement particularly beneficial for elderly populations vulnerable to depression.

signatures.mechanism: Adding or enhancing water elements (fountains, water features, small water bodies) in the immediate pedestrian foreground increases the pixel proportion of blue space captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising the Water View Index (IND_WAT). The increased street-level visibility of water directly augments the blue-space exposure that pedestrians perceive at eye level.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_WAT_FG_SIZ) that introduces new water elements into the foreground tier of the streetscape to increase the Water View Index (IND_WAT). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Helbich2019_2, higher street-view blue-space proportions are inversely associated (DIR_NEG) with geriatric depressive symptoms measured by the GDS-15 score, within the performance dimension PRF_RST (Stress relief and psychological restoration), subdimension PRS_MNT_DEPR. Multilevel regressions in the source study confirmed that adding street-view blue-space variables improved model fit and showed a negative association with depression among elderly residents in Beijing. Therefore, increasing OBJ_WAT presence via ACT_ADD in TER_FG is expected to elevate IND_WAT and, in turn, reduce depressive symptom scores.

source.citation: Helbich, M., Yao, Y., Liu, Y., Zhang, J., Liu, P., & Wang, R. (2019). Using deep learning to examine street view green and blue spaces and their associations with geriatric depression in Beijing, China. Environment International, 126, 107–117.

operation.description: Increase eye-level green space visibility (GVI) through strategic addition of street trees, vertical greening systems on building facades, and foreground vegetation layers along pedestrian corridors to enhance visual greenness exposure in residents' daily viewing range (typically 1.5-2m height perspective).

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced eye-level greenery increases visual green space exposure during daily activities, which promotes outdoor physical activity, walking, and recreational behaviors. The psychological restoration effect of green views reduces stress-related eating behaviors while increased walkability and outdoor engagement contributes to higher energy expenditure, collectively leading to lower BMI and reduced obesity risk.

signatures.mechanism: Street trees planted along pedestrian corridors contribute the largest share of green pixels within the eye-level viewing cone, directly raising the Green View Index (VGI) by occupying upper-foreground portions of the street-view image.

Vertical greening systems applied to near-side building facades convert inert built-surface pixels to green pixels at the 1.5–2 m eye-level band, filling the mid-height stratum of the view that canopy trees alone cannot cover and further increasing VGI.

Low foreground vegetation layers (shrubs, hedge rows) along the pedestrian corridor edge add green pixels in the lower portion of the street-view frame, completing the vertical stack and maximising total VGI coverage across the image.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three simultaneous vegetation additions within the foreground tier to maximise the Green View Index (GVI). SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree) serves as the primary signature, introducing street trees whose crowns dominate the upper visual field. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant) layers vertical greening onto adjacent building facades, converting hard-surface pixels to green at eye height. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub) fills the lower visual stratum with foreground planting. Together the three layers stack vertically within the same depth zone to raise the indicator IND_GVI as measured by the ratio of green pixels to total pixels in four-directional street-view panoramas (see Excerpt [24]). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Xiao2020_1 demonstrates that higher VGI is negatively associated with overweight/obesity status (BMI class), with an effect size of −1.019 on the performance dimension PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_PHY_OBES (DIR_NEG). The intervention therefore targets a reduction in obesity prevalence by increasing residents' daily visual greenness exposure along walking routes within 1 000 m of residential communities.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Xiao, Y., Zhang, Y., Sun, Y., Tao, P., & Kuang, X. (2020). Does Green Space Really Matter for Residents' Obesity? A New Perspective From Baidu Street View. Frontiers in Public Health, 8.

operation.description: Increase street vegetation coverage in lower-GVI districts to reduce spatial inequity of urban greenery and enhance neighbourhood amenity value

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding street trees and vegetation raises the aggregated GVI at the county/jiedao level. Higher GVI is positively correlated with housing price (Spearman r=0.433, p<0.01) and street network density (r=0.489, p<0.01), indicating that visible street greenery contributes to neighbourhood economic vitality and walkability infrastructure quality. The spatial clustering pattern (Moran I=0.314, p<0.001) shows that GVI improvements in low-greenery clusters can shift district-level greenery equity.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along sidewalks and street frontages increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, contributing significantly to the upper and mid-field portions of the pedestrian viewshed and raising the aggregate Green View Index toward the 30% threshold.

Adding shrubs at eye-level along sidewalks and street edges fills the mid-to-lower band of the pedestrian viewshed with greenery, supplementing canopy tree coverage and ensuring continuous visual green contact at the human-perceived horizon line, thereby reinforcing GVI gains.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two foreground vegetation layers—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary)—to collectively raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Xiao2021_2 reports a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 0.489) between IND_GVI and the performance dimension PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes, subdimension PRS_SOC_DEMO), specifically street network density at county/jiedao level. By superimposing canopy trees (which dominate the upper viewshed) with eye-level shrubs (which fill the mid-to-lower viewshed), the intervention maximises green pixel coverage across the full panoramic image frame used to compute GVI. Both signatures target the same depth zone but occupy complementary vertical strata, producing a layered greenery profile that effectively increases GVI and is associated with positive socio-demographic outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Street network density at county/jiedao level

source.citation: Xiao, C., Shi, Q., & Gu, C.-J. (2021). Assessing the Spatial Distribution Pattern of Street Greenery and Its Relationship with Socioeconomic Status and the Built Environment in Shanghai, China. Land, 10(8), 871.

operation.description: Increase the visible vegetation coverage in the foreground view by adding trees, shrubs, and other greenery elements along street edges, building facades, and pedestrian zones to systematically enhance the Green View Index (GVI) from street-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI achieved through foreground vegetation addition enhances the Cooling Power Index (CP) by: (1) increasing direct shading of ground and built surfaces, reducing solar radiation absorption; (2) promoting evapotranspiration which consumes latent heat and cools surrounding air masses; (3) creating multiple vegetation layers that intercept and filter thermal radiation; and (4) establishing visual-thermal synergy where perceived greenness correlates with measurable temperature reduction in the microclimate.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along street edges and pedestrian zones introduces the largest single contributor to green pixel ratio in streetscape imagery, directly raising the Green View Index. Canopy coverage dominates GVI extraction as confirmed by evidence that 'the vegetative parts of canopies can be totally explained as greenery visible to the public.'

Adding shrubs fills the mid-height vegetation layer between tree canopies and ground plane, contributing additional green pixels to the street-level field of view and supplementing GVI where canopy gaps exist.

Adding ground-level greenery (lawns, grasslands) completes the vertical vegetation profile visible in streetscape photos. Evidence excerpt [16] confirms GVI data were extracted from 'canopies, lawns, and grasslands,' so this layer contributes to the composite green pixel count.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates three superimposed vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (grass)—within the same foreground tier to systematically increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI). As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Xin2024_1, higher GVI is associated with improved thermal comfort performance (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_COOL) measured via the Cooling Power Index (CP), with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS) and an effect size of 0.3179. The superposition of canopy trees (primary driver of visible green pixels), shrubs (mid-layer infill), and grass (ground-plane greenery) collectively maximises the ratio of green pixels to total pixels in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising GVI and its associated cooling benefit.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Cooling Power Index (CP)

operation.description: Increase the quantity and spatial coverage of accessible vegetation in the foreground to maximize touchable green interfaces and enhance visual green volume from pedestrian viewpoints.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Green View Index (GVI) through expanded foreground vegetation creates multi-sensory engagement opportunities (visual richness, tactile accessibility, proximity to nature), which triggers positive psychological responses including stress reduction, attention restoration, and enhanced emotional well-being as measured by Positive Response Index.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation quantity and spatial coverage in the immediate foreground increases the ratio of green pixels visible from pedestrian viewpoints, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). The foreground placement ensures the added canopy mass occupies a large proportion of the streetscape image frame, amplifying GVI gains. Simultaneously, proximity within 0–10 m enables tactile contact ('touchable nature'), strengthening the sensory dose of green exposure that the evidence links to positive emotional perception and thermal comfort.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that increases the amount of accessible vegetation in the foreground tier. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Xin2024_4, the Green View Index (GVI) is positively associated (r = 0.31, DIR_POS) with the Positive Response Index (PRI), operating within the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO, subdimension PRS_MNT_MOOD). By adding canopy trees at touchable range, the intervention raises GVI—the proportion of green pixels in pedestrian-level streetscape imagery—thereby increasing the probability of positive emotional responses. The evidence base, drawn from ~15,000 streetscapes in Changchun urban parks, supports that greater vegetative dose in the visible and touchable field promotes thermal comfort perceptions that in turn evoke more positive facial-expression scores.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.
