operation.description: Increase street tree canopy coverage and planting density along high-grade roads (arterials and highways) to compensate for lower baseline GVI values. Prioritize continuous tree rows with large-canopy species in medians and roadside buffers where road width permits greater greenery installation.

pathway.mechanism_description: The study revealed a negative correlation between road grade and GVI (r=-0.336, p<0.01), indicating that wider, faster roads systematically have lower visible greenery. Higher-grade roads typically feature expanded impervious surfaces and prioritize vehicular movement over pedestrian amenity, creating greenery deficits. By strategically adding vegetation—particularly trees with substantial canopy spread—in available planting zones (medians, setbacks), the intervention directly increases the proportion of greenery visible in street-view imagery, thereby elevating GVI values. This targets the spatial inequity where infrastructure hierarchy inversely affects aesthetic green exposure.

signatures.mechanism: Adding continuous rows of large-canopy street trees in medians and roadside buffers increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery. On high-grade (wider) roads, the road surface currently dominates the visual field, depressing GVI; introducing substantial canopy mass in the midground zone directly offsets this deficit by filling the visual band that pavement otherwise occupies.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a single-layer intervention targeting canopy tree addition along arterials and highways. Evidence SVCs_P_Xue2022_2 reports a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −0.096) between road grade and the Green View Index (IND_GVI) under the aesthetic preference dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU): higher-grade roads exhibit systematically lower street greenery because wider carriageways reduce the green-pixel share in panoramic views (excerpt [4]). The intervention counteracts this by adding large-canopy trees—increasing their size/amount (VAR_SIZ)—in medians and roadside buffers, thereby raising GVI on the road classes where it is most deficient. The expected outcome is an upward shift in GVI for high-grade roads, narrowing the inter-grade disparity and improving perceived naturalness (PRS_AES_NATU).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Road Grade (Higher grade = wider/faster roads)

source.citation: Xue, C., Jin, C., Zhou, L., & Li, G. (2022). Exploring the distribution of city street greenery from eye-level: An application of Baidu Map panoramic images data. Geografisk Tidsskrift-Danish Journal of Geography, 122(1), 73–86.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of visible street vegetation in the foreground view to enhance Green View Index (GVI) along street corridors through strategic tree planting, vertical greening, and shrub installation at eye-level viewing distances.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI values, achieved through increased visible greenery in the foreground viewing zone, enhance aesthetic visual quality as perceived by human observers. The mechanism operates through: (1) increased green color proportion in the visual field improving pleasantness, (2) biophilic responses to natural elements reducing stress, (3) visual complexity from vegetation texture enriching scene interest, and (4) softening of hard urban surfaces. Expert evaluations via Elo rating methodology confirm that street scenes with greater visible vegetation consistently receive higher aesthetic quality scores.

signatures.mechanism: Strategic tree planting introduces new canopy cover into the street corridor, directly increasing the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level in street view imagery and thus raising GVI.

Vertical greening on walls and facades fills mid-height portions of the visual field with vegetation pixels that would otherwise register as built surface, supplementing tree canopy contributions to GVI.

Shrub installation at ground and eye-level fills the lower band of the street view frame with green pixels, covering bare ground or hard surfaces and adding a further vegetative layer to cumulative GVI.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes three vegetation-addition signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary)—within the same foreground tier to maximise cumulative green pixel coverage across the full vertical extent of the eye-level street view frame. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Ye2019_4 demonstrates that the resulting increase in Green View Index (IND_GVI) positively drives visual quality (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL, DIR_POS) with a relative importance weight of 0.81—the highest among all measured streetscape elements. The superposition pattern ensures that canopy, wall-plane, and ground-plane greening operate simultaneously at complementary heights within the visual field, collectively converting non-green pixels (built surface, pavement, bare ground) into vegetation pixels captured by semantic segmentation (SegNet), thereby robustly elevating GVI and perceived aesthetic quality along street corridors.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Ye, Y., Zeng, W., Shen, Q., Zhang, X., & Lu, Y. (2019). The visual quality of streets: A human-centred continuous measurement based on machine learning algorithms and street view images. Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science, 46(8), 1439–1457.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street-level vegetation (trees, shrubs, and grass) along pedestrian corridors to enhance Green View Index (GVI) perceived by pedestrians at eye-level.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced street greenery increases visual contact with nature during walking, which creates more aesthetically pleasant and psychologically restorative environments. Higher GVI values reduce perceived stress, increase route attractiveness, and provide perceived shade comfort, thereby encouraging residents to choose walking over motorized transport and engage in outdoor physical activities more frequently. The street-level vegetation acts as a positive environmental cue that promotes active transportation behaviors.

signatures.mechanism: Adding trees, shrubs, and grass along pedestrian corridors increases the absolute quantity and areal coverage of vegetation visible in the pedestrian's eye-level field of view. This raises the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, directly elevating the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI, as documented in the linked evidence, is positively associated with walking behaviour and physical activity levels, because a greener streetscape encourages pedestrians to walk more frequently and for longer durations.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ is a single-signature intervention that increases the amount of street-level vegetation (canopy trees, shrubs, and grass) within the foreground tier of pedestrian corridors. According to evidence SVCs_P_Yin2023_1, this addition raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI) — the area proportion of greenery in the eye-level visual field — which in turn positively (DIR_POS) influences the performance dimension PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_ACTV, specifically walking behaviour and physical activity levels. The operation is classified as SCP_S1 because it involves a single top-level object type (OBJ_VEG), a single spatial variable (VAR_SIZ — quantity and coverage), a single action (ACT_ADD), and a single depth zone (TER_FG).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Walking behavior and physical activity levels

source.citation: Yin, C., Peng, N., Li, Y., Shi, Y., Yang, S., & Jia, P. (2023). A review on street view observations in support of the sustainable development goals. International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation, 117, 103205.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of eye-level vegetation (shrubs, hedgerows, vertical greenery) along pedestrian pathways to enhance green view index within the immediate visual field of walking individuals.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher green view index in the foreground visual field provides direct psychological restoration through attention restoration theory mechanisms. Eye-level greenery creates immediate visual contact with nature during pedestrian movement, reducing momentary stress levels by offering restorative stimuli that counteract environmental stressors and mental fatigue in real-time contexts.

signatures.mechanism: Adding eye-level vegetation (shrubs, hedgerows, vertical greenery) along pedestrian pathways increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI within the immediate visual field of walking individuals provides greater visual contact with greenery, which—through stress-restorative pathways validated by the linked evidence—reduces momentary psychological stress.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ. The intervention adds eye-level vegetation volume and coverage (VAR_SIZ × ACT_ADD) in the foreground tier (TER_FG) to increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as measured by linked evidence SVCs_P_Yu2024_1. That evidence demonstrates a negative association (DIR_NEG, β = −0.20) between dynamic eye-level GVI exposure along pedestrian GPS trajectories and momentary stress (PRS_MNT_STRS) within the Stress relief and psychological restoration performance dimension (PRF_RST). By densifying shrubs, hedgerows, and climbing plants at eye level along walkways, the intervention directly raises the ratio of greenery pixels in the streetscape view, the exact metric captured by PSPNet segmentation of street-view images in the source study. The expected outcome is a reduction in momentary stress levels among pedestrians exposed to the enhanced greenery.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Momentary stress level measured via EMA (1-6 scale)

source.citation: Yu, C., & Kwan, M.-P. (2024). Dynamic greenspace exposure, individual mental health status and momentary stress level: A study using multiple greenspace measurements. Health & Place, 86, 103213.

operation.description: Infill street corridors and pedestrian pathways with vegetation layers to increase green view index (GVI) at eye-level in lower-SES, high-density districts such as Sham Shui Po. Prioritize placement along primary walking routes to maximize visual contact frequency during daily mobility patterns.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased foreground vegetation exposure enhances green view index (GVI) encountered during routine activities, providing consistent visual access to nature that reduces cortisol levels and momentary stress through attention restoration and psychological distancing from urban stressors. The proximity effect of foreground greenery creates immediate perceptual relief that accumulates across multiple daily exposures.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation along street corridors and pedestrian pathways increases the total area of green pixels captured in eye-level panoramic imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI along daily walking routes increases cumulative visual contact with greenery, which is associated with reduced momentary stress through attentional restoration and stress-buffering pathways.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ: the addition of vegetation volume along primary walking routes in high-density, lower-SES districts to increase the quantity of visible greenery at eye level. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Yu2024_4 demonstrates that higher dynamic GVI exposure (IND_GVI), measured via GPS-tracked mobility paths and Google Street View deep-learning segmentation, is negatively associated (β = −0.21) with momentary stress (EMA) on the performance dimension PRF_RST (Stress relief and psychological restoration), subdimension PRS_MNT_STRS. The intervention directly elevates GVI by infilling currently under-greened streetscapes, thereby shifting cumulative greenspace exposure upward for pedestrians during routine daily mobility and producing the expected stress-reduction outcome.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Momentary stress (EMA)

source.citation: Yu, C., & Kwan, M.-P. (2024). Dynamic greenspace exposure, individual mental health status and momentary stress level: A study using multiple greenspace measurements. Health & Place, 86, 103213.

operation.description: Increase the proportion of wild flora including native bushes, dense groves, and naturalistic vegetation assemblages in the foreground, while reducing the coverage of manicured lawns and artificial ornamental plantings to create a more ecologically authentic landscape texture.

pathway.mechanism_description: Wild vegetation with irregular growth patterns, diverse textures, and naturalistic compositions enhances the perception of ecological authenticity and wildness. The tactile and visual complexity of unmanaged flora—including varied leaf structures, spontaneous growth patterns, and seasonal variations—creates immersive sensory experiences that strengthen visitors' sense of intimacy with nature by providing direct encounters with ecological processes rather than horticultural artifice.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing native bushes, dense groves, and naturalistic vegetation assemblages adds wild flora to the foreground, directly increasing the [flora] numerator of the Wildness metric (wildness = arctan([flora] / ([grass] + [non-natural elements]))), shifting landscape texture from manicured to ecologically authentic.

Reducing the coverage of manicured lawns and artificial ornamental plantings decreases the [grass] term in the Wildness denominator, thereby amplifying the wildness ratio and removing the artificially-managed character that suppresses perceived wildness.

scope.description: This S2 montage coordinates two complementary foreground vegetation operations. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (primary) introduces naturalistic wild flora—native shrubs and dense groves—that registers as 'flora, plant, plant life' in the ADE20K semantic palette, directly boosting the numerator of the Wildness index. SIG_REM_VEG_FG_SIZ (secondary) reduces manicured lawn coverage, which the source paper explicitly excludes from wild elements because grass 'usually requires artificial maintenance and management' (Excerpt 19), thereby shrinking the denominator. Acting together, both signatures raise the Wildness indicator (IND_WLD) reported in evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2023_2, which exhibits a positive relationship (r = 0.524) with the aesthetic-naturalness subdimension (PRS_AES_NATU) of the performance dimension PRF_AES. The expected outcome direction is DIR_POS: higher wildness yields stronger subjective agreement that 'the place provides a sense of intimacy with nature.'
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Subjective rating of 'The place provides a sense of intimacy with nature'

source.citation: Zhang, X., Lin, E. S., Tan, P. Y., Qi, J., & Waykool, R. (2023). Assessment of visual landscape quality of urban green spaces using image-based metrics derived from perceived sensory dimensions. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 102, 107200.

operation.description: Install service facilities including benches, lighting fixtures, and informational signboards in the foreground zone to enhance spatial legibility and provide functional amenities that signal active maintenance and care.

pathway.mechanism_description: Service facilities function as tangible 'cues to care' that communicate ongoing stewardship and social order. The presence of well-maintained benches, adequate lighting, and clear signage enhances perceived safety, reduces ambiguity about space usage, and signals that the environment is monitored and valued. These visual cues reduce perceived risk and increase psychological comfort, thereby facilitating attention restoration by allowing users to feel secure enough to engage in restorative experiences. The facilities also support extended stay duration (fascination) and provide physical comfort necessary for stress recovery.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing benches, lighting fixtures, and informational signboards increases the amount of service-oriented built furniture in the immediate sensory zone. Their presence enhances spatial legibility by providing wayfinding cues and signals active maintenance, which elevates perceived restorative potential by making the environment feel cared-for, safe, and functionally supportive of pause and orientation.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_ADD_BLT_FG_SIZ that encodes the addition of service facilities (benches, lighting, signboards) as built furniture in the foreground tier. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_3 associates the Service Facility indicator (IND_SEF) with the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO, subdimension PRS_MNT_REST) through a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 3.880). By increasing the quantity of legible, functional amenities at close range, the intervention raises the Service Facility index, which in turn positively shifts Perceived Restorative Potential—users perceive the space as better maintained, more navigable, and more psychologically supportive.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Restorative Potential

source.citation: Zhang, S., Lu, J., Guo, R., & Yang, Y. (2024). Exploring the Relationship Between Visual Perception of the Urban Riverfront Core Landscape Area and the Vitality of Riverfront Road: A Case Study of Guangzhou. Land, 13(12), 2142.

operation.description: Optimize green space exposure by increasing vegetation coverage within the middleground perception zone (street-level visible greenery), utilizing spatially-explicit grid-based measurement (rather than single-point buffer analysis) to capture the nonlinear dose-response relationship between Green View Index and psychological stress reduction.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced middleground green visibility creates perceptual contact with nature that reduces psychological stress through attention restoration and stress recovery pathways. The nonlinear relationship suggests threshold effects where moderate-to-high GVI levels (optimal dosage) provide maximum stress reduction benefits, while accounting for spatial heterogeneity in exposure patterns across residential neighborhoods prevents underestimation of health impacts.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing the areal and volumetric coverage of street-level vegetation within the middleground zone raises the proportion of green pixels captured in four-directional street-view imagery, thereby elevating the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI, per the nonlinear dose-response curve identified in the evidence, reduces perceived psychological stress.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ that encodes the modulation of vegetation amount within the 10–50 m middleground perception zone. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_10 demonstrates that increasing the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is associated with a negative (DIR_NEG) effect on psychological stress perception (composite score), with an effect size of 0.35, under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL (Aesthetic Preference – Perceived Quality). The intervention targets street-level visible greenery—primarily canopy trees—whose increased coverage directly raises GVI values computed via semantic segmentation of panoramic street-view images. The spatially-explicit grid-based measurement approach ensures that the resulting GVI gain is captured across the full streetscape rather than at isolated sample points, reflecting the nonlinear threshold at which additional greenery yields diminishing but still significant stress-reduction benefits.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Psychological Stress Perception (Composite Score)

source.citation: Zhang, S., Lu, J., Guo, R., & Yang, Y. (2024). Exploring the Relationship Between Visual Perception of the Urban Riverfront Core Landscape Area and the Vitality of Riverfront Road: A Case Study of Guangzhou. Land, 13(12), 2142.

operation.description: Optimize vegetation color composition in middle-ground views through diverse planting design with varied seasonal palettes and chromatic arrangements to enhance visual landscape aesthetic quality and scenic appeal.

pathway.mechanism_description: Diverse vegetation color patterns in middleground views create psychologically cooling effects through visual perception mechanisms: cooler color tones (blues, greens) and varied chromatic compositions reduce perceived thermal stress, enhance visual comfort, and trigger cognitive associations with shade and coolness, thereby improving thermal sensation ratings from 'much hotter' toward 'much cooler' despite unchanged physical temperature

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the chromatic composition of middle-ground vegetation—introducing varied seasonal palettes and diverse color arrangements—enriches the perceived color warmth/coolness and visual richness of the greenway landscape. This enhanced color diversity raises the Visual Beauty Score (IND_BEA_VIS), which in turn acts as a psychological mediator: positive aesthetic appraisal and richer color variation increase spatial richness perception, trigger positive emotions, and ultimately improve subjective thermal sensation toward cooler/more comfortable ratings (effect size β = 0.40).

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_COL) that modifies the colour variable of middle-ground vegetation through diversified planting palettes and chromatic arrangements. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_2 demonstrates that this colour optimisation positively increases the Visual Beauty Score (IND_BEA_VIS) with an effect size of 0.40. The performance dimension is thermal comfort (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE), where the expected outcome direction is DIR_POS: higher visual beauty driven by richer vegetation colour leads to cooler and more comfortable thermal sensation ratings. The pathway operates through colour perception mediating scenic view quality, spatial richness, and positive emotional states, which collectively shift subjective thermal evaluation toward greater comfort without altering the objective microclimate.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Thermal Sensation (Visual evaluation: 1=Much hotter, 5=Much cooler)

source.citation: Zheng, Y., Lan, S., Zhao, J., Liu, Y., He, S., & Liu, C. (2024). Modeling the Effect of Greenways' Multilevel Visual Characteristics on Thermal Perception in Summer Based on Bayesian Network and Computer Vision. Land, 13(11), 1796.

operation.description: Minimize building footprint and reduce structural density in immediate vicinity of sports and recreational facilities to maximize Sky View Factor (SVF), ensuring unobstructed vertical visibility and enhanced spatial openness in exercise zones.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased SVF through building density reduction creates perceptually expansive outdoor environments with greater sky exposure. This enhanced visual openness reduces spatial confinement, improves psychological comfort, strengthens connection to nature, and promotes thermal comfort through better air circulation and natural ventilation. The resulting pleasant environmental conditions encourage longer exercise participation duration by reducing perceived exercise barriers and enhancing overall exercise experience quality.

signatures.mechanism: Removing or minimizing building footprints and reducing structural density in the immediate surroundings of exercise zones eliminates vertical obstructions that occlude the sky hemisphere. With fewer and smaller built masses blocking the overhead view, the Sky View Factor (SVF) increases, reflecting greater unobstructed vertical visibility and enhanced spatial openness perceived by greenway users.

scope.description: SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ encodes the removal/minimization of built-structure mass (OBJ_BLT) at the foreground tier adjacent to recreational facilities. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_5, increasing Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) — achieved here by clearing built obstructions rather than manipulating the sky itself — has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 3.247) with exercise participation duration on the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity, subdimension PRS_WAL_USE). The paper confirms that a higher sky view index 'laterally reflects the higher space openness of the environment' (Excerpt 9) and that space openness (SO), defined as the proportion of 'empty' area including unobstructed sky within the visual field (Excerpt 6), positively influences perceived brightness and comfort. Reducing foreground building presence is the direct spatial manipulation that raises SVF and, consequently, encourages longer exercise engagement.

source.citation: Zheng, Y., Lan, S., Zhao, J., Liu, Y., He, S., & Liu, C. (2024). Modeling the Effect of Greenways' Multilevel Visual Characteristics on Thermal Perception in Summer Based on Bayesian Network and Computer Vision. Land, 13(11), 1796.

operation.description: Increase the proportion of visible vegetation in the urban foreground view through strategic addition of vegetation elements, enhancing Green View Index (GVI) within the pedestrian sight line and street-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI directly reduces air temperature through multiple mechanisms: vegetation provides evapotranspiration cooling, increases surface albedo compared to built materials, creates shading effects that reduce solar heat absorption, and enhances air circulation. The visible green mass in foreground acts as a thermal buffer, intercepting shortwave radiation and releasing latent heat through transpiration, thereby lowering ambient air temperature in the immediate microclimate zone.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements at the pedestrian street level increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater vegetative cover within the immediate visual field provides evapotranspiration and shading, which lowers local air temperature.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that increases the amount of visible vegetation in the pedestrian foreground view, thereby raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhou2021_3 demonstrates that higher GVI is associated with lower air temperature (Ta) under the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_TEMP), with a negative relationship (DIR_NEG) and an effect size of −0.0119 °C per unit increase in GVI. The intervention thus strategically adds street-level vegetation to amplify 3D green visibility, producing a modest but measurable cooling benefit for pedestrians.

source.citation: Zhou, H., Tao, G., Yan, X., & Sun, J. (2021). Influences of greening and structures on urban thermal environments: A case study in Xuzhou City, China. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 66, 127386.

operation.description: Optimize street tree canopy coverage to achieve a minimum Green View Index (GVI) threshold of 30% for standard urban streets and 40% for high-quality green streets. This involves calibrating tree density, canopy size, and spacing along street corridors to maximize visible greenery from pedestrian eye-level perspective (1.6m height). Implementation should consider strategic placement in foreground viewing zones to enhance green visibility within the critical 0-50m viewing distance.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing tree canopy size and coverage in the foreground tier directly enhances the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic views, thereby elevating GVI scores. The 30-40% GVI thresholds correlate with measurable aesthetic improvements as larger tree canopies occupy greater visual field areas at pedestrian eye level. The foreground positioning (0-50m) ensures maximum visual impact, as greenery closer to viewers contributes disproportionately to perceived landscape quality and aesthetic satisfaction compared to background vegetation.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing tree canopy density and individual canopy size in the 0–10 m foreground zone maximises the angular subtense of green pixels in the pedestrian visual field at 1.6 m eye height, directly raising GVI toward the ≥ 30 % threshold

Optimising inter-tree spacing and lateral arrangement in the foreground eliminates canopy gaps, ensuring continuous green coverage across sequential street-view viewpoints and stabilising per-viewpoint GVI readings

Extending canopy-size optimisation into the 10–50 m midground fills the deeper portions of the visual field with green volume; perspectival foreshortening compresses distant canopies, so larger crowns are needed to maintain appreciable GVI contribution at depth

Calibrating midground tree spacing completes a layered canopy corridor; staggered placement ensures overlapping crown silhouettes that produce a visually continuous green backdrop when viewed from the pedestrian standpoint

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes four signatures—SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary), SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ, and SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS—to optimise street-tree canopy coverage across the full 0–50 m pedestrian viewing corridor. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2023_1, the target indicator is the Green View Index (IND_GVI), measured as the ratio of visible vegetation pixels to total pixels in the human visual field. The study proposes a GVI threshold of 0.25–0.30 as a preliminary standard for urban streetscapes, finding that existing two-dimensional greenery metrics (GLR, GCR) consistently overestimate the greenery volume actually perceived by pedestrians. The intervention therefore directly manipulates the three-dimensional visual supply of greenery by jointly adjusting canopy size/density (VAR_SIZ) and spatial arrangement (VAR_POS) of canopy trees (OBJ_VEG : canopy_tree) in both foreground (TER_FG) and midground (TER_MG) tiers. Trees dominate GVI contribution at 82.55 % of all visible greenery, confirming canopy_tree as the operative subtype. On the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), the expected outcome is that the coordinated calibration raises GVI above the proposed 30 % standard, improving perceived streetscape greenery quality. Because the evidence reports a descriptive threshold rather than a directional effect size (DIR_NA), the relationship is normative: meeting the threshold is treated as the minimum acceptable performance level.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Zhu, H., Nan, X., Yang, F., & Bao, Z. (2023). Utilizing the green view index to improve the urban street greenery index system: A statistical study using road patterns and vegetation structures as entry points. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104780.

operation.description: Add central greenery belts (median strips with vegetation) to road profiles, positioning green infrastructure in the middle ground layer of street cross-sections to maximize visual exposure from pedestrian and vehicular viewpoints.

pathway.mechanism_description: Central greenery belts directly occupy the visual field's middle ground, creating continuous green surfaces that are perpendicular to viewing angles along roads. This positioning strategy maximizes Green View Index (GVI) by ensuring vegetation appears prominently in street-level panoramic captures, particularly effective in wide road patterns where central medians provide substantial visual green coverage without obstruction from foreground elements.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing a central greenery belt (median vegetation strip) into the road cross-section increases the total volume of visible green biomass from both pedestrian and vehicular viewpoints. Because the median occupies the centre of the visual field across a range of viewing angles, it raises GVI more effectively than equivalent planting relegated to roadside belts alone. The shrub layer is specifically highlighted as essential to this GVI uplift, filling the mid-canopy visual band that trees and herbs leave open.

scope.description: A single-signature operation (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that introduces a previously absent central greenery belt—primarily composed of shrubs—into the median strip of road profiles. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2023_2 shows that roads possessing a central greenery belt achieve significantly higher Green View Index (IND_GVI) values than roads without one; among road patterns, loop-shaped roads with such belts reach GVI = 0.271 compared to 0.227 for linear roads lacking them. The intervention therefore targets the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL) by increasing the three-dimensional visual greenery volume experienced by street users, with the expectation that GVI values rise toward or beyond the preliminary 30 % threshold proposed by the study.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on GVI levels in different road patterns is not established.

source.citation: Zhu, H., Nan, X., Yang, F., & Bao, Z. (2023). Utilizing the green view index to improve the urban street greenery index system: A statistical study using road patterns and vegetation structures as entry points. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104780.

operation.description: In early-stage road construction planning, prioritize immediate shrub planting along roadside foreground zones rather than waiting for long-term tree canopy maturation. This strategic sequencing considers the temporal development patterns of vegetation layers to accelerate green view coverage.

pathway.mechanism_description: Shrubs achieve visual coverage and canopy closure within 2-3 years, providing immediate GVI improvement in the pedestrian viewshed foreground (0-5m height range). This approach addresses the 10-20 year maturation gap of tree canopies, ensuring continuous green visibility during early road life cycles. The lower-height vegetation layer (shrubs) fills the critical visual zone at eye-level and immediately above, which contributes disproportionately to street-level GVI measurements compared to sparse young tree crowns.

signatures.mechanism: Immediate planting of shrubs along roadside foreground zones introduces rapid green coverage at eye level, providing an early-stage boost to GVI where newly transplanted trees—whose crowns are drastically pruned for transportation—cannot yet supply adequate visual greenery.

As roadside trees achieve stable growth over time their canopy volume expands, progressively increasing the proportion of vegetation pixels in the human view and overtaking shrubs as the dominant GVI contributor at maturity.

scope.description: This S3 strategic programme sequences two temporal phases of OBJ_VEG intervention within the same foreground depth zone. In Phase 1, SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub) delivers immediate green volume at pedestrian eye height, compensating for the low initial canopy contribution of newly transplanted trees. In Phase 2, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree) captures the gradual maturation of tree crowns whose expanding size increasingly dominates the streetscape view. Evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2023_4 reports the outcome measure as potential GVI improvement differentiated by road pattern (ring roads +15.2 %, grid +12.8 %, organic +9.3 %) under the indicator Green View Index (IND_GVI) and the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL. The phased strategy ensures that the GVI improvement trajectory is front-loaded by shrubs (Phase 1) while securing the higher long-term ceiling afforded by mature canopy trees (Phase 2), aligning with the evidence that Tree-Shrub (T-S) vegetation structures perform comparably to full T-S-H structures and significantly better than single-tree structures.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Zhu, H., Nan, X., Yang, F., & Bao, Z. (2023). Utilizing the green view index to improve the urban street greenery index system: A statistical study using road patterns and vegetation structures as entry points. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104780.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of visible vegetation elements (trees, shrubs, and grass) along running routes to enhance Green View Index (GVI) in the foreground visual field.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI creates visually enriched and biophilic running environments that attract runners through enhanced aesthetic appeal, perceived naturalness, and psychological restoration benefits. The presence of visible greenery in the foreground visual field increases route preference and usage frequency, as evidenced by elevated running trajectory density in areas with greater vegetation visibility.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along running routes directly increases the tree-pixel proportion (Tj) in the GVI formula (Excerpt [20]), which is the dominant contributor to visible greenness in street-view imagery. Canopy trees occupy the largest share of the vertical visual field, raising GVI most efficiently.

Adding shrubs increases the plant-pixel proportion (Plj) in the GVI formula. Shrubs fill the mid-height band of the visual field, complementing canopy trees and closing gaps in the green pixel count captured by street-view segmentation.

Adding grass increases the grass-pixel proportion (Grassj) in the GVI formula. Grass covers the ground plane of the visual field, completing the three-component greenness metric and ensuring continuous green coverage at the lowest visual band.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (grass, secondary)—to collectively raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as documented in evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2024_1. The GVI is computed as the mean of tree, plant, and grass pixel proportions across four street-view headings (Excerpt [20]), so all three vegetation layers must be superimposed within the same foreground depth zone to maximise the indicator. The linked evidence (SVCs_P_Zhu2024_1) reports that higher GVI values correspond to higher running trajectory density and preference along routes (performance dimension PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_USE). Although the relationship direction is classified DIR_NA (descriptive), the observed pattern consistently shows runners gravitating toward greener streetscapes (Excerpts [3], [4], [23]). The intervention therefore targets an increase in IND_GVI with the expected outcome of greater route use intensity.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

magnitude.value: descriptive: Higher running trajectory density observed in areas with higher GVI (proxy from linked Evidence)

source.citation: Zhu, J., Li, B., Ouyang, H., Wang, Y., & Bai, Z. (2024). Understanding Urban Residents' Walking Exercise Preferences: An Empirical Study Using Street View Images and Trajectory Data. Buildings, 14(2), 549.

operation.description: Selectively prune tree canopy branches to reduce overhead coverage and maintain Sky View Factor above 0.2 in wooded areas, ensuring adequate sky visibility while preserving tree health and structural integrity.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing Sky View Factor through canopy excision enhances perceived spatial openness and reduces visual enclosure, creating a more inviting and less confined walking environment. Higher SVF (>0.2) improves ambient light penetration, enhances wayfinding clarity, and reduces psychological stress associated with heavily enclosed spaces, thereby positively influencing walking preference and pedestrian pathway selection behavior.

signatures.mechanism: Selective pruning reduces the spatial extent and density of overhead canopy branches, thereby decreasing foliage coverage directly above the walker. This opens gaps in the canopy that increase the proportion of visible sky hemisphere, raising the Sky View Factor (SVF) above the 0.2 threshold. The tree itself is retained (not removed), so the action modifies the size/amount attribute of the existing canopy rather than eliminating the vegetation object entirely.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that modifies the canopy size of foreground trees through selective pruning. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zou2024_1 demonstrates that increasing the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF)—the objective enclosure metric derived from semantically segmented sky elements—has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.102) with walking preference (PRS_WAL_WALK) within the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE). By reducing overhead canopy coverage to maintain SVF > 0.2, the intervention shifts the wooded environment from a fully enclosed condition toward a semi-open condition that enhances perceived vastness and visual transparency, factors the study identifies as promoting pedestrian use of urban forest park trails.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Prioritize vegetation addition interventions in identified 'Cold Spots' (low GVI areas) within central city regions and suburban cores to address spatial heterogeneity of greenery distribution. Increase vegetation coverage in residential communities showing deficit green view index values based on hot/cold spot analysis.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding vegetation in cold spot areas directly increases the Green View Index (GVI) by expanding the visible greenery from street-level perspective. This strategic spatial intervention reduces heterogeneity by elevating greenery levels in underserved areas, thereby improving overall aesthetic performance and visual green exposure equity across residential communities. The background-scale vegetation enhancement creates cohesive green visual environments.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation into residential communities identified as GVI 'cold spots' directly increases the amount of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index values in those deficit areas and reducing the spatial heterogeneity (clustering of low values) detected by Getis-Ord GI* analysis.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that adds vegetation volume in residential communities classified as cold spots of low Green View Index. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Song2023_2 reports significant spatial heterogeneity in GVI across Beijing's residential communities, with low-value clustering concentrated in the central city core (DC, XC) and suburban district centres (HR, MY, PG, MTG). By targeting these deficit zones with vegetation additions, the intervention is expected to raise localised GVI values, narrowing the gap between cold-spot and hot-spot clusters and thereby improving the performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_EQTY (social equity in greenery distribution). Because the evidence reports spatial heterogeneity rather than a directional dose–response, the relationship direction is DIR_NA and effect size is not explicitly reported; the expected outcome direction of the intervention is nevertheless positive—reducing inequitable distribution of visible greenery across communities.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Song, J., Zhu, X., & Yu, X. (2023). Monitoring and mapping vegetation greenery in residential communities using street view images and a Normalized Vegetation Greenery Index: A case study in Beijing, China. Frontiers in Forests and Global Change, 6.

operation.description: In high GVI streets (>0.6), optimize vegetation design by diversifying plant forms (e.g., columnar, spreading, weeping habits), varying species composition to create layered canopy structures, and introducing seasonal color variations through flowering species and foliage changes, rather than simply increasing vegetation quantity or coverage.

pathway.mechanism_description: Excessive vegetation monotony in high GVI environments can cause visual fatigue and reduced aesthetic satisfaction. Optimizing morphological diversity, structural complexity, and chromatic variation enhances visual interest and breaks monotonous green dominance. Diversified vegetation forms create rhythmic visual sequences, multi-layered textures provide depth perception, and color accents establish focal points, collectively improving visual comfort without compromising existing greenery coverage. The negative direction (DIR_NEG) indicates that merely maximizing GVI quantity degrades aesthetic performance, necessitating qualitative optimization strategies.

signatures.mechanism: Diversifying plant growth habits (columnar, spreading, weeping) within already high-GVI streets breaks up the homogeneous silhouette of the green mass, introducing formal variety that enriches the visual field and raises perceived visual comfort without increasing the GVI value itself.

Varying species composition to create vertically layered canopy structures (canopy trees over understory shrubs and ground cover) converts a flat, dense green plane into a textured, permeable vegetation profile along the street corridor, counteracting the visual monotony that depresses VICO at high GVI levels.

Introducing flowering species and foliage with seasonal chromatic variation injects colour contrast into the dominant green matrix, disrupting perceptual saturation and providing temporal visual interest that improves visual comfort scores even when green coverage remains constant.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three simultaneous attribute modifications on the same object class (OBJ_VEG) to qualitatively optimize streets that already exceed a GVI of 0.6. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP diversifies individual plant forms at close range; SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX restructures the vegetation profile into a layered pattern visible along the street corridor; and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_COL introduces chromatic variety through flowering and foliage seasonality. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Sun2023_3 demonstrates that high-GVI streets (mean VICO = 0.390) perform substantially worse on Visual Comfort (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT) than low-GVI streets (mean VICO = 0.717), indicating that beyond a threshold, additional green quantity yields diminishing or negative returns. The combined signatures therefore aim to raise the GVI-to-VICO conversion efficiency: the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is held approximately constant while the intervention redirects its composition toward greater formal, textural, and chromatic complexity, thereby improving the aesthetic-comfort outcome that the evidence shows is currently suppressed at high GVI values.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Visual Comfort (VICO) is not established.

source.citation: Sun, D., Ji, X., Gao, W., Zhou, F., Yu, Y., Meng, Y., Yang, M., Lin, J., & Lyu, M. (2023). The Relation between Green Visual Index and Visual Comfort in Qingdao Coastal Streets. Buildings, 13(2), 457.

operation.description: Strategically position vegetation elements to maximize visual exposure from pedestrian viewpoints, prioritizing eye-level green visibility over horizontal coverage. Concentrate green elements in foreground zones (0-20m viewing distance) along primary sightlines and circulation routes to enhance perceived greenness independent of total area metrics.

pathway.mechanism_description: The non-significant association between Green View Index (GVI) and green space coverage indicates that traditional area-based metrics poorly predict visual greening outcomes. GVI captures human-centered perception by quantifying the proportion of visible greenery in the visual field, which is predominantly influenced by foreground vegetation placement rather than distant or overhead canopy coverage. Strategic positioning of vegetation in immediate viewing zones creates higher perceived green exposure without proportional increases in land coverage, suggesting that spatial configuration and visual accessibility are more critical than quantity for aesthetic performance outcomes.

signatures.mechanism: Repositioning vegetation elements closer to pedestrian viewpoints and along primary sightlines concentrates eye-level green mass in the immediate sensory field, increasing the proportion of green pixels captured in the visual cone (Green View Index). The evidence (SVCs_P_Chen2020_5) shows GVI is not significantly correlated with horizontal green space coverage (r = 0.066, p = 0.760), confirming that the spatial arrangement of vegetation relative to the observer—not total planted area—is the operative variable for perceived greenness.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS encodes the strategic repositioning of vegetation within the foreground tier to maximise eye-level green visibility from pedestrian viewpoints. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chen2020_5 reports a non-significant correlation (r = 0.066, DIR_NS) between the Green View Index (IND_GVI) and horizontal green space coverage at the district level, situated within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). This null relationship demonstrates that simply increasing the planimetric area of urban green does not reliably raise perceived visual greenness. The intervention leverages this insight: by modifying the positional arrangement of vegetation so that planting is concentrated along primary sightlines and circulation corridors in the foreground zone, a greater share of the observer's visual field is occupied by green elements, thereby elevating GVI independently of total coverage metrics. The expected outcome direction remains cautious given the non-significant evidence base; the design logic is sound but empirical confirmation of a positive shift in GVI through repositioning alone requires further validation.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Chen, J., Zhou, C., & Li, F. (2020). Quantifying the green view indicator for assessing urban greening quality: An analysis based on Internet-crawling street view data. Ecological Indicators, 113, 106192.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street-level vegetation in areas identified with low GVI values (affecting 75% of the city area) through strategic planting of trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery along streets, sidewalks, and building facades to enhance visible greenery from pedestrian perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Addition of foreground vegetation directly increases the amount of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby improving GVI scores. Street trees and vegetation at eye-level create immediate visual impact in the foreground plane, which has the strongest influence on GVI calculation as it occupies larger proportions of street view imagery compared to middle or background elements. The increased vegetation coverage enhances environmental performance by improving visual quality, thermal comfort, and psychological well-being for urban residents.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees directly increases canopy volume and leaf area visible at eye-level, which is the dominant contributor to GVI pixel classification. Excerpt [1] confirms GVI correlates strongly with canopy coverage close to sample sites, and excerpt [6] notes street trees reduce pedestrian oppressiveness, indicating their foreground visual dominance.

Adding shrubs along sidewalks fills the lower portion of the eye-level visual field, supplementing canopy-tree coverage in the GVI segmentation. Excerpt [13] explicitly recommends 'planting grass or shrubs instead of tall trees' to alleviate stress, and excerpt [3] notes streetscape grasses/low vegetation have strong mental health associations for older adults.

Adding vertical greenery on building facades converts hard vertical surfaces (classified as 'building' in semantic segmentation) into vegetation pixels in street-view imagery, directly raising GVI. Excerpt [4] notes that buildings and trees together form street enclosure; replacing bare facade area with green cover shifts pixel classification toward vegetation without altering street geometry.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three foreground vegetation additions—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary)—to jointly raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) linked to evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2022_2. The evidence reports that 75.59% of Zhanjiang's street sample points fall within a low GVI range, indicating a city-wide deficit in eye-level visible greenery. The three signatures act in superposition: canopy trees dominate the upper visual field, shrubs fill the lower visual field, and climbing plants convert vertical built surfaces into green pixels, together maximizing the proportion of vegetation pixels captured by semantic segmentation of street-view images. This multi-layer stacking within the same depth zone amplifies GVI more effectively than any single vegetation type alone. The performance dimension is PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_SOC_EQTY; the expected outcome direction is an upward shift in GVI distribution across the city, reducing the share of low-GVI areas and improving equity in residents' exposure to visible street greenery, which the paper associates with mental health benefits (excerpts [15], [17]).

operation.description: Replace solid perimeter walls with transparent or semi-transparent railing fences, open metal grilles, or permeable facade systems to allow visual penetration and reveal interior green vegetation to street-level viewers.

pathway.mechanism_description: Replacing opaque boundary walls with transparent enclosures increases visual connectivity between private/semi-private greenspaces and public streetscapes. This transformation changes the texture (solidity to permeability) of foreground barriers, enabling previously hidden vegetation to contribute to street-level Green View Index. The enhanced visual penetration expands the effective green viewing field by incorporating background greenery that was previously occluded by solid walls, thereby increasing aggregate green visibility metrics without necessarily adding new vegetation.

signatures.mechanism: Replacing solid, opaque perimeter walls with transparent or semi-transparent alternatives (railing fences, open metal grilles, permeable facade systems) changes the surface permeability pattern of the enclosure element. This shifts the Facade Enclosure Type from visually impervious to visually penetrable, removing the physical barrier that previously blocked sightlines to interior green vegetation, thereby increasing the proportion of visible greenery captured at eye level.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_REP_BLT_FG_TEX that replaces the texture/permeability pattern of foreground perimeter walls from solid to transparent or semi-transparent systems. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2022_2 demonstrates that the indicator Facade Enclosure Type (IND_FAC_ENC) mediates the amount of street-side greenery perceived by pedestrians: streets bounded by solid enclosed exterior walls consistently yield low GVI, whereas railing-wall designs and open boundaries allow visual access to affiliated green spaces inside blocks (AGSB). By substituting opaque walls with permeable enclosures, the intervention positively shifts IND_FAC_ENC toward openness, which in turn increases GVI—the outcome measure operating within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_NATU, with an expected positive relationship direction (DIR_POS). The existing interior vegetation is not itself modified; only the enclosure barrier is replaced, enabling latent greenery to register in the pedestrian visual field.

operation.description: Select and prioritize evergreen tree species (e.g., conifers, broadleaf evergreens) in the middleground layer to maintain consistent green coloration and visual continuity throughout seasonal transitions, particularly during winter dormancy periods when deciduous vegetation loses foliage

pathway.mechanism_description: Evergreen species retain foliage year-round, preserving the greenery-dominated visual character that defines stable visual walkability types across seasons. By maintaining consistent canopy color (VAR_COL) in the middle-ground view zone, the visual environment remains recognizable and predictable, preventing seasonal shifts from greenery-dominated to built-dominated or sky-dominated visual walkability categories. This stabilization supports consistent wayfinding and aesthetic continuity in urban walking environments.

signatures.mechanism: Substituting deciduous tree species with evergreen species (conifers, broadleaf evergreens) ensures that the dominant foliage colour remains a consistent green throughout autumn–winter, preventing the seasonal shift from green canopy to bare brown/grey branching that causes the greenery indicator pixel ratio to collapse and triggers a Type A → Type B visual walkability transition.

Evergreen species retain full leaf mass year-round, so the total amount of visible vegetation pixels (Tn + Gn in the greenery formula) remains stable across seasons rather than dropping sharply in winter; this prevents the compensatory rise in sky-pixel openness that characterises the Type A-to-openness-dominated transition documented in the evidence.

scope.description: This is an SCP_S2 superposition operation combining SIG_REP_VEG_MG_COL (primary) and SIG_REP_VEG_MG_SIZ (secondary) acting on the same object in the same depth zone. Both signatures address the performance outcome documented in evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_8, which uses the Type of Visual Walkability indicator (IND_TVW) under the walkability-and-use dimension (PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_PERC). The evidence shows that greenery-dominated streets (Type A) seasonally transition to openness-dominated streets (Type B) from autumn to winter because deciduous canopy loses foliage, reducing the greenery pixel ratio while increasing the sky-view openness ratio. SIG_REP_VEG_MG_COL directly stabilises the green chromaticity of the middleground canopy layer so that the visual scene retains its greenery-dominated character. SIG_REP_VEG_MG_SIZ simultaneously stabilises the volumetric leaf-area presence, preventing the drop in the greenery index (Tn + Gn / Sum) that triggers reclassification to a different TVW type. Together, the two signatures are expected to maintain stable Type A visual walkability classification across all four seasons (the DIR_NA relationship becomes effectively positive stability), thereby improving perceived walkability continuity year-round.

operation.description: Increase pavement area allocation within street cross-sections by widening sidewalk zones and reducing vehicular lane width, thereby increasing the pavement-to-road pixel ratio in street view imagery to create pedestrian-prioritized thoroughfares

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding pavement size through infill operations increases the visual presence of walkable surfaces in the foreground view, directly enhancing the Visible Pedestrian Index (VPI) by providing greater spatial availability for pedestrian movement. Larger pavement allocation improves perceived walkability and encourages active mobility behaviors by creating more comfortable, accessible pedestrian environments

signatures.mechanism: Widening sidewalk zones increases the pavement pixel count (Pn) in the Visual Pavement Index formula (Di = Pn / (Pn + Rn)), directly raising the numerator and the overall VPI ratio, thereby registering greater pedestrian-allocated space in street-view imagery.

Reducing vehicular lane width decreases the road pixel count (Rn) in the VPI denominator (Pn + Rn), amplifying the pavement-to-road ratio shift initiated by the sidewalk widening and reinforcing the visual dominance of pedestrian surfaces.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention employs a deformation pattern that reshapes the street cross-section through two simultaneous ground-plane modifications: SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (sidewalk, increase) and SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (road, decrease). Together they act on the indicator measured by evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_6—the Visual Pavement Index (IND_VPI), defined as Di = Pn/(Pn + Rn). The primary signature increases Pn while the secondary signature decreases Rn, jointly elevating VPI. This targets the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC, aiming to increase the space available for pedestrians. Although the linked evidence reports only descriptive statistics (DIR_NA, Table 3) across urban contexts—noting that pavement-dominated streets (Type C) concentrate in high-density residential areas and urban centres—the intervention logic is that a higher VPI corresponds to pedestrian-prioritized thoroughfares with greater walking comfort and spatial allocation.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and canopy coverage of street trees in the foreground viewing zone to enhance Green View Index (GVI) at eye-level perspective, strategically placing trees along sidewalks and street edges to maximize visible greenery while managing sky view factor.

pathway.mechanism_description: Addition of foreground trees directly increases the proportion of vegetation pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby elevating the Green View Index score. The increased canopy size creates a more balanced visual composition by reducing excessive sky visibility, which improves perceived aesthetic quality through enhanced spatial enclosure and biophilic connection. Deep learning-based visual quality assessment identifies higher greenery proportions as positively correlated with human preference for streetscape aesthetics.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional street trees increases the total quantity and aggregate canopy area visible in the eye-level field of view, directly raising the pixel ratio of green vegetation in street-view imagery and thus elevating the Green View Index (IND_GVI).

Strategically arranging new trees along sidewalks and street edges optimises their placement within the foreground viewing cone, maximising the proportion of visible greenery per viewpoint while moderating sky view factor through canopy interception—thereby further boosting IND_GVI without requiring additional tree numbers.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two simultaneous variable manipulations on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) adds canopy trees to the foreground zone, increasing the sheer amount of green cover captured by street-view cameras. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS (secondary) controls where those trees are sited—along sidewalks and street edges—to maximise their presence in the eye-level perspective frame. Together the two signatures drive the Green View Index (IND_GVI) upward, as documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_ZhangQ2021_2. The evidence associates higher GVI with improved Visual Quality Index Scores under the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS). Supporting excerpts note that sidewalks with well-maintained greenery promote outdoor activity and wellbeing [excerpt 4], and that continuous green corridors and vertical greenery can enhance landscape qualities in high-density areas [excerpt 16].

operation.description: Modify the shape and configuration of natural vegetation elements in the middle ground to optimize visual composition and landscape aesthetic quality in urban green spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Natural vegetation shape modifications in the middle ground enhance visual landscape scores by improving spatial hierarchy, creating visual interest through organic forms, and establishing harmonious natural patterns that increase aesthetic perception and visual preference among observers

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the shape and spatial configuration of natural vegetation clusters in the middle ground alters the perceived morphological complexity and layering of the greenery composition; the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Li2024_4) shows that multi-layered (double-layer) vegetation arrangements yield higher visual landscape scores than single-layer ones, indicating that optimising canopy outline and arrangement increases the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT), which in turn enhances aesthetic quality judgments.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation — SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP — in which the shape and outline of natural vegetation (canopy trees/shrubs) within the middle-ground zone (10–50 m) of an urban green space are modified to optimise visual composition. Evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_4 links this to the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) under the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL): double-layer vegetation configurations produce higher visual landscape scores than single-layer ones (relationship direction DIR_NA, descriptive effect size). The intervention therefore aims to reshape vegetation silhouettes and layering to raise perceived naturalness and, consequently, aesthetic quality.

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

operation.description: Add water features in the middleground to increase visual and textural diversity in urban green spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Water features enhance perceived restoration by providing natural textural elements that facilitate attention restoration through fascination and being away experiences. The visual and auditory qualities of water increase environmental preference and reduce mental fatigue, supporting psychological restoration processes aligned with Attention Restoration Theory.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing water features where previously absent increases the Water View Index (IND_WAT) by raising the proportion of water visible within the middleground scene. According to SRT-based evidence, water as a natural element triggers inherent affective responses favourable to psychological restoration, thereby enhancing perceived restorativeness.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_WAT_MG_SIZ) that adds water features in the middleground zone of urban green spaces. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_6 demonstrates that increasing the Water View Index (IND_WAT) is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 0.032) with the Perceived Restoration Scale (PRS) under the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST. The study validates water as a global explanatory factor for psychological restoration, noting that water visibility presents positive influence on restorative perceptions. By adding water elements at 10–50 m, the intervention raises the amount of water captured in the visual field, directly increasing IND_WAT and thereby supporting the expected positive outcome in perceived restoration.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

operation.description: Modify vegetation spatial configuration and composition patterns to enhance plant species diversity in urban green spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Vegetation configuration modifications (shape variations) were tested but showed no significant relationship with species diversity outcomes, suggesting that diversity may be more influenced by ecological management practices or species selection rather than spatial form alone

signatures.mechanism: Modifying vegetation composition patterns and spatial configuration changes the species-level textural variety and layering across the planting scheme. In landscape ecology, 'composition' (which types and proportions) and 'configuration' (how they are spatially arranged) together constitute landscape pattern—captured here by VAR_TEX. The Diversity Index (IND_DIV) quantifies this richness of species mix. Richer, multi-layered planting with varied ground cover, shrubs, and canopy trees was expected to produce perceptible textural heterogeneity that could support perceived restorativeness, though the empirical result was non-significant (DIR_NS, β = 0.016).

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes the single operation of adjusting vegetation texture/pattern (species composition and spatial configuration) at the social-field scale of an urban park. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_8 measured the Diversity Index (IND_DIV) as a predictor of perceived restoration (PRS_MNT_REST) within the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO). Despite theoretical expectations—supported by related work showing richly layered plants enhance recovery perceptions (Excerpt 25) and that diverse aquatic plantings beside water features create 'rich landscape hierarchy' (Excerpt 23)—the MGWR analysis returned a non-significant relationship (DIR_NS, effect size 0.016), indicating that plant species diversity alone did not meaningfully shift restorative outcomes in this sample. The S1 scope reflects a single coordinated attribute change (texture/pattern) applied to one object class (vegetation) within one depth zone.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation elements in the foreground viewing area to enhance green view index in urban streetscapes through strategic addition of plants.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding vegetation size in the foreground directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby elevating GVI measurements. Larger vegetation masses create more visible green surfaces that occupy greater visual angles from pedestrian viewpoints, resulting in higher restorative potential through enhanced nature exposure.

signatures.mechanism: Strategically adding plants in the immediate foreground viewing zone increases the pixel ratio of greenery elements captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher proportion of visible vegetation in the visual field activates innate stress-recovery and attention-restoration responses (SRT/ART), promoting psychological restoration.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that increases the size and coverage of foreground vegetation through the addition of plants, directly raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_10. The linked evidence associates higher GVI with the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), specifically the subdimension PRS_MNT_REST (Restoration). Excerpts [3], [6], and [11] confirm that richly layered plants with extensive coverage significantly enhance perceived restorativeness, and that innate visual responses to vegetation underpin stress recovery. Excerpt [5] further notes that greenery exerts a relatively stronger restorative influence when prominently visible in the immediate viewing field. Therefore, increasing the vegetative pixel ratio in the foreground is expected to positively shift restoration outcomes, consistent with the direction implied by the evidence record.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of vegetation elements in the foreground and visible areas of the urban streetscape to enhance green visual index (GVI)

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater vegetation size and coverage in the foreground directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, which correlates positively with subjective perception of green quantity. Enhanced visual exposure to vegetation improves aesthetic perception scores through increased greenness visibility

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements and increasing their size in the foreground of the streetscape directly raises the proportion of green-coloured pixels captured in panoramic street-view imagery, thereby increasing the Green View Index (GVI). Because foreground objects occupy the largest solid-angle share of the visual field, new or enlarged vegetation at close range produces the strongest per-unit boost to GVI.

scope.description: SCP_S1 operation comprising a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ. The intervention increases the size and quantity of vegetation in the immediate foreground zone of the urban streetscape. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Cheng2017_1, this raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which serves as a proxy for perceived green quantity under the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU). The relationship direction is DIR_POS with a strong effect size of 0.8727, indicating that greater foreground vegetation coverage substantially improves subjective ratings of green quantity as measured by the 100-respondent questionnaire. No coordination with other object types or phased deployment is required; the single act of adding vegetative mass in the viewer's near field is sufficient to shift the indicator.

operation.description: Increase sky openness by reducing building height, width, or density in the background view to expand the visible sky area and improve sky view factor in the urban scene.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher sky view factor (SVF) creates more open and exposed spatial configurations that reduce visual enclosure. The increased visible sky area diminishes the sense of spatial definition and protective enclosure, leading to lower perceived safety scores as the environment feels more exposed and less territorially defined.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing building height, width, or density in the background view diminishes the built-mass enclosure of the street canyon, thereby increasing the proportion of visible sky (Sky View Factor). As buildings occupy less of the background visual field, the sky dome exposure grows, directly raising SVF.

scope.description: A single-signature operation SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ that modifies the size attributes (height, width, density) of background-tier built structures to increase Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). Although the IOM is framed in terms of sky openness, the physical manipulation targets OBJ_BLT facades whose massing is reduced. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_2, increasing SVF is associated with the social-outcomes performance dimension (PRF_SOC), specifically perceived safety (PRS_SOC_SAFE) measured via Place Pulse Visual Safety Score. The relationship direction is DIR_NEG (β = −0.109), indicating that greater sky openness—achieved by shrinking background building mass—corresponds to lower perceived visual safety, likely because reduced enclosure weakens the sense of spatial definition and surveillance cues that support feelings of safety in urban streetscapes.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and coverage of visible vegetation elements in urban streetscapes to enhance Green View Index (GVI) across different spatial contexts.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI improves visual environmental quality by increasing natural elements in the field of view, which positively correlates with perceived safety (visibility and natural surveillance), wealth (environmental quality and maintenance), liveliness (biodiversity and seasonal variation), and beauty (aesthetic preference for nature) in urban spaces. The spatio-temporal evolution of GVI directly reflects changes in composite perceptual scores.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vegetation elements (predominantly canopy trees) into the streetscape increases the proportion of green pixels captured in pedestrian-level street view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI corresponds to a greater share of the visual field occupied by greenery, which the evidence links to improved perceived safety, wealth, liveliness, and beauty of the urban scene.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ. Vegetation (canopy trees) is added to the streetscape to increase the amount (VAR_SIZ) of visible greenery at the midground social-field tier. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_3, this raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which exhibits a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL). Descriptive effect sizes for composite perception scores are Safety (0.28), Wealthy (0.32), Lively (0.23), and Beautiful (0.30), confirming that greater vegetation coverage consistently improves subjective streetscape quality across all four measures.
