performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Subjective perception score for Class 2 (Calm/Greenery)

source.citation: Ogawa, Y., Oki, T., Zhao, C., Sekimoto, Y., & Shimizu, C. (2024). Evaluating the subjective perceptions of streetscapes using street-view images. Landscape and Urban Planning, 247, 105073.

operation.description: Increase the proportion of vegetation pixels visible in street-level views by adding and enlarging multi-layer planting (canopy trees, understory shrubs, ground cover) along street corridors in both foreground and midground tiers. Strategic positioning and dense canopy form maximize the vegetation pixel ratio captured in street view imagery, thereby improving perceived visual comfort.

signatures.mechanism: Adding new vegetation elements (street trees, hedge rows, planter beds) within 0-10 m of the viewpoint directly increases the count of green pixels in the street view image, raising VEG = (Pixels_Veg / Total_Pixels) × 100. Foreground vegetation occupies the largest angular subtense and therefore contributes the most pixels per unit of real canopy area.

Adding large-canopy trees and continuous green corridors in the 10-50 m midground fills the upper and middle portions of the street view image with vegetation pixels, complementing foreground planting and boosting the overall vegetation index. Midground vegetation also partially occludes building facades, reducing the negative 'building' pixel fraction identified in the study.

Repositioning vegetation into multi-layer arrangements (canopy + understory + ground cover) and staggering planting along both sides of the street maximises the overlap of green surfaces across vertical bands of the panoramic image, ensuring vegetation pixels span low, middle, and upper zones of the visual field and increasing the total VEG ratio beyond what single-layer planting achieves.

Selecting and pruning tree species for broad, spreading canopy forms rather than narrow columnar forms increases the projected pixel footprint of each individual tree in the street view image. A wider canopy shape converts more of the sky and building pixel area into vegetation pixels, raising the VEG index per planted unit.

Increasing foliage density and selecting species with fine, continuous leaf texture reduces sky-through gaps in the canopy. The semantic segmentation algorithm classifies dense foliage patches more reliably as 'vegetation' pixels; sparse canopies with visible sky gaps are partially classified as sky, lowering the effective VEG ratio.

scope.description: Street-corridor scale (SCP_S2) intervention inferred from SVCs_P_Shao2023_1. Signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ act as primary drivers of IND_VEG within PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT, supported by positional layering (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS), canopy form optimisation (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP), and foliage density enhancement (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_TEX). Evidence reports consistent positive correlations (R = 0.723–0.782, p < 0.01) across Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing the proportion of visible vegetation in urban streetscapes, as measured through semantic segmentation of street-view images, directly raises the Green View Index. Across four Chinese megacities, streets with higher GVI and balanced spatial enclosure receive higher visual comfort ratings from survey respondents, with the relationship varying by street-type cluster and city context.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Visual Comfort Score (1-5 Likert)

source.citation: Shao, Y., Yin, Y., Xue, Z., & Ma, D. (2023). Assessing and Comparing the Visual Comfort of Streets across Four Chinese Megacities Using AI-Based Image Analysis and the Perceptive Evaluation Method. Land, 12(4), 834.

operation.description: Increase street-level visible greenery through addition and enhancement of vegetation along streets and slopes in hilly neighborhoods, targeting eye-level canopy coverage within an 800 m residential buffer. The intervention maximises the green pixel proportion captured in panoramic street-view imagery (GVI), which in steep-slope contexts was positively associated with vigorous physical activity.

signatures.mechanism: Adding new street trees and shrubs within 0-10 m of the pedestrian viewpoint directly increases green pixel count in panoramic street-view images, raising GVI.

Expanding the canopy volume of existing foreground vegetation (pruning for spread, species replacement for denser foliage) enlarges the green pixel area per tree, increasing GVI without new planting sites.

Positioning vegetation at eye-level (1.2-2.5 m height bands, e.g., hedges, green walls, raised planters) ensures green pixels occupy the central visual field of street-view captures, disproportionately boosting GVI relative to canopy-only strategies.

In hilly terrain, exposed slopes at 10-50 m distance are highly visible in street-view panoramas. Adding slope vegetation (ground-cover, terraced planting) converts bare or built pixels to green, contributing to GVI across steeper contexts specific to Group 3.
Reducing foreground impervious or built elements (walls, fences, parking surfaces) lowers the non-green pixel denominator and frees planting space, indirectly increasing the GVI ratio.

scope.description: SCP_S2 neighbourhood scale (800 m residential buffer) based on SVCs_P_Sun2022_1; signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ through SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ collectively target IND_GVI within PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_ACTV dimension.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhancing street-level and neighbourhood green space quantity and visibility in hilly urban residential areas increases residents’ daily exposure to greenery. This exposure promotes physical activity by providing attractive, accessible environments for walking and exercise, which in turn mediates improvements in self-reported health outcomes. The relationship operates through both the quantity of accessible green space and the perceived quality of the greenery visible at eye level.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Vigorous Physical Activity (VPA) in Steep Slope Group (Group 3, 800m buffer)

source.citation: Sun, P., Song, Y., & Lu, W. (2022). Effect of Urban Green Space in the Hilly Environment on Physical Activity and Health Outcomes: Mediation Analysis on Multiple Greenery Measures. Land, 11(5), 612.

operation.description: Increase eye-level street greenery by adding and enlarging vegetation elements along neighborhood streets in the foreground and midground tiers. The intervention targets the green pixel ratio captured by street-level imagery, directly raising the Green View Index to promote walking time among older adults.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation elements (street trees, tall shrubs, hedge rows) within 0-10 m of the pedestrian viewpoint directly increases the count of green pixels in eye-level panoramic imagery, raising GVI.

Positioning new and existing vegetation so that canopy and foliage occupy the eye-level visual band (1.2-2.5 m height) maximises green pixel capture in the four cardinal street-view images used to compute GVI.
Enlarging the canopy spread and foliage volume of existing foreground vegetation increases the spatial extent of green pixels per viewpoint, amplifying GVI without requiring new planting sites.

Adding tree rows and green buffers in the 10-50 m midground fills background gaps between foreground elements, contributing additional green pixels and raising the cumulative GVI across the neighbourhood buffer zone.

Selecting species with denser, year-round foliage and layered leaf textures increases the proportion of pixels classified as green within each vegetation element, improving GVI especially in subtropical contexts like Hong Kong.

scope.description: SCP_S2 street/corridor scale — SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS applied along neighbourhood street segments within residential buffer zones, as referenced in SVCs_P_Zang2020_1 where IND_GVI was averaged across all sample points in each community buffer to predict PRF_USE walking outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing street-level vegetation coverage (trees and shrubs) along neighbourhood streets enhances the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery within residential buffers, thereby raising the Green View Index. In the context of compact high-density urban environments, higher eye-level greenness along walking routes is associated with increased total walking time among older adults, as greener streetscapes provide more pleasant and stimulating pedestrian environments.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Total walking time in the past 7 days (minutes)

source.citation: Zang, P., Liu, X., Zhao, Y., Guo, H., Lu, Y., & Xue, C. Q. L. (2020). Eye-Level Street Greenery and Walking Behaviors of Older Adults. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 17(17), 6130.

operation.description: Reduce Sky View Factor along road corridors by introducing large-canopy street trees and strategically positioned overhead vegetation structures in the foreground tier, thereby screening direct and diffuse solar radiation from reaching road surfaces. The intervention targets the weighted sky-pixel ratio in the SVF formula by replacing visible sky portions with vegetative canopy, lowering road surface temperatures in hot-humid contexts.

signatures.subtype: large-canopy street trees (e.g., broad-crowned deciduous or evergreen species)
signatures.mechanism: Tree canopy occupies sky-hemisphere solid angles, directly replacing sky(i) pixels with vegetation pixels in the SVF summation, reducing the weighted sky ratio. Larger canopy spread → greater angular obstruction → lower SVF → reduced shortwave irradiance on road surface → lower RST.

signatures.subtype: horizontal canopy spread / umbrella-form crown architecture
signatures.mechanism: Wider, flatter canopy shapes maximize the horizontal projection of sky obstruction per unit biomass, increasing the angular coverage ω·sky(i) displacement at zenith and near-zenith angles where solar irradiance contribution to RST is greatest in low-latitude hot-humid cities.

signatures.subtype: bilateral / staggered tree rows along road edges
signatures.mechanism: Strategic positioning of trees on both sides of the road corridor creates overlapping screening cones that block solar access across the full road width, reducing sunlit hours on pavement. Bilateral placement converts the road cross-section from open-sky to quasi-canyon geometry, lowering SVF symmetrically across measurement points.

signatures.subtype: secondary tree canopy layer / tall understory vegetation buffer
signatures.mechanism: Mid-ground vegetation contributes to SVF reduction at lower elevation angles of the sky hemisphere, complementing foreground canopy by filling angular gaps between FG tree crowns. This is especially relevant at oblique viewing angles where the fish-eye projection captures distant canopy mass, further depressing the cumulative sky ratio.

While not directly altering SVF, modifying ground surface texture and material properties synergistically reduces RST under the remaining (non-screened) sky exposure. The paper notes water-retaining pavements can reduce RST by ~10°C (Higashiyama et al., 2016), complementing the SVF-mediated radiative pathway with an evaporative cooling co-benefit.

scope.description: SCP_S2 street-corridor scale intervention combining SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP, SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, and SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_TEX to reduce IND_SVF (r = 0.92 with RST per SVCs_P_Zeng2023_1) in the PRF_THR thermal comfort dimension, targeting PRS_THR_TEMP road surface temperature reduction in hot-humid megacity contexts.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing tree canopy coverage along street corridors raises the Green View Factor (GVF) derived from street-view panoramic imagery. Higher GVF reduces the sky-visible fraction (SVF) through canopy shading, which limits direct solar radiation reaching the road surface. This mechanism lowers the daily maximum road surface temperature, with the screening effect of vegetation being most pronounced in east–west oriented street canyons during summer.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Road Surface Temperature (RST)

source.citation: Zeng, L., Lindberg, F., Zhang, X., Pan, H., & Lu, J. (2023). Road surface temperature evaluated with streetview-derived parameters in a hot and humid megacity. Urban Climate, 51, 101585.

operation.description: Strategically add and position multi-layered vegetation in foreground and midground tiers within park settings to increase the Green View Index (green pixel ratio), while incorporating visible water features and screening built elements, to enhance perceived psychological restoration. Vegetation form and placement should balance greenery coverage with visual penetration to avoid excessive visual enclosure, as cautioned by the study's finding that excessive greenery can reduce spatial legibility.

signatures.mechanism: Adding foreground vegetation directly increases green pixel count in the panoramic visual field, raising GVI; greenery validated as significant global predictor of restoration (Model-2: β_std=0.044, p=0.003)

Midground vegetation extends green pixel coverage across depth of field, increasing cumulative GVI and reinforcing spatial continuity of greenery perceived from viewpoints; high spatial autocorrelation of greenery (Moran's I=0.382) confirms clustered distribution supports coherent green coverage

Repositioning vegetation to maximize green pixel coverage along key viewsheds (e.g., entrance square axis noted in excerpt [4]) while preserving visual penetration; optimizes GVI distribution without suppressing spatial openness referenced in excerpt [7]

Water visibility validated as significant positive predictor of restoration (Model-2: β_std=0.032, p=0.025); adding or revealing water in midground complements greenery by engaging innate affective responses aligned with Stress Recovery Theory (SRT, Ulrich 1983)

Building pixel share negatively correlates with restoration (r=−0.217, p<0.01); reducing built-element visibility via vegetative screening simultaneously raises GVI green pixel ratio and removes restorative detractors from the visual field

Modifying vegetation canopy shape and density balances green pixel coverage with visual penetration; prevents excessive greenery from reducing spatial legibility and ensures GVI gains do not compromise the non-significant but design-relevant sky openness (Sky: p=0.275 in Model-1)

scope.description: Site-scale (SCP_S2) park intervention linking SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_ADD_WAT_MG_SIZ, SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ, and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP to evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_1; targets IND_GVI through increased vegetation and water pixel share in panoramic imagery to support PRF_EMO via PRS_MNT_REST
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing visible vegetation in streetscapes raises the greenness index derived from street-view imagery semantic segmentation. At the city and functional-zone scale across multiple Chinese cities, higher eye-level greenness is associated with improved urban landscape quality indicators and correlates positively with city-level socioeconomic development metrics, reflecting the combined environmental and amenity value of street-visible vegetation.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Visual openness (Sky View Index) and Color Richness

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

operation.description: Inferred intervention representing the accumulation of mature street tree canopy in older urban neighborhoods, where decades of vegetation growth combined with narrower road cross-sections amplify the proportion of green pixels captured at eye level. The mechanism links temporal vegetation maturation and compact street geometry to elevated GVI values observed in earlier-built areas of Nanjing.

signatures.mechanism: Mature tree canopies in older neighborhoods occupy a larger solid angle in foreground view, directly increasing the numerator (Sum(Green_Pixels)) of the GVI formula. Decades of growth enlarge crown spread and leaf area visible from street level.

In panoramic images, midground trees behind foreground canopy fill additional green pixels, creating depth-layered vegetation coverage that further raises the green pixel sum across all four cardinal-direction captures.

Vegetation positioned along both edges of narrower streets in older districts occupies a greater angular proportion of the horizontal visual field, maximising the green-to-total pixel ratio per panoramic frame.

Narrower road surfaces in older neighbourhoods (excerpt [25]) reduce the proportion of non-green ground pixels in the denominator (Sum(Total_Pixels)), indirectly increasing GVI even at constant vegetation volume. Less pavement exposure allows vegetation to dominate the visual field.

The compact cross-sectional shape of streets in earlier-built areas constrains the total visual field width, so that overhanging canopies more readily close the canopy gap above the street, converting sky/building pixels to green pixels in the GVI calculation.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 superposition record coordinates 5 signatures linked to evidence SVCs_P_Xue2022_3. The original entry had no explicit phase assignments, so it is normalized as a concurrent multi-signature intervention rather than a phased SCP_S3 programme.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing vegetation maturity and coverage (both street trees and understory planting) along neighbourhood streets raises the Green View Index computed from panoramic street-view images. At the neighbourhood and district scale, higher aggregated GVI correlates with higher housing prices and newer building stock, reflecting the amenity value that visible greenery adds to the urban residential environment.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Building Age (Older buildings / Earlier construction year)

source.citation: Xue, C., Jin, C., Zhou, L., & Li, G. (2022). Exploring the distribution of city street greenery from eye-level: An application of Baidu Map panoramic images data. Geografisk Tidsskrift-Danish Journal of Geography, 122(1), 73–86.

operation.description: Increase streetside vegetation canopy volume and lateral spread along roadways to raise the proportion of green pixels captured in pedestrian-level street view imagery. This primarily entails adding or enlarging tree and shrub plantings in the foreground and midground tiers of the streetscape, thereby directly increasing the Green View Index across different road types.

signatures.mechanism: Adding foreground vegetation (0-10 m from viewpoint) directly increases the count of green pixels relative to total pixels in panoramic street view imagery, raising GVI. Street trees and hedgerows at the roadside dominate the near-field visual proportion.

Adding midground vegetation (10-50 m) along medians, setbacks, and secondary planting strips contributes additional green pixels in the middle depth plane of the street view panorama, supplementing the foreground canopy contribution to GVI.

Narrower road cross-sections reduce the proportion of non-green (paved/built) pixels and shorten the distance to roadside vegetation, enlarging the apparent angular size of tree canopies in the view. The negative correlation between road width and GVI (e.g., T1 r = −0.332**, T4 r = −0.207**) confirms this mediating role.
Repositioning vegetation closer to the pedestrian viewpoint increases its projected pixel area in the hemispherical/panoramic image, boosting GVI without requiring additional planting volume.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 superposition record coordinates 4 signatures linked to evidence SVCs_P_Dong2018_2. The original entry had no explicit phase assignments, so it is normalized as a concurrent multi-signature intervention rather than a phased SCP_S3 programme.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Planting additional street trees along major urban roads increases the proportion of green vegetation pixels visible in street-view images sampled at regular intervals along the road network. By aggregating the Green View Index across road segments and administrative districts, the intervention produces a measurable citywide increase in pedestrian-level green visibility, with the effect varying by road type and district-level urbanisation intensity.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Comparison of GVI across road types is not established.

source.citation: Dong, R., Zhang, Y., & Zhao, J. (2018). How Green Are the Streets Within the Sixth Ring Road of Beijing? An Analysis Based on Tencent Street View Pictures and the Green View Index. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 15(7), 1367.

operation.description: Counteracting spatio-temporal perceptual decline by increasing street-level vegetation coverage through foreground and midground planting interventions, thereby raising the Green View Index and shifting visual cluster membership away from the perceptually negative 'Urban Jungle' (Cluster 1) profile toward greener, higher-quality perception clusters (Clusters 2 and 4). This is consistent with the study's observation that post-construction greening improvements successfully transform visually degraded clusters into perceptually positive ones over successive time periods.

signatures.mechanism: Adding foreground vegetation volume directly increases the green pixel count in panoramic street-level imagery, raising the GVI numerator and shifting pixel-ratio composition away from the built/paved dominance characteristic of Cluster 1 ('Urban Jungle': highest building view factor, lowest vegetation)

Adding midground vegetation extends green pixel coverage into the depth of the visual field, further boosting GVI and approximating the high green-view-factor profile of perceptually positive Cluster 2 ('Flanked by Nature'), while producing the layered greenery balance associated with Cluster 4 ('Balanced Living')

Repositioning vegetation to maximise visual coverage along dominant street-view sightlines increases effective green pixel capture per panoramic image, optimising GVI gains per unit of planting and ensuring seasonal visibility across multi-temporal image acquisition

Modifying visible built-element surfaces with green walls or climbing vegetation reclassifies building-segmented pixels as green-segmented pixels, directly reducing the high building view factor that defines Cluster 1 while simultaneously increasing GVI, compounding both vegetation gain and built-element attenuation in pixel-ratio terms

Removing paved ground surfaces and replacing them with vegetated ground cover reduces the visual pavement component that co-characterises perceptually declining clusters, while converting road/pavement-classified pixels to green-classified pixels, increasing GVI and supporting the compositional shift toward Cluster 4's balanced urban-natural profile

scope.description: SCP_S2 encompasses SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_SIZ, and SIG_REM_GRD_FG_SIZ (supporting), linked to evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_4; multi-tier vegetation intervention targeting IND_GVI under PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL to reverse the descriptive cluster-shift-based perceptual decline identified across spatio-temporal periods
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding vegetation to street-level environments increases the proportion of green pixels in multi-temporal street-view imagery aggregated to hexagonal spatial units. Over time, increased vegetation presence in the visual environment contributes to improved visual environment quality scores, reflecting the role of greenery as a stable positive component in the urban visual landscape that enhances perceived naturalness and visual diversity.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Cluster Shift (Perceptual Decline) is not established.

source.citation: Liang, X., Zhao, T., & Biljecki, F. (2023). Revealing spatio-temporal evolution of urban visual environments with street view imagery. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104802.

operation.description: Increase the proportion of green pixels captured in eye-level street-view imagery by adding or enlarging visible vegetation within the camera's field of view. This primarily involves introducing canopy and shrub mass in the foreground and mid-ground tiers so that green pixels displace non-green pixels (built surfaces, ground, sky) in the segmented image, thereby raising the GVI ratio.

signatures.mechanism: Adding new vegetation mass (tree canopy, hedges, climbing plants) within 0-10 m of the viewpoint maximises green-pixel coverage due to angular proximity; each added unit of leaf area occupies a disproportionately large share of the image frame, directly increasing Sum(Green_Pixels)/Sum(Total_Pixels).

Vegetation added at 10-50 m (the interval at which Mapillary sample points are spaced, per excerpt [25]) fills the visual depth of the streetscape, contributing additional green pixels in the upper and lateral portions of the image where sky or façades would otherwise dominate.

Modifying crown shape (e.g., selecting broad-canopy species or allowing lateral growth instead of pollarding) widens the silhouette of existing vegetation against the image frame, converting more pixels from sky/built to green without necessarily adding new planting locations.

Repositioning vegetation vertically (e.g., adding understory planting, green walls, or lowering branch clearance) shifts green mass into the central band of the street-view image where the segmentation algorithm registers eye-level greenness, improving GVI even if total biomass is unchanged.

Increasing foliage density (species with denser leaf packing, evergreen rather than deciduous selection) reduces see-through gaps in the canopy, converting mixed sky-green pixels to solid green pixels and raising the green-pixel count within the same crown envelope.

Removing or reducing built elements (walls, signage, overhead structures) that occlude vegetation in the camera's line of sight reveals existing green mass that was previously hidden, increasing green-pixel share without new planting.

scope.description: Coordinated (SCP_S2) intervention scope: each GVI value is computed at a discrete street-view sampling point (50 m intervals, excerpt [25]) from a single eye-level camera perspective. Signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ capture the dominant physical changes that shift green-pixel ratio (IND_GVI) toward higher aesthetic naturalness (PRS_AES_NATU) as recorded in SVCs_P_Sanchez2024_1.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: At discrete street-view sampling points, increasing the proportion of visible vegetation (trees, shrubs, grass) in the surrounding streetscape raises the Green View Index computed from semantically segmented open-source street-view imagery. The methodology uses Mapillary images with satellite NDVI supplementation at locations lacking street-view coverage, enabling scalable citywide assessment of eye-level greenness visibility.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Green View Index derived from Mapillary images is not established.

source.citation: Sánchez, I. A. V., & Labib, S. M. (2024). Accessing eye-level greenness visibility from open-source street view images: A methodological development and implementation in multi-city and multi-country contexts. Sustainable Cities and Society, 103, 105262.

operation.description: Increase the visible proportion of ground-level terrain surfaces in the streetscape by expanding and improving ground-plane areas (sidewalks, unpaved surfaces, open ground) in the foreground and midground, while moderating the visual dominance of buildings that occlude terrain. This compositional shift raises the Terrain Ratio, which is positively correlated with perceived visual comfort across multiple Chinese megacities.

signatures.mechanism: Widening or extending ground-level surfaces (sidewalks, plazas, unpaved terrain) increases terrain pixel count in the foreground, directly raising IND_TER_RAT.
Adding visible ground-plane area in the midground (e.g., setback plazas, exposed terrain strips) extends the terrain depth in the image, contributing additional terrain pixels to the total frame.

Modifying ground surface materiality (e.g., permeable paving, natural stone, exposed earth) ensures segmentation algorithms consistently classify these pixels as 'terrain' rather than 'road', reinforcing the ratio while improving perceived surface quality.
Setting back or stepping down building facades reduces building pixel dominance in the lower-to-mid frame, revealing more ground surface behind and increasing the terrain-to-total pixel ratio.

scope.description: SCP_S2 combines SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (primary) with SIG_ADD_GRD_MG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_TEX, and SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_SIZ to jointly shift the compositional balance toward terrain pixels as measured by IND_TER_RAT in evidence SVCs_P_Shao2023_2 under PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying ground-surface materials and mid-ground landscape elements to incorporate more permeable, natural surfaces (grass, ground cover) and reduce hard pavement dominance alters the ground-level visual texture in streetscapes. Combined with appropriate spatial enclosure from trees and buildings, these changes contribute to perceived visual comfort by creating more balanced and pleasant ground-plane conditions.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Perceived Visual Comfort Score is not established.

source.citation: Shao, Y., Yin, Y., Xue, Z., & Ma, D. (2023). Assessing and Comparing the Visual Comfort of Streets across Four Chinese Megacities Using AI-Based Image Analysis and the Perceptive Evaluation Method. Land, 12(4), 834.

operation.description: Adding compound multi-layered vegetation (tree, shrub, and herb layers) along urban street greenery belts to maximize Green View Index. Tree canopy size in the foreground visual field is the dominant driver of GVI, with shrub and herb layers providing supplementary green-pixel coverage that collectively fills the pedestrian visual field across vertical strata.

signatures.mechanism: Tree canopy addition increases green pixel count in the upper and middle portions of the visual field; tree layer contributes the largest GVI share (mean 42.6%, up to 92.96% in compound structures), directly raising the Sum(Green_Pixels)/Sum(Total_Pixels) ratio

Shrub layer addition fills the mid-height band of the visual field with green pixels (mean 31.8% GVI share), supplementing canopy gaps and increasing cumulative green-pixel area beneath the tree crown line

Herb/grass layer addition covers ground-level portions of the visual field with green pixels (mean 25.6% GVI share), converting otherwise bare ground or hard-surface pixels to green in the lower visual register

Shifting from single-layer to multi-layered compound form (tree + shrub + herb) changes the vertical distribution and continuity of green-pixel coverage; 91.09% of sampled viewpoints already use tree-centred compound structures, indicating this structural shape is the normative design condition that maximises GVI

Repositioning vegetation across multiple greenery-belt types (central, divided, street-tree, roadside) modulates the lateral distribution of green pixels relative to the pedestrian viewpoint; road patterns with more belt positions increase the number of green-belt bars intercepting the visual field, raising GVI

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (tree, shrub, herb sub-layers) and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP (compound structure) plus SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS (belt placement) applied at street-segment scale (SCP_S2) as examined in SVCs_P_Zhu2023_3; IND_GVI measured via DeepLab-V3+ semantic segmentation of street-view images relates to aesthetic quality perception (PRS_AES_QUAL) under PRF_AES
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Implementing compound greening structures (tree–shrub–herb combinations) along road green belts increases the multi-layer green-pixel area in the pedestrian visual field, raising the Green View Index. Statistical analysis across 593 roads in Hangzhou demonstrates that roads with compound vegetation structures and multiple green belts achieve significantly higher GVI than those with single-layer planting, improving the overall street greenery quality assessment.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on GVI contribution by vegetation structure is not established.

source.citation: Zhu, H., Nan, X., Yang, F., & Bao, Z. (2023). Utilizing the green view index to improve the urban street greenery index system: A statistical study using road patterns and vegetation structures as entry points. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104780.
